title: VISTA: Enhancing Visual Conditioning via Track-Following Preference Optimization in Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated strong performance across a wide range of robotic manipulation tasks. Despite the success, extending large pretrained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to the action space can induce vision-action misalignment, where action predictions exhibit weak dependence on the current visual state, leading to unreliable action outputs. In this work, we study VLA models through the lens of visual conditioning and empirically show that successful rollouts consistently exhibit stronger visual dependence than failed ones. Motivated by this observation, we propose a training framework that explicitly strengthens visual conditioning in VLA models. Our approach first aligns action prediction with visual input via preference optimization on a track-following surrogate task, and then transfers the enhanced alignment to instruction-following task through latent-space distillation during supervised finetuning. Without introducing architectural modifications or additional data collection, our method improves both visual conditioning and task performance for discrete OpenVLA, and further yields consistent gains when extended to the continuous OpenVLA-OFT setting. Project website: https://vista-vla.github.io/ .

AI.tldr: Presents a two-stage training framework to strengthen visual conditioning in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, improving action–vision dependence and performance without architecture changes or extra data.
AI.motivation: VLA models suffer from vision–action misalignment when transferring large pretrained VLMs to action spaces; successful rollouts show stronger visual dependence, motivating explicit conditioning improvements.
AI.method: Stage 1: preference optimization on a track-following surrogate task to align action predictions with visual input. Stage 2: latent-space distillation during supervised finetuning to transfer the enhanced alignment to instruction-following tasks. No architectural changes or additional data collection required.
AI.result: Improved visual conditioning and task performance for discrete OpenVLA; consistent gains when extended to continuous OpenVLA-OFT.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly strengthening visual conditioning via preference optimization and latent-space distillation improves alignment and performance in VLA tasks, without extra data or architecture changes.

title: Food Portion Estimation: From Pixels to Calories
summary: Reliance on images for dietary assessment is an important strategy to accurately and conveniently monitor an individual's health, making it a vital mechanism in the prevention and care of chronic diseases and obesity. However, image-based dietary assessment suffers from estimating the three dimensional size of food from 2D image inputs. Many strategies have been devised to overcome this critical limitation such as the use of auxiliary inputs like depth maps, multi-view inputs, or model-based approaches such as template matching. Deep learning also helps bridge the gap by either using monocular images or combinations of the image and the auxillary inputs to precisely predict the output portion from the image input. In this paper, we explore the different strategies employed for accurate portion estimation.

AI.tldr: Survey of strategies for 3D portion estimation from 2D dietary images, including depth maps, multi-view inputs, template/model-based approaches, and deep learning with monocular or auxiliary data.
AI.motivation: Accurate portion size is essential for dietary assessment; 2D images lack inherent 3D size information, impeding precise nutrition estimation.
AI.method: Review and compare diverse strategies: depth-assisted methods, multi-view reconstruction, template/model-based matching, and deep learning approaches that fuse image data with auxiliary inputs.
AI.result: Provides an integrative overview outlining the strengths and limitations of each approach and identifying gaps and future directions for robust portion estimation.
AI.conclusion: No single method suffices; effective 3D portion estimation will likely require a combination of depth sensing, multi-view or priors, and data-driven models; integration of these strategies is the recommended path.

title: Signal or 'Noise': Human Reactions to Robot Errors in the Wild
summary: In the real world, robots frequently make errors, yet little is known about people's social responses to errors outside of lab settings. Prior work has shown that social signals are reliable and useful for error management in constrained interactions, but it is unclear if this holds in the real world - especially with a non-social robot in repeated and group interactions with successive or propagated errors. To explore this, we built a coffee robot and conducted a public field deployment ($N = 49$). We found that participants consistently expressed varied social signals in response to errors and other stimuli, particularly during group interactions. Our findings suggest that social signals in the wild are rich (with participants volunteering information about the interaction), but "noisy." We discuss lessons, benefits, and challenges for using social signals in real-world HRI.

AI.tldr: In a public field test (N=49) with a self-built coffee robot, people produced varied, rich social signals in response to robot errors, especially in group settings; signals are informative but noisy, highlighting challenges for real-world social cue-based error management in HRI.
AI.motivation: Assess whether social cues that aid error management in lab studies generalize to real-world, repeated/group interactions with a non-social robot.
AI.method: Public field deployment of a coffee-robot (N=49). Observational study collecting participants' social signals and volunteered commentary during interactions with the robot, focusing on errors and other stimuli across individual and group contexts.
AI.result: Participants consistently expressed varied social signals in response to errors; signals were richer in the wild and more pronounced during group interactions; the signals are informative but noisy.
AI.conclusion: Social signals can be valuable for error management in real-world HRI but present noise and variability; design implications include leveraging rich, voluntary signals while developing methods to handle noise in field settings, especially under group dynamics.

title: Artificial Intelligence as Strange Intelligence: Against Linear Models of Intelligence
summary: We endorse and expand upon Susan Schneider's critique of the linear model of AI progress and introduce two novel concepts: "familiar intelligence" and "strange intelligence". AI intelligence is likely to be strange intelligence, defying familiar patterns of ability and inability, combining superhuman capacities in some domains with subhuman performance in other domains, and even within domains sometimes combining superhuman insight with surprising errors that few humans would make. We develop and defend a nonlinear model of intelligence on which "general intelligence" is not a unified capacity but instead the ability to achieve a broad range of goals in a broad range of environments, in a manner that defies nonarbitrary reduction to a single linear quantity. We conclude with implications for adversarial testing approaches to evaluating AI capacities. If AI is strange intelligence, we should expect that even the most capable systems will sometimes fail in seemingly obvious tasks. On a nonlinear model of AI intelligence, such errors on their own do not demonstrate a system's lack of outstanding general intelligence. Conversely, excellent performance on one type of task, such as an IQ test, cannot warrant assumptions of broad capacities beyond that task domain.

AI.tldr: Proposes that AI progress should be viewed as nonlinear and that 'general intelligence' is not a single scalar but the ability to achieve varied goals across diverse environments; introduces 'familiar intelligence' and 'strange intelligence' and argues for adversarial testing approaches given the possibility that capable systems will fail obvious tasks in some domains.
AI.motivation: Challenging the linear, scalar view of AI progress and the assumption that high performance on one task signals broad capability; extend Susan Schneider's critique and provide a framework for evaluating AI that can exhibit uneven, domain-specific strengths and weaknesses.
AI.method: Theoretical conceptual analysis introducing two notions—'familiar intelligence' and 'strange intelligence'—and presenting a nonlinear model of intelligence where general intelligence is not reducible to a single quantitative measure; discusses implications for evaluation and adversarial testing.
AI.result: A framework in which AI can be both extraordinarily capable in some domains and fallible in others (strange intelligence); general intelligence is reframed as goal- and environment-competence across a broad set of tasks rather than a single scalar; evaluation must consider domain-specific performance and robustness to adversarial tests.
AI.conclusion: If AI is strange intelligence, evaluation should emphasize diverse, adversarial testing and recognition that exemplary performance in one domain does not imply broad capacities, while failures in obvious tasks do not negate high general performance in other domains.

title: Visual concept ranking uncovers medical shortcuts used by large multimodal models
summary: Ensuring the reliability of machine learning models in safety-critical domains such as healthcare requires auditing methods that can uncover model shortcomings. We introduce a method for identifying important visual concepts within large multimodal models (LMMs) and use it to investigate the behaviors these models exhibit when prompted with medical tasks. We primarily focus on the task of classifying malignant skin lesions from clinical dermatology images, with supplemental experiments including both chest radiographs and natural images. After showing how LMMs display unexpected gaps in performance between different demographic subgroups when prompted with demonstrating examples, we apply our method, Visual Concept Ranking (VCR), to these models and prompts. VCR generates hypotheses related to different visual feature dependencies, which we are then able to validate with manual interventions.

AI.tldr: Introduces Visual Concept Ranking (VCR) to audit large multimodal models on medical tasks, revealing demographic subgroup gaps and generating testable hypotheses about visual feature dependencies, which are validated via manual interventions.
AI.motivation: Ensure reliability and fairness of ML models in safety-critical healthcare domains by uncovering hidden model shortcomings in LMMs and providing interpretable debugging signals.
AI.method: Apply Visual Concept Ranking to LMM prompts for medical tasks (malignant skin lesion classification from dermatology images; supplemental chest radiographs and natural images). VCR identifies important visual concepts, generates hypotheses about feature dependencies, and validates these hypotheses through manual interventions.
AI.result: LMMs exhibit unexpected performance gaps across demographic subgroups when prompted with demonstrations; VCR yields hypotheses about visual feature dependencies that can be validated/invalidated through targeted interventions.
AI.conclusion: VCR offers a practical, interpretable auditing framework for understanding and debugging LMM behavior in medical contexts, aiding reliability and fairness improvements.

title: Differentiable Inverse Graphics for Zero-shot Scene Reconstruction and Robot Grasping
comment: Submitted to IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L) for review. This version includes the statement required by IEEE for preprints
summary: Operating effectively in novel real-world environments requires robotic systems to estimate and interact with previously unseen objects. Current state-of-the-art models address this challenge by using large amounts of training data and test-time samples to build black-box scene representations. In this work, we introduce a differentiable neuro-graphics model that combines neural foundation models with physics-based differentiable rendering to perform zero-shot scene reconstruction and robot grasping without relying on any additional 3D data or test-time samples. Our model solves a series of constrained optimization problems to estimate physically consistent scene parameters, such as meshes, lighting conditions, material properties, and 6D poses of previously unseen objects from a single RGBD image and bounding boxes. We evaluated our approach on standard model-free few-shot benchmarks and demonstrated that it outperforms existing algorithms for model-free few-shot pose estimation. Furthermore, we validated the accuracy of our scene reconstructions by applying our algorithm to a zero-shot grasping task. By enabling zero-shot, physically-consistent scene reconstruction and grasping without reliance on extensive datasets or test-time sampling, our approach offers a pathway towards more data efficient, interpretable and generalizable robot autonomy in novel environments.

AI.tldr: A differentiable neuro-graphics framework that fuses neural foundation models with physics-based differentiable rendering to achieve zero-shot scene reconstruction and grasping from a single RGB-D image and object bounding boxes, without extra 3D data or test-time samples, by solving constrained optimization to estimate meshes, lighting, materials, and 6D poses; it sets new benchmarks for model-free few-shot pose estimation and enables zero-shot grasping.
AI.motivation: Robots must operate in novel real-world environments with limited data. Current state-of-the-art relies on large datasets and test-time sampling to build opaque scene representations, hindering data efficiency, interpretability, and generalization. A physically-consistent, differentiable approach could enable zero-shot scene understanding and manipulation.
AI.method: A differentiable neuro-graphics model that combines neural foundation models with physics-based differentiable rendering. It solves constrained optimization problems to estimate physically consistent scene parameters—meshes, lighting conditions, material properties, and 6D poses of unseen objects—from a single RGB-D image and bounding boxes, without requiring additional 3D data or test-time samples.
AI.result: On standard model-free few-shot benchmarks, the method outperforms existing algorithms for model-free few-shot pose estimation. The zero-shot scene reconstructions enable accurate zero-shot grasping, validating the approach’s data efficiency, interpretability, and potential for generalization in novel environments.
AI.conclusion: Zero-shot, physically-consistent scene reconstruction and grasping without extensive datasets or test-time sampling advances data-efficient, interpretable robot autonomy in unseen settings.

title: DeepRead: Document Structure-Aware Reasoning to Enhance Agentic Search
summary: With the rapid progress of tool-using and agentic large language models (LLMs), Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is evolving from one-shot, passive retrieval into multi-turn, decision-driven evidence acquisition. Despite strong results in open-domain settings, existing agentic search frameworks commonly treat long documents as flat collections of chunks, underutilizing document-native priors such as hierarchical organization and sequential discourse structure. We introduce DeepRead, a structure-aware, multi-turn document reasoning agent that explicitly operationalizes these priors for long-document question answering. DeepRead leverages LLM-based OCR model to convert PDFs into structured Markdown that preserves headings and paragraph boundaries. It then indexes documents at the paragraph level and assigns each paragraph a coordinate-style metadata key encoding its section identity and in-section order. Building on this representation, DeepRead equips the LLM with two complementary tools: a Retrieve tool that localizes relevant paragraphs while exposing their structural coordinates (with lightweight scanning context), and a ReadSection tool that enables contiguous, order-preserving reading within a specified section and paragraph range. Our experiments demonstrate that DeepRead achieves significant improvements over Search-o1-style agentic search in document question answering. The synergistic effect between retrieval and reading tools is also validated. Our fine-grained behavioral analysis reveals a reading and reasoning paradigm resembling human-like ``locate then read'' behavior.

AI.tldr: A structure-aware, multi-turn document reasoning agent (DeepRead) for long-document QA that preserves document hierarchy and order via paragraph-level indexing and two tools to enable a locate-then-read workflow, outperforming flat-retrieval agentic search.
AI.motivation: Long documents contain hierarchical organization and discourse structure that are lost when treated as flat chunks; exploiting these priors can improve evidence collection and reasoning in agentic search for QA.
AI.method: Convert PDFs with an LLM-based OCR into structured Markdown preserving headings/paragraphs; index at the paragraph level with coordinates encoding section identity and in-section order; provide two tools to the LLM: (1) Retrieve to localize relevant paragraphs with coordinates and light context, (2) ReadSection to read contiguous, ordered ranges within a section; evaluate in a multi-turn, decision-driven setting.
AI.result: Demonstrates significant improvements over traditional flat-search agentic methods; shows a synergistic effect between retrieval and reading tools; behavioral analysis suggests a human-like locate-then-read reading pattern.
AI.conclusion: Structure-aware handling of long documents enables more efficient and accurate long-document QA by leveraging native document priors via specialized tools, suggesting a general design principle for agentic search in long texts.

title: A Causal Perspective for Enhancing Jailbreak Attack and Defense
summary: Uncovering the mechanisms behind "jailbreaks" in large language models (LLMs) is crucial for enhancing their safety and reliability, yet these mechanisms remain poorly understood. Existing studies predominantly analyze jailbreak prompts by probing latent representations, often overlooking the causal relationships between interpretable prompt features and jailbreak occurrences. In this work, we propose Causal Analyst, a framework that integrates LLMs into data-driven causal discovery to identify the direct causes of jailbreaks and leverage them for both attack and defense. We introduce a comprehensive dataset comprising 35k jailbreak attempts across seven LLMs, systematically constructed from 100 attack templates and 50 harmful queries, annotated with 37 meticulously designed human-readable prompt features. By jointly training LLM-based prompt encoding and GNN-based causal graph learning, we reconstruct causal pathways linking prompt features to jailbreak responses. Our analysis reveals that specific features, such as "Positive Character" and "Number of Task Steps", act as direct causal drivers of jailbreaks. We demonstrate the practical utility of these insights through two applications: (1) a Jailbreaking Enhancer that targets identified causal features to significantly boost attack success rates on public benchmarks, and (2) a Guardrail Advisor that utilizes the learned causal graph to extract true malicious intent from obfuscated queries. Extensive experiments, including baseline comparisons and causal structure validation, confirm the robustness of our causal analysis and its superiority over non-causal approaches. Our results suggest that analyzing jailbreak features from a causal perspective is an effective and interpretable approach for improving LLM reliability. Our code is available at https://github.com/Master-PLC/Causal-Analyst.

AI.tldr: Causal Analyst combines LLM-based prompt encoding with GNN-based causal graph learning to identify direct causal features driving jailbreaks, using a 35k-attack dataset across 7 LLMs and 37 human-readable prompt features; identifies drivers like 'Positive Character' and 'Number of Task Steps'; offers a Jailbreaking Enhancer and a Guardrail Advisor; demonstrates superiority over non-causal baselines; code released.
AI.motivation: Existing analyses focus on latent representations and correlations, lacking explicit causal links between prompt features and jailbreaks. A causal understanding is needed for interpretable, robust defenses and safer LLMs.
AI.method: Construct a large, diverse jailbreak dataset (35k attempts, 7 LLMs, 100 attack templates, 50 harmful queries, 37 features). Jointly train an LLM-based prompt encoder and a graph neural network (GNN) to learn a causal graph linking prompt features to jailbreak responses, enabling causal path discovery and application to attack/defense.
AI.result: Identified direct causal drivers (e.g., 'Positive Character', 'Number of Task Steps'). Demonstrated two applications: (1) Jailbreaking Enhancer increases attack success on public benchmarks; (2) Guardrail Advisor uses the causal graph to extract true intent from obfuscated queries. Extensive experiments validate robustness and superiority over non-causal baselines.
AI.conclusion: A causal perspective on jailbreak features yields interpretable, effective improvements in LLM reliability and safety, with the provided code for replication.

title: CLEAR-HPV: Interpretable Concept Discovery for HPV-Associated Morphology in Whole-Slide Histology
summary: Human papillomavirus (HPV) status is a critical determinant of prognosis and treatment response in head and neck and cervical cancers. Although attention-based multiple instance learning (MIL) achieves strong slide-level prediction for HPV-related whole-slide histopathology, it provides limited morphologic interpretability. To address this limitation, we introduce Concept-Level Explainable Attention-guided Representation for HPV (CLEAR-HPV), a framework that restructures the MIL latent space using attention to enable concept discovery without requiring concept labels during training. Operating in an attention-weighted latent space, CLEAR-HPV automatically discovers keratinizing, basaloid, and stromal morphologic concepts, generates spatial concept maps, and represents each slide using a compact concept-fraction vector. CLEAR-HPV's concept-fraction vectors preserve the predictive information of the original MIL embeddings while reducing the high-dimensional feature space (e.g., 1536 dimensions) to only 10 interpretable concepts. CLEAR-HPV generalizes consistently across TCGA-HNSCC, TCGA-CESC, and CPTAC-HNSCC, providing compact, concept-level interpretability through a general, backbone-agnostic framework for attention-based MIL models of whole-slide histopathology.

AI.tldr: CLEAR-HPV introduces an attention-guided MIL framework that discovers interpretable morphologic concepts from HPV-related histopathology without concept labels, producing compact concept-fraction representations and spatial maps that generalize across datasets.
AI.motivation: Improve interpretability of attention-based MIL predictions for HPV-associated cancers by mapping latent features to morphologic concepts without needing labeled concepts.
AI.method: Restructure MIL latent space using attention to enable unsupervised concept discovery; generate spatial concept maps; represent each slide with a 10-dimensional concept-fraction vector derived from 1536-d embeddings; backbone-agnostic.
AI.result: Concept-fraction vectors retain predictive power while reducing dimensionality; automatic discovery of keratinizing, basaloid, and stromal concepts; good generalization across TCGA-HNSCC, TCGA-CESC, CPTAC-HNSCC.
AI.conclusion: Offers a general, backbone-agnostic framework for attention-based MIL in whole-slide histopathology, delivering interpretable, compact representations and concept maps without requiring concept labels.

title: MINT: Minimal Information Neuro-Symbolic Tree for Objective-Driven Knowledge-Gap Reasoning and Active Elicitation
summary: Joint planning through language-based interactions is a key area of human-AI teaming. Planning problems in the open world often involve various aspects of incomplete information and unknowns, e.g., objects involved, human goals/intents -- thus leading to knowledge gaps in joint planning. We consider the problem of discovering optimal interaction strategies for AI agents to actively elicit human inputs in object-driven planning. To this end, we propose Minimal Information Neuro-Symbolic Tree (MINT) to reason about the impact of knowledge gaps and leverage self-play with MINT to optimize the AI agent's elicitation strategies and queries. More precisely, MINT builds a symbolic tree by making propositions of possible human-AI interactions and by consulting a neural planning policy to estimate the uncertainty in planning outcomes caused by remaining knowledge gaps. Finally, we leverage LLM to search and summarize MINT's reasoning process and curate a set of queries to optimally elicit human inputs for best planning performance. By considering a family of extended Markov decision processes with knowledge gaps, we analyze the return guarantee for a given MINT with active human elicitation. Our evaluation on three benchmarks involving unseen/unknown objects of increasing realism shows that MINT-based planning attains near-expert returns by issuing a limited number of questions per task while achieving significantly improved rewards and success rates.

AI.tldr: MINT is a neuro-symbolic framework that actively elicits human inputs to reduce knowledge gaps in object-driven open-world planning, using a symbolic decision tree, a neural planning policy, and LLM-assisted reasoning to achieve near-expert performance with few questions.
AI.motivation: Open-world planning suffers from incomplete information and unknowns (e.g., objects, human goals). There is a need for optimal interaction strategies to elicit human inputs to close knowledge gaps and improve planning performance.
AI.method: Construct MINT: build a symbolic tree of possible human-AI interactions; use a neural planning policy to estimate outcome uncertainty due to knowledge gaps; optimize elicitation strategies via self-play; employ LLM to search and summarize MINT's reasoning and curate queries; analyze with extended MDPS to guarantee returns; evaluate on benchmarks with unseen objects.
AI.result: MINT-based planning achieves near-expert returns with a limited set of questions per task and shows significantly improved rewards and success rates across three benchmarks with unseen/unknown objects.
AI.conclusion: MINT provides an effective framework for active human elicitation in planning under knowledge gaps, offering both practical performance gains and theoretical return guarantees; suggests a scalable path for human-AI collaboration in open-world tasks.

title: ARGaze: Autoregressive Transformers for Online Egocentric Gaze Estimation
summary: Online egocentric gaze estimation predicts where a camera wearer is looking from first-person video using only past and current frames, a task essential for augmented reality and assistive technologies. Unlike third-person gaze estimation, this setting lacks explicit head or eye signals, requiring models to infer current visual attention from sparse, indirect cues such as hand-object interactions and salient scene content. We observe that gaze exhibits strong temporal continuity during goal-directed activities: knowing where a person looked recently provides a powerful prior for predicting where they look next. Inspired by vision-conditioned autoregressive decoding in vision-language models, we propose ARGaze, which reformulates gaze estimation as sequential prediction: at each timestep, a transformer decoder predicts current gaze by conditioning on (i) current visual features and (ii) a fixed-length Gaze Context Window of recent gaze target estimates. This design enforces causality and enables bounded-resource streaming inference. We achieve state-of-the-art performance across multiple egocentric benchmarks under online evaluation, with extensive ablations validating that autoregressive modeling with bounded gaze history is critical for robust prediction. We will release our source code and pre-trained models.

AI.tldr: ARGaze reframes online egocentric gaze estimation as autoregressive sequential decoding using a transformer decoder conditioned on current visual features and a bounded history of gaze targets; it achieves SOTA on online benchmarks with bounded-resource streaming inference and will release code.
AI.motivation: In egocentric gaze, explicit head/eye cues are unavailable; gaze exhibits temporal continuity, so leveraging recent gaze targets provides a strong prior for predicting the next gaze and enables real-time streaming.
AI.method: A vision-conditioned autoregressive model: at each timestep, a transformer decoder predicts the current gaze, conditioned on current visual features and a fixed-length Gaze Context Window of recent gaze targets; enforces causality for online inference.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on multiple egocentric datasets under online evaluation; ablations indicate autoregressive modeling with bounded gaze history is essential for robustness.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective online gaze estimation via autoregressive decoding with bounded history, enabling real-time, bounded-resource inference; code and models to be released.

title: A Framework for Combining Optimization-Based and Analytic Inverse Kinematics
summary: Analytic and optimization methods for solving inverse kinematics (IK) problems have been deeply studied throughout the history of robotics. The two strategies have complementary strengths and weaknesses, but developing a unified approach to take advantage of both methods has proved challenging. A key challenge faced by optimization approaches is the complicated nonlinear relationship between the joint angles and the end-effector pose. When this must be handled concurrently with additional nonconvex constraints like collision avoidance, optimization IK algorithms may suffer high failure rates. We present a new formulation for optimization IK that uses an analytic IK solution as a change of variables, and is fundamentally easier for optimizers to solve. We test our methodology on three popular solvers, representing three different paradigms for constrained nonlinear optimization. Extensive experimental comparisons demonstrate that our new formulation achieves higher success rates than the old formulation and baseline methods across various challenging IK problems, including collision avoidance, grasp selection, and humanoid stability.

AI.tldr: A new optimization IK formulation uses an analytic IK solution as a change of variables, easing nonconvex optimization and improving success rates under constraints across various IK tasks and solvers.
AI.motivation: Inverse kinematics solved via optimization struggle due to nonlinear joint-pose relations and nonconvex constraints (e.g., collisions), leading to high failure rates; a unified, more tractable approach is sought.
AI.method: Introduce a change-of-variables formulation that parameterizes the problem with an analytic IK solution, making optimization easier to solve; evaluate on three solvers representing different constrained nonlinear optimization paradigms; test on problems including collision avoidance, grasp selection, and humanoid stability.
AI.result: The proposed formulation achieves higher success rates than the old formulation and baseline methods across challenging IK scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Analytic-based change-of-variables for IK improves robustness and solvability of optimization-based IK, indicating broad applicability across constrained IK tasks.

title: Evaluating Large Language Models on Solved and Unsolved Problems in Graph Theory: Implications for Computing Education
summary:
Large Language Models are increasingly used by students to explore advanced material in computer science, including graph theory. As these tools become integrated into undergraduate and graduate coursework, it is important to understand how reliably they support mathematically rigorous thinking. This study examines the performance of a LLM on two related graph theoretic problems: a solved problem concerning the gracefulness of line graphs and an open problem for which no solution is currently known. We use an eight stage evaluation protocol that reflects authentic mathematical inquiry, including interpretation, exploration, strategy formation, and proof construction.
  The model performed strongly on the solved problem, producing correct definitions, identifying relevant structures, recalling appropriate results without hallucination, and constructing a valid proof confirmed by a graph theory expert. For the open problem, the model generated coherent interpretations and plausible exploratory strategies but did not advance toward a solution. It did not fabricate results and instead acknowledged uncertainty, which is consistent with the explicit prompting instructions that directed the model to avoid inventing theorems or unsupported claims.
  These findings indicate that LLMs can support exploration of established material but remain limited in tasks requiring novel mathematical insight or critical structural reasoning. For computing education, this distinction highlights the importance of guiding students to use LLMs for conceptual exploration while relying on independent verification and rigorous argumentation for formal problem solving.

AI.tldr: LLMs can assist in exploring established graph theory material; they perform well on solved problems but struggle with novel mathematical insight; education should use them for conceptual exploration with independent verification for formal solutions.
AI.motivation: Assess how reliably LLMs support mathematically rigorous thinking in CS/graph theory and to inform computing education about appropriate use
AI.method: Eight-stage evaluation protocol simulating authentic mathematical inquiry; test on a solved graph theory problem (gracefulness of line graphs) and an open problem; evaluate interpretation, exploration, strategy formation, proof construction; check hallucinations and expert-validated proofs; explicit prompting to avoid invented theorems
AI.result: On solved problem: correct definitions, relevant structures, recollection of results, valid proof confirmed by graph theory expert. On open problem: coherent interpretations and plausible exploratory strategies but no solution; did not fabricate results; acknowledged uncertainty as prompted
AI.conclusion: LLMs can support exploration of established material but have limited capability for novel mathematical insight or deep structural reasoning; educators should guide students to use LLMs for conceptual exploration and rely on independent verification and rigorous argumentation for formal problem solving

title: Mind the Performance Gap: Capability-Behavior Trade-offs in Feature Steering
summary: Feature steering has emerged as a promising approach for controlling LLM behavior through direct manipulation of internal representations, offering advantages over prompt engineering. However, its practical effectiveness in real-world applications remains poorly understood, particularly regarding potential trade-offs with output quality. We show that feature steering methods substantially degrade model performance even when successfully controlling target behaviors, a critical trade-off. Specifically, we evaluate Goodfire's Auto Steer against prompt engineering baselines across 14 steering queries (covering innocuous and safety-relevant behaviors) on 171 Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU) questions using Llama-8B and Llama-70B, measuring accuracy, coherence, and behavioral control. Our findings show that Auto Steer successfully modifies target behaviors (achieving scores of 3.33 vs. 2.98 for prompting on Llama-8B and 3.57 vs. 3.10 on Llama-70B), but causes dramatic performance degradation: accuracy on the MMLU questions drops from 66% to 46% on Llama-8B and 87% to 73% on Llama-70B, with coherence falling from 4.62 to 2.24 and 4.94 to 3.89 respectively. Simple prompting achieves the best overall balance. These findings highlight limitations of current feature steering methods for practical deployment where task performance cannot be sacrificed. More broadly, our work demonstrates that mechanistic control methods face fundamental capability-behavior trade-offs that must be empirically characterized before deployment.

AI.tldr: Feature steering to control LLM behavior degrades task performance, showing a capability-behavior trade-off that challenges deployment viability.
AI.motivation: Assess practical viability of feature steering (internal representation manipulation) vs prompt-based control by examining performance and control trade-offs on large language models.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation of Goodfire's Auto Steer versus prompt-based baselines across 14 steering queries (innocuous and safety-relevant) on 171 MMLU questions using Llama-8B and Llama-70B. Metrics: accuracy, coherence, and behavioral control.
AI.result: Auto Steer achieves targeted behavior modification (scores slightly higher than prompting on both models) but substantially degrades overall task performance (accuracy drops from 66% to 46% on 8B; 87% to 73% on 70B) and coherence (4.62 to 2.24; 4.94 to 3.89). Simple prompting offers the best overall balance.
AI.conclusion: Mechanistic control methods exhibit fundamental capability-behavior trade-offs; comprehensive empirical characterization is required before deployment, as current feature steering may not be suitable for real-world tasks where performance is critical.

title: AirGlove: Exploring Egocentric 3D Hand Tracking and Appearance Generalization for Sensing Gloves
summary: Sensing gloves have become important tools for teleoperation and robotic policy learning as they are able to provide rich signals like speed, acceleration and tactile feedback. A common approach to track gloved hands is to directly use the sensor signals (e.g., angular velocity, gravity orientation) to estimate 3D hand poses. However, sensor-based tracking can be restrictive in practice as the accuracy is often impacted by sensor signal and calibration quality. Recent advances in vision-based approaches have achieved strong performance on human hands via large-scale pre-training, but their performance on gloved hands with distinct visual appearances remains underexplored. In this work, we present the first systematic evaluation of vision-based hand tracking models on gloved hands under both zero-shot and fine-tuning setups. Our analysis shows that existing bare-hand models suffer from substantial performance degradation on sensing gloves due to large appearance gap between bare-hand and glove designs. We therefore propose AirGlove, which leverages existing gloves to generalize the learned glove representations towards new gloves with limited data. Experiments with multiple sensing gloves show that AirGlove effectively generalizes the hand pose models to new glove designs and achieves a significant performance boost over the compared schemes.

AI.tldr: AirGlove improves vision-based gloved-hand tracking by transferring glove-specific representations to unseen glove designs using limited data, boosting cross-glove generalization over bare-hand baselines.
AI.motivation: To address the performance gap of vision-based bare-hand hand tracking models when applied to sensing gloves, caused by a large appearance gap between bare hands and glove designs, and to enable robust tracking for teleoperation and policy learning.
AI.method: Propose AirGlove, a glove-aware representation transfer framework that leverages existing glove data to generalize learned glove representations to new glove designs with limited additional data; evaluated in zero-shot and fine-tuning setups across multiple sensing gloves.
AI.result: AirGlove yields significant performance gains over competing schemes and demonstrates effective generalization to unseen glove designs in experiments with multiple sensing gloves.
AI.conclusion: Vision-based hand tracking for gloved hands can be substantially improved by glove-aware representation transfer; AirGlove broadens the applicability of vision-based tracking in teleoperation and policy learning contexts involving sensing gloves.

title: Towards Reducible Uncertainty Modeling for Reliable Large Language Model Agents
summary: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) for large language models (LLMs) is a key building block for safety guardrails of daily LLM applications. Yet, even as LLM agents are increasingly deployed in highly complex tasks, most UQ research still centers on single-turn question-answering. We argue that UQ research must shift to realistic settings with interactive agents, and that a new principled framework for agent UQ is needed. This paper presents the first general formulation of agent UQ that subsumes broad classes of existing UQ setups. Under this formulation, we show that prior works implicitly treat LLM UQ as an uncertainty accumulation process, a viewpoint that breaks down for interactive agents in an open world. In contrast, we propose a novel perspective, a conditional uncertainty reduction process, that explicitly models reducible uncertainty over an agent's trajectory by highlighting "interactivity" of actions. From this perspective, we outline a conceptual framework to provide actionable guidance for designing UQ in LLM agent setups. Finally, we conclude with practical implications of the agent UQ in frontier LLM development and domain-specific applications, as well as open remaining problems.

AI.tldr: A general agent UQ framework for interactive LLMs that shifts from an accumulation view to a conditional uncertainty reduction view along the agent’s trajectory, offering a conceptual framework, practical implications, and open problems.
AI.motivation: Safety guardrails for LLMs require reliable uncertainty estimates in interactive, open-world settings; existing UQ work focuses on single-turn QA and lacks a principled treatment for agents.
AI.method: Formulates the first general agent UQ framework that subsumes existing UQ setups; identifies the implicit uncertainty accumulation assumption; introduces a conditional uncertainty reduction process that captures reducible uncertainty through agent interactivity; outlines a conceptual framework for designing UQ in LLM agent settings.
AI.result: Demonstrates that prior accumulation-based views break down for interactive agents, proposes a new conditional reduction perspective, and provides a framework plus discussion on practical implications and remaining open problems.
AI.conclusion: Agent UQ is essential for frontier LLM development and domain-specific applications; numerous open problems remain to be addressed in interactive, real-world agent settings.

title: DCER: Dual-Stage Compression and Energy-Based Reconstruction
comment: 13 pages, 2 figures, 8 tables. Submitted to ICML 2026. Code will be available on GitHub
summary:
Multimodal fusion faces two robustness challenges: noisy inputs degrade representation quality, and missing modalities cause prediction failures. We propose DCER, a
  unified framework addressing both challenges through dual-stage compression and energy-based reconstruction. The compression stage operates at two levels:
  within-modality frequency transforms (wavelet for audio, DCT for video) remove noise while preserving task-relevant patterns, and cross-modality bottleneck tokens
  force genuine integration rather than modality-specific shortcuts. For missing modalities, energy-based reconstruction recovers representations via gradient descent
  on a learned energy function, with the final energy providing intrinsic uncertainty quantification (\r{ho} > 0.72 correlation with prediction error). Experiments on
  CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, and CH-SIMS demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across all benchmarks, with a U-shaped robustness pattern favoring multimodal fusion at
  both complete and high-missing conditions. The code will be available on Github.

AI.tldr: DCER provides robust multimodal fusion via dual-stage compression and energy-based reconstruction to handle noise and missing modalities, achieving state-of-the-art results on MOSI, MOSEI, and CH-SIMS with intrinsic uncertainty estimation.
AI.motivation: Robustness gaps in multimodal fusion arise from noisy inputs and missing modalities, and current methods lack a unified approach and intrinsic uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Two-stage compression: within-modality denoising via frequency transforms (wavelet for audio, DCT for video) and cross-modality bottleneck tokens to enforce genuine integration; energy-based reconstruction for missing modalities via gradient descent on a learned energy function, with the energy serving as an uncertainty signal.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, and CH-SIMS; observed a U-shaped robustness pattern, with strong fusion performance under complete and high-missing conditions; intrinsic uncertainty correlates with prediction error (R>0.72).
AI.conclusion: DCER delivers a unified, robust multimodal fusion framework combining dual-stage compression and energy-based reconstruction, achieving strong performance and providing principled uncertainty estimates; code will be released on GitHub.

title: SHaSaM: Submodular Hard Sample Mining for Fair Facial Attribute Recognition
summary: Deep neural networks often inherit social and demographic biases from annotated data during model training, leading to unfair predictions, especially in the presence of sensitive attributes like race, age, gender etc. Existing methods fall prey to the inherent data imbalance between attribute groups and inadvertently emphasize on sensitive attributes, worsening unfairness and performance. To surmount these challenges, we propose SHaSaM (Submodular Hard Sample Mining), a novel combinatorial approach that models fairness-driven representation learning as a submodular hard-sample mining problem. Our two-stage approach comprises of SHaSaM-MINE, which introduces a submodular subset selection strategy to mine hard positives and negatives - effectively mitigating data imbalance, and SHaSaM-LEARN, which introduces a family of combinatorial loss functions based on Submodular Conditional Mutual Information to maximize the decision boundary between target classes while minimizing the influence of sensitive attributes. This unified formulation restricts the model from learning features tied to sensitive attributes, significantly enhancing fairness without sacrificing performance. Experiments on CelebA and UTKFace demonstrate that SHaSaM achieves state-of-the-art results, with up to 2.7 points improvement in model fairness (Equalized Odds) and a 3.5% gain in Accuracy, within fewer epochs as compared to existing methods.

AI.tldr: Two-stage SHaSaM framework uses submodular hard-sample mining and Submodular Conditional Mutual Information losses to achieve fairer representations under data imbalance, achieving state-of-the-art Equalized Odds and accuracy on CelebA and UTKFace.
AI.motivation: Deep neural networks inherit biases from annotated data, with data imbalance across attribute groups causing unfair predictions. Existing methods may emphasize sensitive attributes, worsening fairness. A method that effectively mines hard samples and minimizes sensitive attribute leakage is needed.
AI.method: SHaSaM-MINE performs submodular subset selection to mine hard positives/negatives, mitigating data imbalance. SHaSaM-LEARN introduces a family of combinatorial losses based on Submodular Conditional Mutual Information to maximize the decision boundary between target classes while minimizing the influence of sensitive attributes. The approach yields a unified framework that discourages learning features tied to sensitive attributes.
AI.result: Experiments on CelebA and UTKFace show state-of-the-art results, with up to 2.7 points improvement in Equalized Odds and a 3.5% gain in Accuracy, achieved in fewer epochs than competing methods.
AI.conclusion: The unified combinatorial formulation effectively restrains learning of sensitive attribute features without sacrificing performance, yielding notable gains in fairness and accuracy on standard benchmarks.

title: Optimizing Mission Planning for Multi-Debris Rendezvous Using Reinforcement Learning with Refueling and Adaptive Collision Avoidance
comment: Accpeted at Conference: 15th IAA Symposium on Small Satellites for Earth System Observation At: Berlin
summary:
As the orbital environment around Earth becomes increasingly crowded with debris, active debris removal (ADR) missions face significant challenges in ensuring safe operations while minimizing the risk of in-orbit collisions. This study presents a reinforcement learning (RL) based framework to enhance adaptive collision avoidance in ADR missions, specifically for multi-debris removal using small satellites. Small satellites are increasingly adopted due to their flexibility, cost effectiveness, and maneuverability, making them well suited for dynamic missions such as ADR.
  Building on existing work in multi-debris rendezvous, the framework integrates refueling strategies, efficient mission planning, and adaptive collision avoidance to optimize spacecraft rendezvous operations. The proposed approach employs a masked Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm, enabling the RL agent to dynamically adjust maneuvers in response to real-time orbital conditions. Key considerations include fuel efficiency, avoidance of active collision zones, and optimization of dynamic orbital parameters.
  The RL agent learns to determine efficient sequences for rendezvousing with multiple debris targets, optimizing fuel usage and mission time while incorporating necessary refueling stops. Simulated ADR scenarios derived from the Iridium 33 debris dataset are used for evaluation, covering diverse orbital configurations and debris distributions to demonstrate robustness and adaptability. Results show that the proposed RL framework reduces collision risk while improving mission efficiency compared to traditional heuristic approaches.
  This work provides a scalable solution for planning complex multi-debris ADR missions and is applicable to other multi-target rendezvous problems in autonomous space mission planning.

AI.tldr: An RL-based framework using masked PPO for adaptive collision avoidance in multi-debris ADR missions with small satellites, featuring refueling and efficient mission planning; evaluated on Iridium 33 debris scenarios showing reduced collision risk and improved efficiency over heuristics.
AI.motivation: Address increasing debris congestion by enabling safe, fuel-efficient, multi-target rendezvous using small satellites; leverage reinforcement learning to adapt to real-time orbital conditions and avoid active collision zones.
AI.method: Masked Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) reinforcement learning agent integrated with refueling strategies and dynamic mission planning for multi-debris rendezvous. Simulation-based evaluation using Iridium 33 debris datasets to model diverse orbital configurations and debris distributions; focuses on fuel efficiency, collision avoidance, and dynamic orbital parameter optimization.
AI.result: The RL framework reduces collision risk and improves mission efficiency relative to traditional heuristic approaches; demonstrates robustness across varied orbital configurations and supports scalable planning for multi-debris ADR missions.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a scalable solution for complex multi-debris ADR missions and can generalize to other multi-target rendezvous in autonomous space mission planning.

title: LISA: Laplacian In-context Spectral Analysis
summary: We propose Laplacian In-context Spectral Analysis (LISA), a method for inference-time adaptation of Laplacian-based time-series models using only an observed prefix. LISA combines delay-coordinate embeddings and Laplacian spectral learning to produce diffusion-coordinate state representations, together with a frozen nonlinear decoder for one-step prediction. We introduce lightweight latent-space residual adapters based on either Gaussian-process regression or an attention-like Markov operator over context windows. Across forecasting and autoregressive rollout experiments, LISA improves over the frozen baseline and is often most beneficial under changing dynamics. This work links in-context adaptation to nonparametric spectral methods for dynamical systems.

AI.tldr: LISA enables inference-time adaptation of Laplacian-based time-series models via delay-coordinate embeddings, spectral learning, and lightweight latent adapters, improving forecasting under changing dynamics.
AI.motivation: Address nonstationary, regime-shifting dynamics by enabling quick, data-driven adaptation at inference time without retraining, leveraging nonparametric spectral methods for dynamical systems.
AI.method: Integrates delay-coordinate embeddings with Laplacian spectral learning to form diffusion-coordinate state representations; uses a frozen nonlinear decoder for one-step prediction; introduces lightweight latent-space residual adapters based on Gaussian-process regression or an attention-like Markov operator over context windows.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across forecasting and autoregressive rollout tasks shows LISA improves over the frozen baseline, with the largest gains when dynamics change.
AI.conclusion: Connects in-context adaptation to nonparametric spectral methods for dynamical systems, offering a practical framework for fast, nonparametric adaptation of Laplacian-based time-series models.

title: LOBSTgER-enhance: an underwater image enhancement pipeline
comment: 12 pages, 30 figures, work done as part of LOBSTgER
summary:
Underwater photography presents significant inherent challenges including reduced contrast, spatial blur, and wavelength-dependent color distortions. These effects can obscure the vibrancy of marine life and awareness photographers in particular are often challenged with heavy post-processing pipelines to correct for these distortions.
  We develop an image-to-image pipeline that learns to reverse underwater degradations by introducing a synthetic corruption pipeline and learning to reverse its effects with diffusion-based generation. Training and evaluation are performed on a small high-quality dataset of awareness photography images by Keith Ellenbogen. The proposed methodology achieves high perceptual consistency and strong generalization in synthesizing 512x768 images using a model of ~11M parameters after training from scratch on ~2.5k images.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based underwater restoration using a learned inverse of synthetic corruption, trained from a small, high-quality dataset to produce high-res (512×768) images with ~11M parameters.
AI.motivation: Underwater imagery suffers from reduced contrast, blur, and wavelength-dependent color distortions; conventional pipelines rely on heavy post-processing; a data-driven reversal framework is needed, especially with limited high-quality data.
AI.method: Introduce an image-to-image pipeline that first simulates underwater degradations, then trains a diffusion-based model from scratch on ~2.5k images (Keith Ellenbogen), yielding an ~11M-parameter network that outputs 512×768 images.
AI.result: Achieves high perceptual consistency and strong generalization in synthesizing 512×768 outputs on the target dataset.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of diffusion-based restoration for underwater scenes from small curated datasets, enabling high-resolution outputs with a modest model size.

title: MobileManiBench: Simplifying Model Verification for Mobile Manipulation
summary: Vision-language-action models have advanced robotic manipulation but remain constrained by reliance on the large, teleoperation-collected datasets dominated by the static, tabletop scenes. We propose a simulation-first framework to verify VLA architectures before real-world deployment and introduce MobileManiBench, a large-scale benchmark for mobile-based robotic manipulation. Built on NVIDIA Isaac Sim and powered by reinforcement learning, our pipeline autonomously generates diverse manipulation trajectories with rich annotations (language instructions, multi-view RGB-depth-segmentation images, synchronized object/robot states and actions). MobileManiBench features 2 mobile platforms (parallel-gripper and dexterous-hand robots), 2 synchronized cameras (head and right wrist), 630 objects in 20 categories, 5 skills (open, close, pull, push, pick) with over 100 tasks performed in 100 realistic scenes, yielding 300K trajectories. This design enables controlled, scalable studies of robot embodiments, sensing modalities, and policy architectures, accelerating research on data efficiency and generalization. We benchmark representative VLA models and report insights into perception, reasoning, and control in complex simulated environments.

AI.tldr: A simulation-first framework MobileManiBench for mobile-based robotic manipulation, enabling large-scale, annotated RL-generated data and benchmarking of vision-language-action models in a controlled simulated setting.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of teleoperation-dominated, static tabletop datasets and provide controlled, scalable evaluation of VLA architectures across embodiments, sensing modalities, and policies before real-world deployment.
AI.method: Build MobileManiBench on NVIDIA Isaac Sim. Use reinforcement learning to autonomously generate diverse manipulation trajectories with rich annotations (language instructions, multi-view RGB-D segmentation, synchronized object/robot states and actions). Two mobile platforms (parallel-gripper and dexterous-hand), two synchronized cameras (head and right wrist), 630 objects in 20 categories, 5 skills (open, close, pull, push, pick) across 100 tasks in 100 realistic scenes, yielding 300K trajectories.
AI.result: Benchmark representative vision-language-action models and derive insights into perception, reasoning, and control in complex simulated environments; demonstrate the utility of a scalable, annotated simulation benchmark for data efficiency and generalization studies.
AI.conclusion: A simulation-first framework and MobileManiBench enable controlled, scalable evaluation of VLA policies across embodiments and sensing modalities, accelerating research toward data-efficient and generalizable robotic manipulation.

title: VERA-MH: Reliability and Validity of an Open-Source AI Safety Evaluation in Mental Health
summary: Millions now use leading generative AI chatbots for psychological support. Despite the promise related to availability and scale, the single most pressing question in AI for mental health is whether these tools are safe. The Validation of Ethical and Responsible AI in Mental Health (VERA-MH) evaluation was recently proposed to meet the urgent need for an evidence-based automated safety benchmark. This study aimed to examine the clinical validity and reliability of the VERA-MH evaluation for AI safety in suicide risk detection and response. We first simulated a large set of conversations between large language model (LLM)-based users (user-agents) and general-purpose AI chatbots. Licensed mental health clinicians used a rubric (scoring guide) to independently rate the simulated conversations for safe and unsafe chatbot behaviors, as well as user-agent realism. An LLM-based judge used the same scoring rubric to evaluate the same set of simulated conversations. We then compared rating alignment across (a) individual clinicians and (b) clinician consensus and the LLM judge, and (c) examined clinicians' ratings of user-agent realism. Individual clinicians were generally consistent with one another in their safety ratings (chance-corrected inter-rater reliability [IRR]: 0.77), thus establishing a gold-standard clinical reference. The LLM judge was strongly aligned with this clinical consensus (IRR: 0.81) overall and within key conditions. Clinician raters generally perceived the user-agents to be realistic. For the potential mental health benefits of AI chatbots to be realized, attention to safety is paramount. Findings from this human evaluation study support the clinical validity and reliability of VERA-MH: an open-source, fully automated AI safety evaluation for mental health. Further research will address VERA-MH generalizability and robustness.

AI.tldr: VERA-MH is validated as a clinically aligned, automated safety benchmark for mental-health AI, showing strong agreement with clinician judgments on safety and realism in simulated suicide-risk conversations; open-source and automated, with generalizability and robustness to be tested.
AI.motivation: There is an urgent need for evidence-based automated safety evaluation benchmarks for AI deployments in mental health to ensure safe suicide-risk detection and response.
AI.method: Simulated large-scale conversations between user-agents and general-purpose AI chatbots; licensed mental health clinicians rate each conversation for safe/unsafe chatbot behaviors and user-agent realism using a rubric; an LLM-based judge applies the same rubric to the same conversations; inter-method and inter-rater reliability assessed by comparing clinician ratings, clinician consensus, and LLM judge; additionally assess realism perceptions.
AI.result: Clinicians showed good consistency (IRR = 0.77). The LLM judge aligned strongly with clinical consensus (IRR = 0.81) overall and in key conditions. Clinicians generally rated user-agents as realistic. VERA-MH is open-source and fully automated, supporting clinical validity and reliability of AI safety evaluation; generalizability and robustness require further research.
AI.conclusion: The study supports VERA-MH as a valid, reliable automated safety evaluation framework for mental health AI, with plans to test generalizability and robustness in future work.

title: Physics as the Inductive Bias for Causal Discovery
summary: Causal discovery is often a data-driven paradigm to analyze complex real-world systems. In parallel, physics-based models such as ordinary differential equations (ODEs) provide mechanistic structure for many dynamical processes. Integrating these paradigms potentially allows physical knowledge to act as an inductive bias, improving identifiability, stability, and robustness of causal discovery in dynamical systems. However, such integration remains challenging: real dynamical systems often exhibit feedback, cyclic interactions, and non-stationary data trend, while many widely used causal discovery methods are formulated under acyclicity or equilibrium-based assumptions. In this work, we propose an integrative causal discovery framework for dynamical systems that leverages partial physical knowledge as an inductive bias. Specifically, we model system evolution as a stochastic differential equation (SDE), where the drift term encodes known ODE dynamics and the diffusion term corresponds to unknown causal couplings beyond the prescribed physics. We develop a scalable sparsity-inducing MLE algorithm that exploits causal graph structure for efficient parameter estimation. Under mild conditions, we establish guarantees to recover the causal graph. Experiments on dynamical systems with diverse causal structures show that our approach improves causal graph recovery and produces more stable, physically consistent estimates than purely data-driven state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: Integrates known ODE physics into SDE-based causal discovery by modeling drift with known dynamics and diffusion for unknown couplings; proposes a sparsity-inducing MLE leveraging graph structure; provides guarantees for causal graph recovery; empirical results show improved recovery and physical consistency over baselines.
AI.motivation: Causal discovery in dynamical systems benefits from physics-informed inductive bias to improve identifiability, stability, and robustness in the presence of feedback, cycles, and non-stationarity, where purely data-driven methods struggle.
AI.method: Model system evolution as a stochastic differential equation with drift encoding prescribed ODE dynamics; diffusion term captures unknown causal couplings beyond the prescribed physics; develop scalable sparsity-inducing maximum likelihood estimation that exploits causal graph structure for efficient parameter estimation.
AI.result: Under mild conditions, guarantees to recover the causal graph are established; experiments on dynamical systems with diverse causal structures show improved causal graph recovery and more stable, physically consistent estimates compared to purely data-driven baselines.
AI.conclusion: Partial physical knowledge can serve as an effective inductive bias for dynamical causal discovery, yielding better graph recovery and physically plausible estimates; the framework is scalable and robust across various causal structures.

title: ShapePuri: Shape Guided and Appearance Generalized Adversarial Purification
summary: Deep neural networks demonstrate impressive performance in visual recognition, but they remain vulnerable to adversarial attacks that is imperceptible to the human. Although existing defense strategies such as adversarial training and purification have achieved progress, diffusion-based purification often involves high computational costs and information loss. To address these challenges, we introduce Shape Guided Purification (ShapePuri), a novel defense framework enhances robustness by aligning model representations with stable structural invariants. ShapePuri integrates two components: a Shape Encoding Module (SEM) that provides dense geometric guidance through Signed Distance Functions (SDF), and a Global Appearance Debiasing (GAD) module that mitigates appearance bias via stochastic transformations. In our experiments, ShapePuri achieves $84.06\%$ clean accuracy and $81.64\%$ robust accuracy under the AutoAttack protocol, representing the first defense framework to surpass the $80\%$ threshold on this benchmark. Our approach provides a scalable and efficient adversarial defense that preserves prediction stability during inference without requiring auxiliary modules or additional computational cost.

AI.tldr: A shape-guided purification framework (ShapePuri) improves adversarial robustness by combining a Shape Encoding Module (SDF-based geometry) and a Global Appearance Debiasing module, achieving over 80% robust accuracy on AutoAttack while maintaining efficiency and accuracy.
AI.motivation: To overcome computationally expensive diffusion-based purifications and information loss, and to align model representations with stable geometric invariants for robust vision.
AI.method: Two-part design: SEM provides dense geometric guidance via Signed Distance Functions; GAD applies stochastic transformations to reduce appearance bias; integration yields shape-informed purification with no extra inference cost or auxiliary modules.
AI.result: On AutoAttack, ShapePuri attains 84.06% clean accuracy and 81.64% robust accuracy, surpassing the 80% robust accuracy benchmark for the first time.
AI.conclusion: ShapePuri offers a scalable, efficient adversarial defense that preserves prediction stability during inference without additional computational burden.

title: Low-Cost Underwater In-Pipe Centering and Inspection Using a Minimal-Sensing Robot
summary: Autonomous underwater inspection of submerged pipelines is challenging due to confined geometries, turbidity, and the scarcity of reliable localization cues. This paper presents a minimal-sensing strategy that enables a free-swimming underwater robot to center itself and traverse a flooded pipe of known radius using only an IMU, a pressure sensor, and two sonars: a downward-facing single-beam sonar and a rotating 360 degree sonar. We introduce a computationally efficient method for extracting range estimates from single-beam sonar intensity data, enabling reliable wall detection in noisy and reverberant conditions. A closed-form geometric model leverages the two sonar ranges to estimate the pipe center, and an adaptive, confidence-weighted proportional-derivative (PD) controller maintains alignment during traversal. The system requires no Doppler velocity log, external tracking, or complex multi-sensor arrays. Experiments in a submerged 46 cm-diameter pipe using a Blue Robotics BlueROV2 heavy remotely operated vehicle demonstrate stable centering and successful full-pipe traversal despite ambient flow and structural deformations. These results show that reliable in-pipe navigation and inspection can be achieved with a lightweight, computationally efficient sensing and processing architecture, advancing the practicality of autonomous underwater inspection in confined environments.

AI.tldr: Minimal-sensing in-pipe navigation for submerged pipelines using IMU, pressure sensor, and two sonars; closed-form geometry to estimate pipe center; adaptive PD controller; validated in a 46 cm pipe; lightweight, robust approach.
AI.motivation: In-water inspection inside submerged pipelines is hampered by restricted geometry, turbidity, and scarce localization cues; a low-cost, single-system sensing approach is desirable.
AI.method: Combine IMU, pressure sensor, downward single-beam sonar and a rotating 360° sonar; extract range estimates from single-beam intensity data; use a closed-form geometry to estimate pipe center from the two ranges; apply an adaptive confidence-weighted PD controller for centering during traversal; no DVL or external tracking; tested on BlueROV2 in a 46 cm pipe.
AI.result: Demonstrated stable centering and successful full-pipe traversal under ambient flow and deformations; robust to noise and reverberation; computes efficiently on lightweight hardware.
AI.conclusion: Shows that reliable in-pipe navigation and inspection is achievable with a lightweight, computationally efficient sensing/processing stack, increasing practicality of autonomous underwater inspection in confined environments.

title: Evaluating Robustness and Adaptability in Learning-Based Mission Planning for Active Debris Removal
comment: Presented at Conference: International Conference on Space Robotics (ISPARO,2025) At: Sendai,Japan
summary: Autonomous mission planning for Active Debris Removal (ADR) must balance efficiency, adaptability, and strict feasibility constraints on fuel and mission duration. This work compares three planners for the constrained multi-debris rendezvous problem in Low Earth Orbit: a nominal Masked Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) policy trained under fixed mission parameters, a domain-randomized Masked PPO policy trained across varying mission constraints for improved robustness, and a plain Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) baseline. Evaluations are conducted in a high-fidelity orbital simulation with refueling, realistic transfer dynamics, and randomized debris fields across 300 test cases in nominal, reduced fuel, and reduced mission time scenarios. Results show that nominal PPO achieves top performance when conditions match training but degrades sharply under distributional shift, while domain-randomized PPO exhibits improved adaptability with only moderate loss in nominal performance. MCTS consistently handles constraint changes best due to online replanning but incurs orders-of-magnitude higher computation time. The findings underline a trade-off between the speed of learned policies and the adaptability of search-based methods, and suggest that combining training-time diversity with online planning could be a promising path for future resilient ADR mission planners.

AI.tldr: A comparison of three planners for constrained multi-debris rendezvous in LEO: nominal PPO trained on fixed parameters, domain-randomized PPO trained across varied constraints, and MCTS. Domain-randomized PPO offers robustness under distributional shifts; MCTS provides best constraint adaptability but with high computation; a hybrid approach may be most promising.
AI.motivation: Autonomous ADR mission planning must balance efficiency, adaptability, and strict feasibility constraints (fuel, time) in a dynamic debris field, justifying a comparison of learning-based and search-based planners.
AI.method: Three planners are evaluated in a high-fidelity orbital simulation with refueling, realistic transfer dynamics, and randomized debris fields across 300 test cases under nominal, reduced fuel, and reduced time scenarios: (1) nominal Masked PPO trained on fixed mission parameters, (2) domain-randomized Masked PPO trained across varying constraints, and (3) plain Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) baseline.
AI.result: Nominal PPO excels when test conditions match training; performance degrades under distributional shift. Domain-randomized PPO shows improved adaptability with modest nominal performance loss. MCTS handles constraint changes best due to online replanning but requires orders-of-magnitude more computation time.
AI.conclusion: There is a trade-off between rapid, once-trained policies and the adaptability of search-based planning; incorporating training-time diversity with online planning may yield resilient ADR mission planners.

title: Temporal Pair Consistency for Variance-Reduced Flow Matching
summary: Continuous-time generative models, such as diffusion models, flow matching, and rectified flow, learn time-dependent vector fields but are typically trained with objectives that treat timesteps independently, leading to high estimator variance and inefficient sampling. Prior approaches mitigate this via explicit smoothness penalties, trajectory regularization, or modified probability paths and solvers. We introduce Temporal Pair Consistency (TPC), a lightweight variance-reduction principle that couples velocity predictions at paired timesteps along the same probability path, operating entirely at the estimator level without modifying the model architecture, probability path, or solver. We provide a theoretical analysis showing that TPC induces a quadratic, trajectory-coupled regularization that provably reduces gradient variance while preserving the underlying flow-matching objective. Instantiated within flow matching, TPC improves sample quality and efficiency across CIFAR-10 and ImageNet at multiple resolutions, achieving lower FID at identical or lower computational cost than prior methods, and extends seamlessly to modern SOTA-style pipelines with noise-augmented training, score-based denoising, and rectified flow.

AI.tldr: Temporal Pair Consistency (TPC) is a lightweight variance-reduction technique for continuous-time generative models that couples velocity predictions at paired timesteps along the same probability path, reducing gradient variance without changing the model, path, or solver, and improving sample quality and efficiency across datasets and modern pipelines.
AI.motivation: Estimators for time-dependent vector fields in diffusion/flow-matching frameworks suffer high variance due to treating timesteps independently; existing remedies (smoothness penalties, trajectory regularization, path/solver modifications) add complexity or restrict flexibility. A simple, estimator-level variance-reduction method is sought.
AI.method: Introduce Temporal Pair Consistency (TPC), coupling velocity predictions at paired timesteps along the same probability path. This induces a quadratic trajectory-coupled regularization at the estimator level, reducing gradient variance while preserving the original flow-matching objective. No changes to model architecture, probability path, or solver. Theoretical analysis shows variance reduction; implemented within flow matching, compatible with noise-augmented training, score-based denoising, and rectified flow.
AI.result: Empirical improvements: within flow matching, TPC improves sample quality and efficiency on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet across resolutions, achieving lower FID at equal or lower computational cost than prior methods.
AI.conclusion: TPC offers a principled, low-overhead variance reduction that preserves the original objective and extends to state-of-the-art pipelines, yielding better or comparable sample quality with less variance and without architectural/solver changes.

title: PoseGaussian: Pose-Driven Novel View Synthesis for Robust 3D Human Reconstruction
summary: We propose PoseGaussian, a pose-guided Gaussian Splatting framework for high-fidelity human novel view synthesis. Human body pose serves a dual purpose in our design: as a structural prior, it is fused with a color encoder to refine depth estimation; as a temporal cue, it is processed by a dedicated pose encoder to enhance temporal consistency across frames. These components are integrated into a fully differentiable, end-to-end trainable pipeline. Unlike prior works that use pose only as a condition or for warping, PoseGaussian embeds pose signals into both geometric and temporal stages to improve robustness and generalization. It is specifically designed to address challenges inherent in dynamic human scenes, such as articulated motion and severe self-occlusion. Notably, our framework achieves real-time rendering at 100 FPS, maintaining the efficiency of standard Gaussian Splatting pipelines. We validate our approach on ZJU-MoCap, THuman2.0, and in-house datasets, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance in perceptual quality and structural accuracy (PSNR 30.86, SSIM 0.979, LPIPS 0.028).

AI.tldr: PoseGaussian introduces pose-guided Gaussian Splatting for real-time, high-fidelity human novel-view synthesis by embedding pose signals into both geometry and temporal pathways, achieving 100 FPS and state-of-the-art metrics.
AI.motivation: Dynamic human scene rendering faces challenges from articulated motion and self-occlusion. Traditional approaches treat pose only as a condition or for warping, offering limited robustness and generalization. An end-to-end approach that fuses pose into both geometric and temporal modules aims to improve depth estimation, temporal consistency, and overall robustness.
AI.method: PoseGaussian integrates pose as a structural prior—fused with a color encoder to refine depth—and as a temporal cue—processed by a dedicated pose encoder to bolster temporal consistency. These components operate within a fully differentiable, end-to-end trainable Gaussian Splatting pipeline, enabling real-time rendering (~100 FPS). The model is evaluated on ZJU-MoCap, THuman2.0, and in-house datasets.
AI.result: The method achieves state-of-the-art perceptual quality and structural accuracy, with PSNR 30.86, SSIM 0.979, and LPIPS 0.028, while maintaining real-time performance.
AI.conclusion: Embedding pose signals into both geometric and temporal stages enhances robustness and generalization for dynamic human scenes, enabling high-fidelity, real-time novel-view synthesis as demonstrated by strong results across multiple datasets.

title: GAMMS: Graph based Adversarial Multiagent Modeling Simulator
