Extensive experiments across 11 embedding models--spanning static, contextualized, and universal types--demonstrate that recos consistently outperforms traditional cosine similarity, achieving higher correlation with human judgments on standard Semantic Textual Similarity (STS) benchmarks. Our work establishes recos as a mathematically principled and empirically superior alternative, offering enhanced accuracy for semantic analysis in complex embedding spaces.

AI.tldr: Introduces recos, a dot-product-based similarity that uses a tighter bound than Cauchy–Schwarz by normalizing the dot product with sorted vector components, enabling ordinal concordance-based similarity; shows improved STS correlations across 11 embedding models.
AI.motivation: Cosine similarity inherits Cauchy–Schwarz constraints, failing to capture nonlinear/semi-ordinal semantic relationships; need a metric with tighter theoretical bound and broader expressivity.
AI.method: Derives a tighter upper bound for dot product; defines recos as dot product normalized by sorted components; evaluates on 11 embedding models (static, contextualized, universal) across standard STS benchmarks; reports correlations with human judgments.
AI.result: Recos consistently outperforms cosine in STS, achieving higher correlation with human judgments across diverse models.
AI.conclusion: Recos provides a principled, empirically superior alternative for semantic similarity in complex embedding spaces; practical implications for semantic analysis and downstream tasks.

title: Euphonium: Steering Video Flow Matching via Process Reward Gradient Guided Stochastic Dynamics
summary: While online Reinforcement Learning has emerged as a crucial technique for aligning flow matching models with human preferences, current approaches are hindered by inefficient exploration during training rollouts. Relying on undirected stochasticity and sparse outcome rewards, these methods struggle to discover high-reward samples, resulting in data-inefficient and slow optimization. To address these limitations, we propose Euphonium, a novel framework that steers generation via process reward gradient guided dynamics. Our key insight is to formulate the sampling process as a theoretically principled Stochastic Differential Equation that explicitly incorporates the gradient of a Process Reward Model into the flow drift. This design enables dense, step-by-step steering toward high-reward regions, advancing beyond the unguided exploration in prior works, and theoretically encompasses existing sampling methods (e.g., Flow-GRPO, DanceGRPO) as special cases. We further derive a distillation objective that internalizes the guidance signal into the flow network, eliminating inference-time dependency on the reward model. We instantiate this framework with a Dual-Reward Group Relative Policy Optimization algorithm, combining latent process rewards for efficient credit assignment with pixel-level outcome rewards for final visual fidelity. Experiments on text-to-video generation show that Euphonium achieves better alignment compared to existing methods while accelerating training convergence by 1.66x.

AI.tldr: Euphonium is a principled SDE-based framework that guides generation via the gradient of a Process Reward Model, with a distillation objective to embed guidance into the flow network. It unifies prior sampling methods (Flow-GRPO, DanceGRPO) as special cases and improves data efficiency, achieving faster convergence and better alignment in text-to-video generation.
AI.motivation: Mitigate inefficient exploration and sparse rewards in online RL for aligning flow-based generative models with human preferences, aiming for data-efficient, faster optimization.
AI.method: Model sampling as a stochastic differential equation whose drift includes the gradient of a Process Reward Model. Derive a distillation objective to internalize guidance into the flow network, eliminating inference-time reward-model dependence. Instantiate with a Dual-Reward Group Relative Policy Optimization algorithm that uses latent process rewards for credit assignment and pixel-level outcome rewards for final fidelity.
AI.result: Empirical results on text-to-video generation show improved alignment over baselines and a 1.66x acceleration in training convergence.
AI.conclusion: Euphonium offers a theoretically principled, more data-efficient framework for steering generative flows via reward gradients, unifying existing sampling approaches and enabling reward-free inference after distillation; it demonstrates strong alignment and faster convergence in the evaluated domain.

title: Fast-SAM3D: 3Dfy Anything in Images but Faster
summary: SAM3D enables scalable, open-world 3D reconstruction from complex scenes, yet its deployment is hindered by prohibitive inference latency. In this work, we conduct the \textbf{first systematic investigation} into its inference dynamics, revealing that generic acceleration strategies are brittle in this context. We demonstrate that these failures stem from neglecting the pipeline's inherent multi-level \textbf{heterogeneity}: the kinematic distinctiveness between shape and layout, the intrinsic sparsity of texture refinement, and the spectral variance across geometries. To address this, we present \textbf{Fast-SAM3D}, a training-free framework that dynamically aligns computation with instantaneous generation complexity. Our approach integrates three heterogeneity-aware mechanisms: (1) \textit{Modality-Aware Step Caching} to decouple structural evolution from sensitive layout updates; (2) \textit{Joint Spatiotemporal Token Carving} to concentrate refinement on high-entropy regions; and (3) \textit{Spectral-Aware Token Aggregation} to adapt decoding resolution. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Fast-SAM3D delivers up to \textbf{2.67$\times$} end-to-end speedup with negligible fidelity loss, establishing a new Pareto frontier for efficient single-view 3D generation. Our code is released in https://github.com/wlfeng0509/Fast-SAM3D.

AI.tldr: Training-free, heterogeneity-aware acceleration for SAM3D yielding up to 2.67x speedup with minimal fidelity loss.
AI.motivation: SAM3D's substantial inference latency; standard acceleration tricks fail due to neglected multi-level heterogeneity in the pipeline (shape vs layout dynamics, sparse texture refinement, and geometry-dependent spectral variance).
AI.method: Three heterogeneity-aware mechanisms: (1) Modality-Aware Step Caching to separate structural updates from layout changes; (2) Joint Spatiotemporal Token Carving to focus refinement on high-entropy regions; (3) Spectral-Aware Token Aggregation to adapt decoding resolution. The framework is training-free and dynamically aligns computation with instantaneous generation complexity.
AI.result: Up to 2.67× end-to-end speedup with negligible fidelity loss; establishes a new Pareto frontier for efficient single-view 3D generation; code released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that accounting for pipeline heterogeneity enables efficient inference in large-scale 3D generation; the proposed mechanisms yield substantial speedups without sacrificing quality and may generalize to other generative pipelines with similar multi-level heterogeneity.

title: Hallucination-Resistant Security Planning with a Large Language Model
comment: Accepted to IEEE/IFIP Network Operations and Management Symposium 2026. To appear in the conference proceedings
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are promising tools for supporting security management tasks, such as incident response planning. However, their unreliability and tendency to hallucinate remain significant challenges. In this paper, we address these challenges by introducing a principled framework for using an LLM as decision support in security management. Our framework integrates the LLM in an iterative loop where it generates candidate actions that are checked for consistency with system constraints and lookahead predictions. When consistency is low, we abstain from the generated actions and instead collect external feedback, e.g., by evaluating actions in a digital twin. This feedback is then used to refine the candidate actions through in-context learning (ICL). We prove that this design allows to control the hallucination risk by tuning the consistency threshold. Moreover, we establish a bound on the regret of ICL under certain assumptions. To evaluate our framework, we apply it to an incident response use case where the goal is to generate a response and recovery plan based on system logs. Experiments on four public datasets show that our framework reduces recovery times by up to 30% compared to frontier LLMs.

AI.tldr: A principled LLM-based decision-support framework for incident response that abstains when actions conflict with system constraints and uses external feedback via a digital twin to refine actions through in-context learning, reducing recovery times by up to 30% on four datasets.
AI.motivation: LLMs are unreliable and prone to hallucinations, which is risky for security management. The work proposes a safety-first framework to leverage LLMs for incident response while enforcing constraints and incorporating external feedback to improve decisions.
AI.method: An iterative loop where the LLM proposes candidate actions, which are checked for consistency with system constraints and lookahead predictions. If consistency is low, actions are rejected and external feedback (e.g., from a digital twin) is gathered and used to refine candidates via in-context learning (ICL). The approach includes tuning a consistency threshold to control hallucination risk and provides a theoretical bound on ICL regret under certain assumptions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four public datasets shows the framework reduces incident recovery times by up to 30% compared to frontier LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates safer use of LLMs for security management by combining constraint-based checks, abstention with external feedback, and ICL, yielding significant performance gains and theoretical guarantees on hallucination risk and ICL regret.

title: FlashBlock: Attention Caching for Efficient Long-Context Block Diffusion
summary: Generating long-form content, such as minute-long videos and extended texts, is increasingly important for modern generative models. Block diffusion improves inference efficiency via KV caching and block-wise causal inference and has been widely adopted in diffusion language models and video generation. However, in long-context settings, block diffusion still incurs substantial overhead from repeatedly computing attention over a growing KV cache. We identify an underexplored property of block diffusion: cross-step redundancy of attention within a block. Our analysis shows that attention outputs from tokens outside the current block remain largely stable across diffusion steps, while block-internal attention varies significantly. Based on this observation, we propose FlashBlock, a cached block-external attention mechanism that reuses stable attention output, reducing attention computation and KV cache access without modifying the diffusion process. Moreover, FlashBlock is orthogonal to sparse attention and can be combined as a complementary residual reuse strategy, substantially improving model accuracy under aggressive sparsification. Experiments on diffusion language models and video generation demonstrate up to 1.44$\times$ higher token throughput and up to 1.6$\times$ reduction in attention time, with negligible impact on generation quality. Project page: https://caesarhhh.github.io/FlashBlock/.

AI.tldr: Introduces FlashBlock, a cached cross-step attention mechanism for block diffusion that reuses attention outputs from outside the current block to boost long-context generation throughput with minimal quality loss.
AI.motivation: Long-context diffusion suffers from high attention cost due to growing KV caches; cross-step redundancy indicates cross-block attention is stable and exploitable for reuse.
AI.method: Empirically observe that cross-step attention outputs outside the current block are stable while block-internal attention changes; design a block-external attention cache (FlashBlock) that reuses these stable outputs without modifying the diffusion process; show compatibility with sparse attention and combine as a residual reuse strategy; evaluate on diffusion language models and video generation.
AI.result: Achieves up to 1.44× token throughput and up to 1.6× reduction in attention time; negligible degradation in generation quality; performs well with aggressive sparsification and is orthogonal to existing sparse attention methods.
AI.conclusion: FlashBlock provides a non-intrusive, orthogonal efficiency improvement for block-diffusion-based long-context generation, reducing attention computation and KV-cache access while maintaining quality, and can be combined with sparse attention for further gains.

title: Scalable and General Whole-Body Control for Cross-Humanoid Locomotion
summary: Learning-based whole-body controllers have become a key driver for humanoid robots, yet most existing approaches require robot-specific training. In this paper, we study the problem of cross-embodiment humanoid control and show that a single policy can robustly generalize across a wide range of humanoid robot designs with one-time training. We introduce XHugWBC, a novel cross-embodiment training framework that enables generalist humanoid control through: (1) physics-consistent morphological randomization, (2) semantically aligned observation and action spaces across diverse humanoid robots, and (3) effective policy architectures modeling morphological and dynamical properties. XHugWBC is not tied to any specific robot. Instead, it internalizes a broad distribution of morphological and dynamical characteristics during training. By learning motion priors from diverse randomized embodiments, the policy acquires a strong structural bias that supports zero-shot transfer to previously unseen robots. Experiments on twelve simulated humanoids and seven real-world robots demonstrate the strong generalization and robustness of the resulting universal controller.

AI.tldr: A single generalist policy learns cross-embodiment control for humanoids via randomized morphologies and semantically aligned spaces, enabling zero-shot transfer across twelve simulated and seven real robots.
AI.motivation: Address the need for robot-specific training and enable universal, cross-embodiment humanoid control.
AI.method: Physics-consistent morphological randomization; semantically aligned observation and action spaces across diverse humanoid robots; architectures that model morphological and dynamical properties; training on a broad distribution of embodiments to learn motion priors.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong generalization and robustness across twelve simulated humanoids and seven real-world robots, achieving a universal controller.
AI.conclusion: XHugWBC provides a generalist, robot-agnostic humanoid controller trained once, with zero-shot transfer to unseen morphologies.

title: Position: Universal Time Series Foundation Models Rest on a Category Error
summary: This position paper argues that the pursuit of "Universal Foundation Models for Time Series" rests on a fundamental category error, mistaking a structural Container for a semantic Modality. We contend that because time series hold incompatible generative processes (e.g., finance vs. fluid dynamics), monolithic models degenerate into expensive "Generic Filters" that fail to generalize under distributional drift. To address this, we introduce the "Autoregressive Blindness Bound," a theoretical limit proving that history-only models cannot predict intervention-driven regime shifts. We advocate replacing universality with a Causal Control Agent paradigm, where an agent leverages external context to orchestrate a hierarchy of specialized solvers, from frozen domain experts to lightweight Just-in-Time adaptors. We conclude by calling for a shift in benchmarks from "Zero-Shot Accuracy" to "Drift Adaptation Speed" to prioritize robust, control-theoretic systems.

AI.tldr: The abstract argues that universal foundation models for time series are conceptually flawed due to a mismatch between a structural container and a semantic modality, driven by incompatible domain generative processes and distributional drift. It introduces an Autoregressive Blindness Bound, advocates a Causal Control Agent architecture with modular solvers, and proposes drift-adaptation speed as a new benchmark.
AI.motivation: Time-series domains (e.g., finance, fluid dynamics) exhibit distinct generative processes. History-only, monolithic models struggle with distributional drift and intervention-driven regime shifts, motivating a shift toward modular, context-aware approaches.
AI.method: Proposes a theoretical bound—the Autoregressive Blindness Bound—claiming impossibility for history-only models to predict interventions. Advises a Causal Control Agent that orchestrates a hierarchy of specialized solvers (frozen domain experts to lightweight JIT adaptors) and shifts benchmarks from zero-shot accuracy to drift adaptation speed.
AI.result: Being a position/perspective paper, it provides theoretical arguments and architectural proposals without empirical validation. The bound is framed as a limitation, and the proposed architecture outlines how to realize modular, context-aware time-series systems.
AI.conclusion: Encourages abandoning universal time-series models in favor of a Causal Control Agent framework with modular solvers, and adopts drift-adaptation speed as the primary performance benchmark to prioritize robust control-theoretic behavior.

title: Depth-Wise Emergence of Prediction-Centric Geometry in Large Language Models
summary: We show that decoder-only large language models exhibit a depth-wise transition from context-processing to prediction-forming phases of computation accompanied by a reorganization of representational geometry. Using a unified framework combining geometric analysis with mechanistic intervention, we demonstrate that late-layer representations implement a structured geometric code that enables selective causal control over token prediction. Specifically, angular organization of the representation geometry parametrizes prediction distributional similarity, while representation norms encode context-specific information that does not determine prediction. Together, these results provide a mechanistic-geometric account of the dynamics of transforming context into predictions in LLMs.

AI.tldr: Decoder-only LLMs show a depth-wise shift from context processing to prediction formation, with late-layer geometry forming a structured code that enables selective causal control of token predictions; angular geometry encodes distributional similarity while norms encode context-specific but non-predictive information, yielding a mechanistic-geometric view of context-to-prediction transformation.
AI.motivation: To uncover how decoder-only LLMs transform contextual information into predictions by linking geometric structure of representations with actionable interventions, providing a unified interpretability framework.
AI.method: Geometric analysis combined with mechanistic intervention within a unified framework. Investigation focuses on late-layer representations, their angular organization and norms, and how these relate to prediction distribution and causal control.
AI.result: Identification of a depth-wise transition in computations from context processing to prediction formation. Late-layer representations exhibit a structured geometric code enabling selective causal control over predictions. Angular organization parametrizes distributional similarity, while representation norms encode context-specific information that does not determine predictions.
AI.conclusion: The work offers a mechanistic-geometric account of how context is transformed into predictions in decoder-only LLMs, with implications for interpretability and controllability of model behavior.

title: Wid3R: Wide Field-of-View 3D Reconstruction via Camera Model Conditioning
summary: We present Wid3R, a feed-forward neural network for visual geometry reconstruction that supports wide field-of-view camera models. Prior methods typically assume that input images are rectified or captured with pinhole cameras, since both their architectures and training datasets are tailored to perspective images only. These assumptions limit their applicability in real-world scenarios that use fisheye or panoramic cameras and often require careful calibration and undistortion. In contrast, Wid3R is a generalizable multi-view 3D estimation method that can model wide field-of-view camera types. Our approach leverages a ray representation with spherical harmonics and a novel camera model token within the network, enabling distortion-aware 3D reconstruction. Furthermore, Wid3R is the first multi-view foundation model to support feed-forward 3D reconstruction directly from 360 imagery. It demonstrates strong zero-shot robustness and consistently outperforms prior methods, achieving improvements of up to +77.33 on Stanford2D3D.

AI.tldr: Wid3R introduces a generalizable feed-forward network for wide-field-of-view 3D reconstruction that handles fisheye/panoramic imagery via a ray representation with spherical harmonics and a camera-model token; it's the first multi-view foundation model enabling direct 360-degree reconstruction, achieving strong zero-shot results and up to +77.33 improvement on Stanford2D3D.
AI.motivation: Prev. multi-view 3D methods assume rectified/pinhole cameras, requiring undistortion and calibration; this limits applicability to real-world fisheye/panoramic cameras. A distortion-aware, wide-FOV capable, generalizable model is needed.
AI.method: Proposes ray-based geometry representation with spherical harmonics; introduces a camera-model token integrated into a feed-forward network; supports wide FOV including 360 imagery; treats 3D estimation in a way compatible with foundation-model style generalization.
AI.result: Outperforms prior methods with substantial gains; up to +77.33 improvement on Stanford2D3D; demonstrates strong zero-shot robustness; first to support 360 imagery in a multi-view foundation model.
AI.conclusion: Wid3R broadens applicability of learned 3D reconstruction to diverse camera models and 360 imagery, offering distortion-aware, generalizable performance and enabling foundation-model style capabilities in multi-view geometry.

title: A Hybrid Autoencoder for Robust Heightmap Generation from Fused Lidar and Depth Data for Humanoid Robot Locomotion
summary: Reliable terrain perception is a critical prerequisite for the deployment of humanoid robots in unstructured, human-centric environments. While traditional systems often rely on manually engineered, single-sensor pipelines, this paper presents a learning-based framework that uses an intermediate, robot-centric heightmap representation. A hybrid Encoder-Decoder Structure (EDS) is introduced, utilizing a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for spatial feature extraction fused with a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) core for temporal consistency. The architecture integrates multimodal data from an Intel RealSense depth camera, a LIVOX MID-360 LiDAR processed via efficient spherical projection, and an onboard IMU. Quantitative results demonstrate that multimodal fusion improves reconstruction accuracy by 7.2% over depth-only and 9.9% over LiDAR-only configurations. Furthermore, the integration of a 3.2 s temporal context reduces mapping drift.

AI.tldr: Multimodal learning framework using a robot-centric heightmap with a CNN+GRU Encoder-Decoder improves terrain reconstruction and reduces drift with 3.2 s temporal context.
AI.motivation: Reliable terrain perception is essential for humanoid robots in unstructured, human-centric environments. Traditional single-sensor pipelines are limited; a learning-based, multi-sensor fusion approach can enhance robustness and accuracy.
AI.method: A hybrid Encoder-Decoder Structure (EDS) with a CNN for spatial feature extraction and a GRU core for temporal consistency, operating on a robot-centric heightmap. Multimodal data are fused from an Intel RealSense depth camera, LIVOX MID-360 LiDAR (processed via efficient spherical projection), and an onboard IMU.
AI.result: Multimodal fusion yields 7.2% improvement in reconstruction accuracy over depth-only and 9.9% over LiDAR-only configurations. Incorporating 3.2 s of temporal context reduces mapping drift.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multimodal, temporally- aware heightmap framework enhances terrain perception fidelity and stability, supporting more robust humanoid robot operation in unstructured environments.

title: Aspect-Aware MOOC Recommendation in a Heterogeneous Network
summary: MOOC recommendation systems have received increasing attention to help learners navigate and select preferred learning content. Traditional methods such as collaborative filtering and content-based filtering suffer from data sparsity and over-specialization. To alleviate these limitations, graph-based approaches have been proposed; however, they still rely heavily on manually predefined metapaths, which often capture only superficial structural relationships and impose substantial burdens on domain experts as well as significant engineering costs. To overcome these limitations, we propose AMR (Aspect-aware MOOC Recommendation), a novel framework that models path-specific multiple aspects by embedding the semantic content of nodes within each metapath. AMR automatically discovers metapaths through bi-directional walks, derives aspect-aware path representations using a bi-LSTM-based encoder, and incorporates these representations as edge features in the learner-learner and KC-KC subgraphs to achieve fine-grained semantically informed KC recommendations. Extensive experiments on the large-scale MOOCCube and PEEK datasets show that AMR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art graph neural network baselines across key metrics such as HR@K and nDCG@K. Further analysis confirms that AMR effectively captures rich path-specific aspect information, allowing more accurate recommendations than those methods that rely solely on predefined metapaths. The code will be available upon accepted.

AI.tldr: AMR automates path-specific, aspect-aware MOOC recommendations by automatically discovering metapaths, encoding semantic node content with a bi-LSTM, and using these as edge features in GNNs, yielding state-of-the-art results on large MOOC datasets.
AI.motivation: Address data sparsity and over-specialization of traditional CF/content-based methods and the heavy reliance on manually predefined metapaths in graph-based approaches, by enabling fine-grained, semantically informed recommendations without extensive manual engineering.
AI.method: Automatically discover metapaths via bi-directional walks; build aspect-aware path representations using a bi-LSTM encoder on the semantic content of nodes along each path; integrate these representations as edge features in learner-learner and KC-KC subgraphs for KC-based recommendations.
AI.result: AMR outperforms state-of-the-art graph neural network baselines on large-scale MOOCCube and PEEK datasets across HR@K and nDCG@K; analysis shows that it captures rich path-specific aspect information beyond predefined metapaths.
AI.conclusion: AMR demonstrates that automatic, aspect-aware metapath modeling yields more accurate MOOC recommendations and reduces reliance on manually crafted metapaths; code availability is promised upon acceptance.

title: Comparing Euclidean and Hyperbolic K-Means for Generalized Category Discovery
comment: 11 pages, 4 figures. To be published in the VISAPP
summary: Hyperbolic representation learning has been widely used to extract implicit hierarchies within data, and recently it has found its way to the open-world classification task of Generalized Category Discovery (GCD). However, prior hyperbolic GCD methods only use hyperbolic geometry for representation learning and transform back to Euclidean geometry when clustering. We hypothesize this is suboptimal. Therefore, we present Hyperbolic Clustered GCD (HC-GCD), which learns embeddings in the Lorentz Hyperboloid model of hyperbolic geometry, and clusters these embeddings directly in hyperbolic space using a hyperbolic K-Means algorithm. We test our model on the Semantic Shift Benchmark datasets, and demonstrate that HC-GCD is on par with the previous state-of-the-art hyperbolic GCD method. Furthermore, we show that using hyperbolic K-Means leads to better accuracy than Euclidean K-Means. We carry out ablation studies showing that clipping the norm of the Euclidean embeddings leads to decreased accuracy in clustering unseen classes, and increased accuracy for seen classes, while the overall accuracy is dataset dependent. We also show that using hyperbolic K-Means leads to more consistent clusters when varying the label granularity.

AI.tldr: Introduces HC-GCD which learns embeddings in the Lorentz Hyperboloid and clusters in hyperbolic space with hyperbolic K-Means; matches state-of-the-art hyperbolic GCD and outperforms Euclidean clustering; shows dataset-dependent effects of norm clipping and higher cluster consistency with hyperbolic clustering.
AI.motivation: Tackle suboptimality of prior hyperbolic GCD methods that convert to Euclidean space for clustering; exploit hyperbolic geometry throughout to improve open-world category discovery.
AI.method: Employ Lorentz model for hyperbolic embeddings; use hyperbolic K-Means for clustering directly in hyperbolic space; perform ablations (norm clipping) and evaluate on Semantic Shift Benchmark datasets; compare to Euclidean clustering.
AI.result: HC-GCD on par with state-of-the-art hyperbolic GCD; hyperbolic K-Means yields better clustering than Euclidean K-Means; ablation shows norm clipping decreases accuracy for unseen classes but increases for seen classes; clustering consistency improves with hyperbolic clustering; results dataset-dependent.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end hyperbolic representation and clustering yields improved stability and accuracy for GCD, supporting the advantage of hyperbolic clustering.

title: MTPano: Multi-Task Panoramic Scene Understanding via Label-Free Integration of Dense Prediction Priors
summary: Comprehensive panoramic scene understanding is critical for immersive applications, yet it remains challenging due to the scarcity of high-resolution, multi-task annotations. While perspective foundation models have achieved success through data scaling, directly adapting them to the panoramic domain often fails due to severe geometric distortions and coordinate system discrepancies. Furthermore, the underlying relations between diverse dense prediction tasks in spherical spaces are underexplored. To address these challenges, we propose MTPano, a robust multi-task panoramic foundation model established by a label-free training pipeline. First, to circumvent data scarcity, we leverage powerful perspective dense priors. We project panoramic images into perspective patches to generate accurate, domain-gap-free pseudo-labels using off-the-shelf foundation models, which are then re-projected to serve as patch-wise supervision. Second, to tackle the interference between task types, we categorize tasks into rotation-invariant (e.g., depth, segmentation) and rotation-variant (e.g., surface normals) groups. We introduce the Panoramic Dual BridgeNet, which disentangles these feature streams via geometry-aware modulation layers that inject absolute position and ray direction priors. To handle the distortion from equirectangular projections (ERP), we incorporate ERP token mixers followed by a dual-branch BridgeNet for interactions with gradient truncation, facilitating beneficial cross-task information sharing while blocking conflicting gradients from incompatible task attributes. Additionally, we introduce auxiliary tasks (image gradient, point map, etc.) to fertilize the cross-task learning process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MTPano achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks and delivers competitive results against task-specific panoramic specialist foundation models.

AI.tldr: MTPano is a label-free, multi-task panoramic foundation model that uses perspective priors and geometry-aware dual-branch fusion to handle ERP distortions and cross-task relations, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of high-resolution, multi-task panoramic annotations and severe geometric distortions when transferring perspective foundation models to panoramic data; underexplored cross-task relationships in spherical spaces.
AI.method: 1) Generate pseudo-labels by projecting panoramas into perspective patches and using off-the-shelf foundation models, then reproject to provide patch-wise supervision. 2) Categorize tasks into rotation-invariant and rotation-variant groups; Panoramic Dual BridgeNet with geometry-aware modulation layers injecting absolute position and ray direction priors. 3) Use ERP token mixers and a dual-branch BridgeNet with gradient truncation to enable cross-task information sharing while blocking conflicting gradients. 4) Add auxiliary tasks (image gradients, point maps, etc.) to enrich cross-task learning.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks and is competitive with task-specific panoramic specialist foundation models.
AI.conclusion: MTPano demonstrates the effectiveness of label-free cross-task learning in panoramic scenes, leveraging perspective priors and geometry-aware fusion to mitigate ERP distortions and enhance cross-task information flow.

title: Residual Reinforcement Learning for Waste-Container Lifting Using Large-Scale Cranes with Underactuated Tools
summary: This paper studies the container lifting phase of a waste-container recycling task in urban environments, performed by a hydraulic loader crane equipped with an underactuated discharge unit, and proposes a residual reinforcement learning (RRL) approach that combines a nominal Cartesian controller with a learned residual policy. All experiments are conducted in simulation, where the task is characterized by tight geometric tolerances between the discharge-unit hooks and the container rings relative to the overall crane scale, making precise trajectory tracking and swing suppression essential. The nominal controller uses admittance control for trajectory tracking and pendulum-aware swing damping, followed by damped least-squares inverse kinematics with a nullspace posture term to generate joint velocity commands. A PPO-trained residual policy in Isaac Lab compensates for unmodeled dynamics and parameter variations, improving precision and robustness without requiring end-to-end learning from scratch. We further employ randomized episode initialization and domain randomization over payload properties, actuator gains, and passive joint parameters to enhance generalization. Simulation results demonstrate improved tracking accuracy, reduced oscillations, and higher lifting success rates compared to the nominal controller alone.

AI.tldr: Residual reinforcement learning augments a nominal Cartesian admittance controller with a PPO policy to improve precision and robustness in simulating a waste-container lifting task using an underactuated discharge unit on a hydraulic loader crane; results show better tracking, reduced swing, and higher lifting success than the nominal controller alone.
AI.motivation: Enable precise, robust container lifting in urban environments with tight geometric tolerances, where unmodeled dynamics and parameter variations degrade model-based controllers. The approach combines a model-based controller with a learned residual policy to handle these discrepancies without end-to-end learning.
AI.method: A nominal Cartesian controller performs trajectory tracking via admittance control and pendulum-aware swing damping, followed by damped least-squares IK with a nullspace posture term for joint commands. A PPO-trained residual policy (in Isaac Lab) compensates for unmodeled dynamics and parameter variations. To promote generalization, the method employs randomized episode initialization and domain randomization over payload properties, actuator gains, and passive joint parameters.
AI.result: Simulation experiments show improved tracking accuracy, reduced oscillations, and higher lifting success rates when using the residual policy, compared to the nominal controller alone; domain randomization aids generalization.
AI.conclusion: Residual RL can effectively augment model-based robotics controllers for underactuated lifting tasks by learning residual corrections that address unmodeled dynamics, with demonstrated gains in simulation and potential for real-world transfer under domain randomization.

title: PieArena: Frontier Language Agents Achieve MBA-Level Negotiation Performance and Reveal Novel Behavioral Differences
summary: We present an in-depth evaluation of LLMs' ability to negotiate, a central business task that requires strategic reasoning, theory of mind, and economic value creation. To do so, we introduce PieArena, a large-scale negotiation benchmark grounded in multi-agent interactions over realistic scenarios drawn from an MBA negotiation course at an elite business school. We find systematic evidence of AGI-level performance in which a representative frontier agent (GPT-5) matches or outperforms trained business-school students, despite a semester of general negotiation instruction and targeted coaching immediately prior to the task. We further study the effects of joint-intentionality agentic scaffolding and find asymmetric gains, with large improvements for mid- and lower-tier LMs and diminishing returns for frontier LMs. Beyond deal outcomes, PieArena provides a multi-dimensional negotiation behavioral profile, revealing novel cross-model heterogeneity, masked by deal-outcome-only benchmarks, in deception, computation accuracy, instruction compliance, and perceived reputation. Overall, our results suggest that frontier language agents are already intellectually and psychologically capable of deployment in high-stakes economic settings, but deficiencies in robustness and trustworthiness remain open challenges.

AI.tldr: Frontier LLMs (e.g., GPT-5) match or surpass trained MBAs in a large-scale negotiation benchmark (PieArena); joint-intentionality scaffolding boosts mid/low-tier models; reveals cross-model behavioral heterogeneity; robustness and trustworthiness remain as challenges for deployment in high-stakes economics.
AI.motivation: Assess whether modern LLMs can perform strategic, multi-agent negotiation tasks at or near human expert levels, and understand behavioral dimensions beyond outcomes, to inform potential deployment in business contexts.
AI.method: Introduce PieArena, a large-scale, multi-agent negotiation benchmark derived from MBA-course scenarios. evaluate a frontier agent (GPT-5) against trained business-school students. Analyze effects of joint-intentionality agentic scaffolding on model performance. Collect multi-dimensional behavioral data (deception, computation accuracy, instruction compliance, perceived reputation) in addition to deal outcomes.
AI.result: Evidence of AGI-level performance: frontier agent GPT-5 matches or exceeds trained MBAs. Joint-intentionality scaffolding yields large gains for mid/low-tier LMs but diminishing returns for frontier LMs. Cross-model heterogeneity emerges beyond deal outcomes, with variation in deception, computation accuracy, instruction compliance, and perceived reputation. Overall, frontier agents show capability for high-stakes economic settings but notable robustness and trustworthiness issues remain.
AI.conclusion: Frontier language agents are potentially deployable in high-stakes economic settings, but significant work is needed to address robustness, reliability, and trust concerns.

title: Linear Model Merging Unlocks Simple and Scalable Multimodal Data Mixture Optimization
summary: Selecting the best data mixture is critical for successful Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) of Multimodal Large Language Models. However, determining the optimal mixture weights across multiple domain-specific datasets remains a significant bottleneck due to the combinatorial search space and the high cost associated with even a single training run. This is the so-called Data Mixture Optimization (DMO) problem. On the other hand, model merging unifies domain-specific experts through parameter interpolation. This strategy is efficient, as it only requires a single training run per domain, yet oftentimes leads to suboptimal models. In this work, we take the best of both worlds, studying model merging as an efficient strategy for estimating the performance of different data mixtures. We train domain-specific multimodal experts and evaluate their weighted parameter-space combinations to estimate the efficacy of corresponding data mixtures. We conduct extensive experiments on 14 multimodal benchmarks, and empirically demonstrate that the merged proxy models exhibit a high rank correlation with models trained on actual data mixtures. This decouples the search for optimal mixtures from the resource-intensive training process, thereby providing a scalable and efficient strategy for navigating the complex landscape of mixture weights. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/BerasiDavide/mLLMs_merging_4_DMO.

AI.tldr: Using model merging as a cheap proxy to estimate data-mixture performance for multimodal LLMs, enabling efficient Data Mixture Optimization (DMO) by predicting effective mixture weights from merged domain-specific experts; high correlation with true mixtures across 14 benchmarks; code released.
AI.motivation: DMO is a combinatorial and costly problem, requiring many training runs to identify optimal domain-weight mixtures. A rapid proxy for performance would dramatically reduce compute.
AI.method: Train domain-specific multimodal experts; form weighted parameter-space interpolations (merged models); evaluate their performance as proxies for the corresponding data mixtures; assess the rank correlation between proxy scores and actual mixture performance across 14 benchmarks.
AI.result: Merged proxy models exhibit high rank correlation with models trained on actual data mixtures, enabling reliable estimation of optimal mixtures with substantially less training.
AI.conclusion: Model merging provides an efficient, scalable proxy for DMO in SFT of Multimodal LLMs, decoupling the search for optimal mixtures from expensive training; code is publicly available.

title: Consistency-Preserving Concept Erasure via Unsafe-Safe Pairing and Directional Fisher-weighted Adaptation
summary: With the increasing versatility of text-to-image diffusion models, the ability to selectively erase undesirable concepts (e.g., harmful content) has become indispensable. However, existing concept erasure approaches primarily focus on removing unsafe concepts without providing guidance toward corresponding safe alternatives, which often leads to failure in preserving the structural and semantic consistency between the original and erased generations. In this paper, we propose a novel framework, PAIRed Erasing (PAIR), which reframes concept erasure from simple removal to consistency-preserving semantic realignment using unsafe-safe pairs. We first generate safe counterparts from unsafe inputs while preserving structural and semantic fidelity, forming paired unsafe-safe multimodal data. Leveraging these pairs, we introduce two key components: (1) Paired Semantic Realignment, a guided objective that uses unsafe-safe pairs to explicitly map target concepts to semantically aligned safe anchors; and (2) Fisher-weighted Initialization for DoRA, which initializes parameter-efficient low-rank adaptation matrices using unsafe-safe pairs, encouraging the generation of safe alternatives while selectively suppressing unsafe concepts. Together, these components enable fine-grained erasure that removes only the targeted concepts while maintaining overall semantic consistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving effective concept erasure while preserving structural integrity, semantic coherence, and generation quality.

AI.tldr: PAIRed Erasing (PAIR) reframes concept erasure as consistency-preserving semantic realignment using unsafe–safe pairs, enabling targeted removal of unsafe concepts while preserving structure, semantics, and image quality.
AI.motivation: Existing erasure methods remove unsafe concepts but often disrupt structural/semantic fidelity, leading to incoherent or low-quality generations. There is a need for erasure that guides safe alternatives without degrading overall coherence.
AI.method: Generate safe counterparts from unsafe inputs to create paired unsafe–safe multimodal data. Introduce two components: (1) Paired Semantic Realignment, a guided objective that maps target unsafe concepts to semantically aligned safe anchors using unsafe–safe pairs; (2) Fisher-weighted Initialization for DoRA, initializing parameter-efficient low-rank adapters (LoRA) with unsafe–safe pairs to promote safe alternatives while suppressing unsafe concepts.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the approach outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in effective concept erasure while preserving structural integrity, semantic coherence, and generation quality.
AI.conclusion: PAIR enables fine-grained erasure that removes targeted concepts while maintaining overall semantic structure, demonstrating effective consistency-preserving semantic realignment for text-to-image diffusion models.

title: ProAct: Agentic Lookahead in Interactive Environments
summary: Existing Large Language Model (LLM) agents struggle in interactive environments requiring long-horizon planning, primarily due to compounding errors when simulating future states. To address this, we propose ProAct, a framework that enables agents to internalize accurate lookahead reasoning through a two-stage training paradigm. First, we introduce Grounded LookAhead Distillation (GLAD), where the agent undergoes supervised fine-tuning on trajectories derived from environment-based search. By compressing complex search trees into concise, causal reasoning chains, the agent learns the logic of foresight without the computational overhead of inference-time search. Second, to further refine decision accuracy, we propose the Monte-Carlo Critic (MC-Critic), a plug-and-play auxiliary value estimator designed to enhance policy-gradient algorithms like PPO and GRPO. By leveraging lightweight environment rollouts to calibrate value estimates, MC-Critic provides a low-variance signal that facilitates stable policy optimization without relying on expensive model-based value approximation. Experiments on both stochastic (e.g., 2048) and deterministic (e.g., Sokoban) environments demonstrate that ProAct significantly improves planning accuracy. Notably, a 4B parameter model trained with ProAct outperforms all open-source baselines and rivals state-of-the-art closed-source models, while demonstrating robust generalization to unseen environments. The codes and models are available at https://github.com/GreatX3/ProAct

AI.tldr: ProAct enables LLM agents to plan with accurate lookahead via GLAD and MC-Critic; improves long-horizon planning; 4B model outperforms baselines; code released.
AI.motivation: Address compounding errors during future-state simulation in long-horizon planning for interactive environments.
AI.method: Grounded LookAhead Distillation (GLAD): supervised fine-tuning on trajectories from environment-based search, compressing search trees into concise causal reasoning chains. Monte-Carlo Critic (MC-Critic): plug-and-play auxiliary value estimator to improve policy-gradient methods (PPO, GRPO) using lightweight environment rollouts to calibrate value estimates.
AI.result: Better planning accuracy across stochastic (e.g., 2048) and deterministic (Sokoban) environments; a 4B parameter ProAct model outperforms open-source baselines and rivals state-of-the-art closed-source models, with strong generalization to unseen environments.
AI.conclusion: ProAct demonstrates effective lookahead reasoning and stable policy optimization for robust LLM-based planning; practical, scalable approach with publicly available code/models.

title: Transolver-3: Scaling Up Transformer Solvers to Industrial-Scale Geometries
summary: Deep learning has emerged as a transformative tool for the neural surrogate modeling of partial differential equations (PDEs), known as neural PDE solvers. However, scaling these solvers to industrial-scale geometries with over $10^8$ cells remains a fundamental challenge due to the prohibitive memory complexity of processing high-resolution meshes. We present Transolver-3, a new member of the Transolver family as a highly scalable framework designed for high-fidelity physics simulations. To bridge the gap between limited GPU capacity and the resolution requirements of complex engineering tasks, we introduce two key architectural optimizations: faster slice and deslice by exploiting matrix multiplication associative property and geometry slice tiling to partition the computation of physical states. Combined with an amortized training strategy by learning on random subsets of original high-resolution meshes and a physical state caching technique during inference, Transolver-3 enables high-fidelity field prediction on industrial-scale meshes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Transolver-3 is capable of handling meshes with over 160 million cells, achieving impressive performance across three challenging simulation benchmarks, including aircraft and automotive design tasks.

AI.tldr: Transolver-3 is a scalable neural PDE solver framework that handles meshes >160M cells by combining two optimization tricks (slice/deslice leveraging matrix-multiply associativity and geometry slice tiling), randomized subset training, and state caching, achieving high-fidelity predictions on industrial-scale simulations (aircraft, automotive).
AI.motivation: Neural PDE solvers suffer from prohibitive memory complexity when scaling to industrial-scale, high-resolution meshes (>10^8 cells), limiting applicability to real-world engineering tasks.
AI.method: Introduce two architectural optimizations: (1) faster slice and deslice by exploiting the associative property of matrix multiplication; (2) geometry slice tiling to partition computation of physical states. Also adopt an amortized training strategy by learning on random subsets of the original high-resolution meshes and a physical state caching technique during inference. Transolver-3 is presented as a scalable framework to enable high-fidelity field prediction on industrial-scale meshes.
AI.result: Demonstrates capability to handle meshes with over 160 million cells and achieves impressive performance across three challenging simulation benchmarks, including aircraft and automotive design tasks.
AI.conclusion: Transolver-3 mitigates memory and compute bottlenecks, enabling scalable, high-fidelity physics simulations on industrial-scale meshes. The combination of architectural optimizations, subset training, and state caching supports practical deployment in heavy-engineering design pipelines.

title: Learning with Adaptive Prototype Manifolds for Out-of-Distribution Detection
summary: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is a critical task for the safe deployment of machine learning models in the real world. Existing prototype-based representation learning methods have demonstrated exceptional performance. Specifically, we identify two fundamental flaws that universally constrain these methods: the Static Homogeneity Assumption (fixed representational resources for all classes) and the Learning-Inference Disconnect (discarding rich prototype quality knowledge at inference). These flaws fundamentally limit the model's capacity and performance. To address these issues, we propose APEX (Adaptive Prototype for eXtensive OOD Detection), a novel OOD detection framework designed via a Two-Stage Repair process to optimize the learned feature manifold. APEX introduces two key innovations to address these respective flaws: (1) an Adaptive Prototype Manifold (APM), which leverages the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle to automatically determine the optimal prototype complexity $K_c^*$ for each class, thereby fundamentally resolving prototype collision; and (2) a Posterior-Aware OOD Scoring (PAOS) mechanism, which quantifies prototype quality (cohesion and separation) to bridge the learning-inference disconnect. Comprehensive experiments on benchmarks such as CIFAR-100 validate the superiority of our method, where APEX achieves new state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: APEX: adaptive prototype per class via MDL to fix prototype collision, plus PAOS to align training-time prototypes with inference-time scoring; achieves state-of-the-art OOD detection on CIFAR-100.
AI.motivation: OOD detection is essential for safe ML deployment. Prototype-based methods suffer from two universal flaws: Static Homogeneity (fixed resource allocation per class) and Learning-Inference Disconnect (prototype quality during training is not leveraged at inference).
AI.method: Two-stage repair: (1) Adaptive Prototype Manifold (APM) uses Minimum Description Length (MDL) to automatically select the optimal per-class prototype count K_c^*, addressing prototype collision; (2) Posterior-Aware OOD Scoring (PAOS) measures prototype quality (cohesion and separation) to bridge learning and inference.
AI.result: Empirical validation on benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-100) shows state-of-the-art OOD detection performance, confirming the effectiveness of adaptive prototype complexity and posterior-aware scoring.
AI.conclusion: APEX effectively resolves the two foundational flaws in prototype-based representation learning, delivering improved OOD detection by adapting prototype complexity per class and explicitly evaluating prototype quality at inference.

title: Visuo-Tactile World Models
summary: We introduce multi-task Visuo-Tactile World Models (VT-WM), which capture the physics of contact through touch reasoning. By complementing vision with tactile sensing, VT-WM better understands robot-object interactions in contact-rich tasks, avoiding common failure modes of vision-only models under occlusion or ambiguous contact states, such as objects disappearing, teleporting, or moving in ways that violate basic physics. Trained across a set of contact-rich manipulation tasks, VT-WM improves physical fidelity in imagination, achieving 33% better performance at maintaining object permanence and 29% better compliance with the laws of motion in autoregressive rollouts. Moreover, experiments show that grounding in contact dynamics also translates to planning. In zero-shot real-robot experiments, VT-WM achieves up to 35% higher success rates, with the largest gains in multi-step, contact-rich tasks. Finally, VT-WM demonstrates significant downstream versatility, effectively adapting its learned contact dynamics to a novel task and achieving reliable planning success with only a limited set of demonstrations.

AI.tldr: VT-WM integrates visuo-tactile world modeling to improve contact-rich manipulation, achieving strong gains in object permanence, physics compliance, planning, and zero-shot real-robot transfer, with good adaptation to new tasks.
AI.motivation: Vision-only models struggle under occlusion and contact ambiguity; a model that reasons about contact via tactile sensing can improve physical fidelity and planning in manipulation tasks.
AI.method: Train multi-task visuo-tactile world models that fuse vision and tactile data to capture contact dynamics, evaluate through autoregressive rollouts in simulation and zero-shot real-robot experiments across several contact-rich tasks, and test transfer to novel tasks with limited demonstrations.
AI.result: Ventures show 33% improvement in maintaining object permanence and 29% better compliance with the laws of motion in autoregressive rollouts; zero-shot real-robot success rates improve up to 35%, especially in multi-step tasks; the model also generalizes to novel tasks with few demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: Grounding physics of contact via tactile sensing improves imagination fidelity and planning, enabling robust real-world manipulation and versatile downstream performance with limited data.

title: AgentXRay: White-Boxing Agentic Systems via Workflow Reconstruction
summary: Large Language Models have shown strong capabilities in complex problem solving, yet many agentic systems remain difficult to interpret and control due to opaque internal workflows. While some frameworks offer explicit architectures for collaboration, many deployed agentic systems operate as black boxes to users. We address this by introducing Agentic Workflow Reconstruction (AWR), a new task aiming to synthesize an explicit, interpretable stand-in workflow that approximates a black-box system using only input--output access. We propose AgentXRay, a search-based framework that formulates AWR as a combinatorial optimization problem over discrete agent roles and tool invocations in a chain-structured workflow space. Unlike model distillation, AgentXRay produces editable white-box workflows that match target outputs under an observable, output-based proxy metric, without accessing model parameters. To navigate the vast search space, AgentXRay employs Monte Carlo Tree Search enhanced by a scoring-based Red-Black Pruning mechanism, which dynamically integrates proxy quality with search depth. Experiments across diverse domains demonstrate that AgentXRay achieves higher proxy similarity and reduces token consumption compared to unpruned search, enabling deeper workflow exploration under fixed iteration budgets.

AI.tldr: AgentXRay reconstructs an editable white-box workflow that mirrors a black-box agentic system using only input-output access, via a search-based framework with MCTS and Red-Black pruning.
AI.motivation: Opaque agentic systems hinder interpretability, control, and safety; there is a need for explicit, human-interpretable stand-ins that don’t require access to model parameters.
AI.method: Formulates Agentic Workflow Reconstruction (AWR) as a combinatorial optimization over discrete agent roles and tool invocations in a chain-structured workflow. AgentXRay uses Monte Carlo Tree Search enhanced with a Red-Black pruning mechanism to balance proxy quality and search depth, optimizing a proxy metric based solely on outputs.
AI.result: AgentXRay achieves higher proxy similarity and reduces token consumption compared to unpruned search, enabling deeper workflow exploration under fixed iteration budgets across diverse domains.
AI.conclusion: AWR via AgentXRay yields editable, interpretable white-box workflows that approximate black-box systems, improving interpretability and controllability without requiring access to model parameters.

title: Multimodal Latent Reasoning via Hierarchical Visual Cues Injection
summary: The advancement of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) has enabled impressive perception capabilities. However, their reasoning process often remains a "fast thinking" paradigm, reliant on end-to-end generation or explicit, language-centric chains of thought (CoT), which can be inefficient, verbose, and prone to hallucination. This work posits that robust reasoning should evolve within a latent space, integrating multimodal signals seamlessly. We propose multimodal latent reasoning via HIerarchical Visual cuEs injection (\emph{HIVE}), a novel framework that instills deliberate, "slow thinking" without depending on superficial textual rationales. Our method recursively extends transformer blocks, creating an internal loop for iterative reasoning refinement. Crucially, it injectively grounds this process with hierarchical visual cues from global scene context to fine-grained regional details directly into the model's latent representations. This enables the model to perform grounded, multi-step inference entirely in the aligned latent space. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that test-time scaling is effective when incorporating vision knowledge, and that integrating hierarchical information significantly enhances the model's understanding of complex scenes.

AI.tldr: HIVE introduces latent-space, hierarchical visual cue injection for slow, grounded reasoning in multimodal LLMs, achieving iterative latent reasoning via extended transformer blocks and improved scene understanding with scalable test-time vision integration.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal LLM reasoning relies on fast end-to-end generation or language-centric chain-of-thought, which can be inefficient and hallucination-prone. A latent-space, multimodal reasoning framework could provide grounded, efficient multi-step inference with fewer textual dependencies.
AI.method: Proposes HIVE: a framework that recursively extends transformer blocks to form an internal loop for iterative latent reasoning. It injects hierarchical visual cues—from global scene context down to regional details—directly into latent representations, enabling grounded, multi-step inference entirely in latent space.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show that incorporating vision knowledge at test time scales well, and that leveraging hierarchical information improves understanding of complex scenes.
AI.conclusion: Latent-space, hierarchically grounded visual cues enable robust, grounded slow-thinking reasoning in multimodal LLMs, reducing reliance on surface-level textual rationales while improving scene comprehension.

title: CommCP: Efficient Multi-Agent Coordination via LLM-Based Communication with Conformal Prediction
comment: IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA 2026); Project Website: https://comm-cp.github.io/
summary: To complete assignments provided by humans in natural language, robots must interpret commands, generate and answer relevant questions for scene understanding, and manipulate target objects. Real-world deployments often require multiple heterogeneous robots with different manipulation capabilities to handle different assignments cooperatively. Beyond the need for specialized manipulation skills, effective information gathering is important in completing these assignments. To address this component of the problem, we formalize the information-gathering process in a fully cooperative setting as an underexplored multi-agent multi-task Embodied Question Answering (MM-EQA) problem, which is a novel extension of canonical Embodied Question Answering (EQA), where effective communication is crucial for coordinating efforts without redundancy. To address this problem, we propose CommCP, a novel LLM-based decentralized communication framework designed for MM-EQA. Our framework employs conformal prediction to calibrate the generated messages, thereby minimizing receiver distractions and enhancing communication reliability. To evaluate our framework, we introduce an MM-EQA benchmark featuring diverse, photo-realistic household scenarios with embodied questions. Experimental results demonstrate that CommCP significantly enhances the task success rate and exploration efficiency over baselines. The experiment videos, code, and dataset are available on our project website: https://comm-cp.github.io.

AI.tldr: CommCP introduces an LLM-based decentralized communication framework for multi-agent multi-task Embodied Question Answering (MM-EQA). It uses conformal prediction to calibrate messages, reducing distractions and improving reliability. A new MM-EQA benchmark with photo-realistic household scenes is proposed, and experiments show significant improvements in task success rate and exploration efficiency over baselines, with code and data released on the project site.
AI.motivation: Robots with diverse manipulation capabilities must cooperate to complete natural-language assignments, where effective information gathering and communication are critical. Existing Embodied QA (EQA) frameworks are often single-task or non-cooperative; MM-EQA and decentralized communication address the need for efficient, non-redundant information exchange in a multi-agent, multi-task setting.
AI.method: Propose CommCP, a decentralized, LLM-based communication framework for MM-EQA. The method incorporates conformal prediction to calibrate generated messages, aiming to minimize receiver distractions and enhance reliability. Evaluation is conducted on a new MM-EQA benchmark consisting of diverse, photo-realistic household scenarios with embodied questions, comparing the approach against baselines.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate that CommCP significantly improves task success rate and exploration efficiency compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: CommCP advances MM-EQA by enabling reliable, calibrated inter-agent communication and more efficient information gathering in cooperative, heterogeneous-robot settings. The introduced benchmark provides a platform for evaluation; future work could explore scaling, robustness, and real-world deployments.

title: PATHWAYS: Evaluating Investigation and Context Discovery in AI Web Agents
summary: We introduce PATHWAYS, a benchmark of 250 multi-step decision tasks that test whether web-based agents can discover and correctly use hidden contextual information. Across both closed and open models, agents typically navigate to relevant pages but retrieve decisive hidden evidence in only a small fraction of cases. When tasks require overturning misleading surface-level signals, performance drops sharply to near chance accuracy. Agents frequently hallucinate investigative reasoning by claiming to rely on evidence they never accessed. Even when correct context is discovered, agents often fail to integrate it into their final decision. Providing more explicit instructions improves context discovery but often reduces overall accuracy, revealing a tradeoff between procedural compliance and effective judgement. Together, these results show that current web agent architectures lack reliable mechanisms for adaptive investigation, evidence integration, and judgement override.

AI.tldr: PATHWAYS benchmark reveals that current web agents struggle with hidden contextual information, evidence integration, and judgement override; surface-level success is common, but decisive hidden evidence is rarely retrieved; explicit instructions boost context discovery at cost to accuracy.
AI.motivation: Assess how multi-step web tasks expose gaps in discovery, evidence use, and override ability; understand tradeoffs between instruction-following and judgement.
AI.method: Introduce PATHWAYS with 250 tasks; evaluate closed and open models; measure context discovery rate, ability to retrieve hidden evidence, evidence integration, and judgement override; manipulate instructions to observe effects.
AI.result: Agents often find relevant pages but rarely access decisive hidden evidence; overturning misleading signals lowers accuracy to near chance; agents hallucinate investigative reasoning; even with context discovered, integration into final decision is poor; more explicit instructions improve discovery but reduce accuracy; indicates a tradeoff.
AI.conclusion: Current web agent architectures lack reliable adaptive investigation, evidence integration, and judgement override; PATHWAYS highlights need for improved inference, evidence access, and decision-quality mechanisms.

title: Privileged Information Distillation for Language Models
comment: Abstract border should have been purple
summary: Training-time privileged information (PI) can enable language models to succeed on tasks they would otherwise fail, making it a powerful tool for reinforcement learning in hard, long-horizon settings. However, transferring capabilities learned with PI to policies that must act without it at inference time remains a fundamental challenge. We study this problem in the context of distilling frontier models for multi-turn agentic environments, where closed-source systems typically hide their internal reasoning and expose only action trajectories. This breaks standard distillation pipelines, since successful behavior is observable but the reasoning process is not. For this, we introduce π-Distill, a joint teacher-student objective that trains a PI-conditioned teacher and an unconditioned student simultaneously using the same model. Additionally, we also introduce On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD), an alternative approach that trains using Reinforcement Learning (RL) with a reverse KL-penalty between the student and the PI-conditioned teacher. We show that both of these algorithms effectively distill frontier agents using action-only PI. Specifically we find that π-Distill and in some cases OPSD, outperform industry standard practices (Supervised finetuning followed by RL) that assume access to full Chain-of-Thought supervision across multiple agentic benchmarks, models, and forms of PI. We complement our results with extensive analysis that characterizes the factors enabling effective learning with PI, focusing primarily on π-Distill and characterizing when OPSD is competitive.

AI.tldr: Proposes π-Distill and OPSD to distill frontier models using training-time privileged information (PI) when only action trajectories are observable, outperforming standard supervised finetuning + RL baselines.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between PI-enabled training and inference-time policies that must operate without PI; standard distillation fails when chain-of-thought (CoT) is private, so robust methods are needed for action-only PI in frontier models.
AI.method: π-Distill trains a PI-conditioned teacher and an unconditioned student within the same model, optimizing both jointly using the same data and action trajectories; the PI-conditioned teacher leverages PI during training while the student remains PI-free at inference. On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) uses Reinforcement Learning with a reverse KL-penalty constraining the student to resemble the PI-conditioned teacher.
AI.result: Both methods effectively distill frontier agents using action-only PI; π-Distill (and in some cases OPSD) outperform industry-standard supervised finetuning followed by RL that assumes access to full Chain-of-Thought supervision. Results replicated across multiple agentic benchmarks, models, and PI forms, with extensive analyses.
AI.conclusion: PI-based distillation is a viable path to transfer PI-enabled capabilities to inference-time policies lacking PI; π-Distill provides a robust joint teacher-student objective, while OPSD offers a competitive alternative under RL with a KL penalty; the work identifies key factors that enable learning with PI and guidance on when OPSD is most effective.

title: Breaking Semantic Hegemony: Decoupling Principal and Residual Subspaces for Generalized OOD Detection
summary: While feature-based post-hoc methods have made significant strides in Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection, we uncover a counter-intuitive Simplicity Paradox in existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) models: these models exhibit keen sensitivity in distinguishing semantically subtle OOD samples but suffer from severe Geometric Blindness when confronting structurally distinct yet semantically simple samples or high-frequency sensor noise. We attribute this phenomenon to Semantic Hegemony within the deep feature space and reveal its mathematical essence through the lens of Neural Collapse. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the spectral concentration bias, induced by the high variance of the principal subspace, numerically masks the structural distribution shift signals that should be significant in the residual subspace. To address this issue, we propose D-KNN, a training-free, plug-and-play geometric decoupling framework. This method utilizes orthogonal decomposition to explicitly separate semantic components from structural residuals and introduces a dual-space calibration mechanism to reactivate the model's sensitivity to weak residual signals. Extensive experiments demonstrate that D-KNN effectively breaks Semantic Hegemony, establishing new SOTA performance on both CIFAR and ImageNet benchmarks. Notably, in resolving the Simplicity Paradox, it reduces the FPR95 from 31.3% to 2.3%; when addressing sensor failures such as Gaussian noise, it boosts the detection performance (AUROC) from a baseline of 79.7% to 94.9%.

AI.tldr: Counter-intuitive Simplicity Paradox in SOTA OOD detection: models excel at semantically subtle OOD but are geometrically blind to structurally distinct or noisy samples due to Semantic Hegemony; proposes D-KNN to decouple semantic and structural information via orthogonal decomposition and dual-space calibration; achieves SOTA on CIFAR/ImageNet, notably reducing FPR95 and boosting AUROC under Gaussian noise.
AI.motivation: Address mismatch between semantic sensitivity and geometric blindness in feature-based post-hoc OOD methods; reveal Semantic Hegemony and its neural-collapse-based mechanism; develop a training-free, plug-and-play solution.
AI.method: D-KNN: training-free framework; uses orthogonal decomposition to separate semantic components from structural residuals; introduces dual-space calibration to reactivate sensitivity to weak residual signals; operates in a geometric space rather than learning new parameters.
AI.result: Empirical results show strong improvements: FPR95 from 31.3% to 2.3%; AUROC on Gaussian noise from 79.7% to 94.9%; claims SOTA on CIFAR and ImageNet.
AI.conclusion: Breaks Semantic Hegemony and resolves the Simplicity Paradox; validates the neural-collapse-inspired view of feature space; provides a practical, effective method for robust OOD detection.

title: RaBiT: Residual-Aware Binarization Training for Accurate and Efficient LLMs
summary: Efficient deployment of large language models (LLMs) requires extreme quantization, forcing a critical trade-off between low-bit efficiency and performance. Residual binarization enables hardware-friendly, matmul-free inference by stacking binary ($\pm$1) layers, but is plagued by pathological feature co-adaptation. We identify a key failure mode, which we term inter-path adaptation: during quantization-aware training (QAT), parallel residual binary paths learn redundant features, degrading the error-compensation structure and limiting the expressive capacity of the model. While prior work relies on heuristic workarounds (e.g., path freezing) that constrain the solution space, we propose RaBiT, a novel quantization framework that resolves co-adaptation by algorithmically enforcing a residual hierarchy. Its core mechanism sequentially derives each binary path from a single shared full-precision weight, which ensures that every path corrects the error of the preceding one. This process is stabilized by a robust initialization that prioritizes functional preservation over mere weight approximation. RaBiT redefines the 2-bit accuracy-efficiency frontier: it achieves state-of-the-art performance, rivals even hardware-intensive Vector Quantization (VQ) methods, and delivers a $4.49\times$ inference speed-up over full-precision models on an RTX 4090.

AI.tldr: RaBiT tackles inter-path adaptation in residual binarization for extreme-quantized LLMs by sequentially deriving each binary path from a shared full-precision weight to enforce a residual hierarchy, with a robust initialization that prioritizes functional preservation. It achieves state-of-the-art 2-bit accuracy–efficiency and ~4.5x speedup over FP on GPUs.
AI.motivation: Enable efficient deployment of large language models under extreme quantization (2-bit) without sacrificing performance. Residual binarization introduces inter-path adaptation where parallel binary paths learn redundant features, compromising error compensation and expressivity; prior heuristics (e.g., path freezing) constrain optimization.
AI.method: Propose RaBiT, which enforces a residual hierarchy by sequentially deriving each binary path from a single shared full-precision weight. This creates a progressive error-correction chain across paths. Stabilized by a robust, function-preserving initialization during QAT.
AI.result: RaBiT achieves state-of-the-art performance on the 2-bit frontier, rivals hardware-intense VQ methods, and delivers a 4.49× inference speed-up over full-precision models on an RTX 4090.
AI.conclusion: RaBiT introduces a principled residual-binaraization framework that mitigates inter-path adaptation, expands the effective 2-bit accuracy–efficiency frontier, and offers substantial practical speedups for deploying LLMs on commodity hardware.

title: Stochastic hierarchical data-driven optimization: application to plasma-surface kinetics
summary: This work introduces a stochastic hierarchical optimization framework inspired by Sloppy Model theory for the efficient calibration of physical models. Central to this method is the use of a reduced Hessian approximation, which identifies and targets the stiff parameter subspace using minimal simulation queries. This strategy enables efficient navigation of highly anisotropic landscapes, avoiding the computational burden of exhaustive sampling. To ensure rigorous inference, we integrate this approach with a probabilistic formulation that derives a principled objective loss function directly from observed data. We validate the framework by applying it to the problem of plasma-surface interactions, where accurate modelling is strictly limited by uncertainties in surface reactivity parameters and the computational cost of kinetic simulations. Comparative analysis demonstrates that our method consistently outperforms baseline optimization techniques in sample efficiency. This approach offers a general and scalable tool for optimizing models of complex reaction systems, ranging from plasma chemistry to biochemical networks.

AI.tldr: A stochastic, hierarchical optimization method using a reduced Hessian to identify stiff parameter subspaces, enabling sample-efficient calibration of complex reaction models under uncertainty; validated on plasma-surface interactions and argued to generalize to broader reaction networks.
AI.motivation: Calibrating expensive physical-chemistry models under uncertainty is computationally challenging due to anisotropic, stiff parameter landscapes; there is a need to reduce simulations while maintaining rigorous inference; Sloppy Model theory motivates focusing on stiff directions; integration with a probabilistic framework yields a principled loss derived from data.
AI.method: Compute a reduced Hessian to locate the stiff subspace; perform hierarchical stochastic optimization that targets this subspace with minimal simulation queries; formulate a probabilistic objective directly from observed data; validate on plasma–surface interactions using kinetic simulations.
AI.result: The method achieves higher sample efficiency than baseline optimizers, robustly navigating anisotropic loss landscapes; demonstrated improvements in plasma-surface calibration and suggested general applicability to complex reaction systems.
