AI.conclusion: A scalable, general tool for calibrating models of complex reaction systems under uncertainty, combining reduced Hessian-based subspace identification with probabilistic inference to drive efficient, data-consistent optimization across domains such as plasma chemistry and biochemical networks.

title: Imagine a City: CityGenAgent for Procedural 3D City Generation
summary: The automated generation of interactive 3D cities is a critical challenge with broad applications in autonomous driving, virtual reality, and embodied intelligence. While recent advances in generative models and procedural techniques have improved the realism of city generation, existing methods often struggle with high-fidelity asset creation, controllability, and manipulation. In this work, we introduce CityGenAgent, a natural language-driven framework for hierarchical procedural generation of high-quality 3D cities. Our approach decomposes city generation into two interpretable components, Block Program and Building Program. To ensure structural correctness and semantic alignment, we adopt a two-stage learning strategy: (1) Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT). We train BlockGen and BuildingGen to generate valid programs that adhere to schema constraints, including non-self-intersecting polygons and complete fields; (2) Reinforcement Learning (RL). We design Spatial Alignment Reward to enhance spatial reasoning ability and Visual Consistency Reward to bridge the gap between textual descriptions and the visual modality. Benefiting from the programs and the models' generalization, CityGenAgent supports natural language editing and manipulation. Comprehensive evaluations demonstrate superior semantic alignment, visual quality, and controllability compared to existing methods, establishing a robust foundation for scalable 3D city generation.

AI.tldr: CityGenAgent introduces a natural language-driven, hierarchical procedural framework for high-quality 3D city generation, decomposing into Block and Building programs with supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning to enhance correctness, spatial alignment, and visual-textual consistency.
AI.motivation: Automated generation of interactive 3D cities is crucial for autonomous driving, VR, and embodied intelligence, but current methods lack high-fidelity asset creation, controllability, and manipulability despite advances in generative and procedural techniques.
AI.method: Proposes CityGenAgent, a NL-driven hierarchical procedural pipeline decomposing generation into Block Program and Building Program. Train via two-stage learning: (1) Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) to produce schema-adherent, valid programs; (2) Reinforcement Learning (RL) with Spatial Alignment Reward and Visual Consistency Reward to align spatial reasoning with textual descriptions and visuals. Supports natural language editing and manipulation.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show superior semantic alignment, visual quality, and controllability compared with existing methods, indicating strong potential for scalable 3D city generation.
AI.conclusion: NL-driven hierarchical generation with explicit program decomposition and RL rewards provides a robust, controllable, and scalable foundation for 3D city generation; CityGenAgent enables effective NL-based editing and manipulation.

title: ReFORM: Reflected Flows for On-support Offline RL via Noise Manipulation
comment: 24 pages, 17 figures; Accepted by the fourteenth International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2026)
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) aims to learn the optimal policy from a fixed dataset generated by behavior policies without additional environment interactions. One common challenge that arises in this setting is the out-of-distribution (OOD) error, which occurs when the policy leaves the training distribution. Prior methods penalize a statistical distance term to keep the policy close to the behavior policy, but this constrains policy improvement and may not completely prevent OOD actions. Another challenge is that the optimal policy distribution can be multimodal and difficult to represent. Recent works apply diffusion or flow policies to address this problem, but it is unclear how to avoid OOD errors while retaining policy expressiveness. We propose ReFORM, an offline RL method based on flow policies that enforces the less restrictive support constraint by construction. ReFORM learns a behavior cloning (BC) flow policy with a bounded source distribution to capture the support of the action distribution, then optimizes a reflected flow that generates bounded noise for the BC flow while keeping the support, to maximize the performance. Across 40 challenging tasks from the OGBench benchmark with datasets of varying quality and using a constant set of hyperparameters for all tasks, ReFORM dominates all baselines with hand-tuned hyperparameters on the performance profile curves.

AI.tldr: Offline RL with flow policies (ReFORM) that enforce a support constraint by construction, using a BC flow with bounded source to capture action support and a reflected flow to add bounded noise while preserving support, achieving strong results on OGBench with fixed hyperparameters.
AI.motivation: Alleviate OOD errors in offline RL and address multimodal optimal policies without overly constraining policy improvement; existing distance-penalty methods can restrict performance and may not fully prevent OOD actions.
AI.method: Learn a BC flow policy with bounded source to capture action distribution support; optimize a reflected flow that generates bounded noise for the BC flow while keeping support fixed; resulting policy remains within the support of the learned BC flow, mitigating OOD while improving performance.
AI.result: On 40 tasks from the OGBench benchmark with varying data quality, ReFORM dominates baselines when using a constant set of hyperparameters across all tasks, according to performance-profile curves.
AI.conclusion: ReFORM provides a less restrictive, construction-based support constraint via flow policies, enabling expressive multimodal policies and effective OOD control, with strong empirical performance across diverse datasets.

title: Clinical Validation of Medical-based Large Language Model Chatbots on Ophthalmic Patient Queries with LLM-based Evaluation
summary: Domain specific large language models are increasingly used to support patient education, triage, and clinical decision making in ophthalmology, making rigorous evaluation essential to ensure safety and accuracy. This study evaluated four small medical LLMs Meerkat-7B, BioMistral-7B, OpenBioLLM-8B, and MedLLaMA3-v20 in answering ophthalmology related patient queries and assessed the feasibility of LLM based evaluation against clinician grading. In this cross sectional study, 180 ophthalmology patient queries were answered by each model, generating 2160 responses. Models were selected for parameter sizes under 10 billion to enable resource efficient deployment. Responses were evaluated by three ophthalmologists of differing seniority and by GPT-4-Turbo using the S.C.O.R.E. framework assessing safety, consensus and context, objectivity, reproducibility, and explainability, with ratings assigned on a five point Likert scale. Agreement between LLM and clinician grading was assessed using Spearman rank correlation, Kendall tau statistics, and kernel density estimate analyses. Meerkat-7B achieved the highest performance with mean scores of 3.44 from Senior Consultants, 4.08 from Consultants, and 4.18 from Residents. MedLLaMA3-v20 performed poorest, with 25.5 percent of responses containing hallucinations or clinically misleading content, including fabricated terminology. GPT-4-Turbo grading showed strong alignment with clinician assessments overall, with Spearman rho of 0.80 and Kendall tau of 0.67, though Senior Consultants graded more conservatively. Overall, medical LLMs demonstrated potential for safe ophthalmic question answering, but gaps remained in clinical depth and consensus, supporting the feasibility of LLM based evaluation for large scale benchmarking and the need for hybrid automated and clinician review frameworks to guide safe clinical deployment.

AI.tldr: Four small medical LLMs (≤10B) were evaluated on 180 ophthalmology queries each (2160 responses). Meerkat-7B performed best; MedLLaMA3-v20 showed substantial hallucinations. GPT-4-Turbo aligned with clinicians. LLM-based evaluation is feasible but requires hybrid clinician review for safe deployment.
AI.motivation: Assess safety, accuracy, and clinical usefulness of domain-specific ophthalmology LLMs and validate the feasibility of LLM-based benchmarking at scale.
AI.method: Cross-sectional evaluation with four LLMs under 10B; 180 ophthalmology patient queries per model (2160 responses total); responses rated by three ophthalmologists of varying seniority and by GPT-4-Turbo using the S.C.O.R.E. framework (Safety/Consensus/Context/Objectivity/Reproducibility/Explainability) on a 5-point Likert scale. Agreement between LLM and clinician grades assessed via Spearman rho, Kendall tau, and kernel density estimates.
AI.result: Meerkat-7B achieved the highest mean scores across seniority groups (Senior Consultants 3.44; Consultants 4.08; Residents 4.18). MedLLaMA3-v20 had 25.5% of responses containing hallucinations or clinically misleading content. GPT-4-Turbo grading aligned well with clinicians (Spearman 0.80; Kendall 0.67); Senior Consultants graded more conservatively. Overall, medical LLMs show potential for safe ophthalmic QA but show gaps in clinical depth and consensus, supporting feasibility of LLM-based benchmarking and the need for hybrid automated-plus-clinician review for deployment.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific ophthalmology LLMs can safely answer patient queries with caveats; LLM-based evaluation is a feasible scalability approach for benchmarking and guiding safe deployment via hybrid, clinician-in-the-loop frameworks.

title: Near-Optimal Dynamic Matching via Coarsening with Application to Heart Transplantation
summary: Online matching has been a mainstay in domains such as Internet advertising and organ allocation, but practical algorithms often lack strong theoretical guarantees. We take an important step toward addressing this by developing new online matching algorithms based on a coarsening approach. Although coarsening typically implies a loss of granularity, we show that, to the contrary, aggregating offline nodes into capacitated clusters can yield near-optimal theoretical guarantees. We apply our methodology to heart transplant allocation to develop theoretically grounded policies based on structural properties of historical data. In realistic simulations, our policy closely matches the performance of the omniscient benchmark. Our work bridges the gap between data-driven heuristics and pessimistic theoretical lower bounds, and provides rigorous justification for prior clustering-based approaches in organ allocation.

AI.tldr: Coarsening via capacitated clustering for online matching yields near-optimal guarantees and strong empirical performance, applied to organ allocation to bridge data-driven heuristics with worst-case bounds.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between data-driven heuristics and pessimistic theoretical lower bounds in online matching by introducing coarsening-based aggregation to preserve theoretical guarantees while leveraging historical data.
AI.method: Develop online matching algorithms that coarsen the offline graph by aggregating nodes into capacitated clusters, then solve the online problem on the coarse graph and translate decisions back to the original nodes. Apply this to heart transplant allocation using structural properties of historical data and evaluate via realistic simulations.
AI.result: The approach achieves near-optimal theoretical guarantees and empirical performance that closely matches an omniscient benchmark in simulations, validating clustering-based strategies.
AI.conclusion: Coarsening can retain granularity and deliver rigorous guarantees in online matching; supports data-driven, clustering-based approaches for organ allocation and potentially other domains.

title: SAIL: Self-Amplified Iterative Learning for Diffusion Model Alignment with Minimal Human Feedback
summary: Aligning diffusion models with human preferences remains challenging, particularly when reward models are unavailable or impractical to obtain, and collecting large-scale preference datasets is prohibitively expensive. \textit{This raises a fundamental question: can we achieve effective alignment using only minimal human feedback, without auxiliary reward models, by unlocking the latent capabilities within diffusion models themselves?} In this paper, we propose \textbf{SAIL} (\textbf{S}elf-\textbf{A}mplified \textbf{I}terative \textbf{L}earning), a novel framework that enables diffusion models to act as their own teachers through iterative self-improvement. Starting from a minimal seed set of human-annotated preference pairs, SAIL operates in a closed-loop manner where the model progressively generates diverse samples, self-annotates preferences based on its evolving understanding, and refines itself using this self-augmented dataset. To ensure robust learning and prevent catastrophic forgetting, we introduce a ranked preference mixup strategy that carefully balances exploration with adherence to initial human priors. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SAIL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple benchmarks while using merely 6\% of the preference data required by existing approaches, revealing that diffusion models possess remarkable self-improvement capabilities that, when properly harnessed, can effectively replace both large-scale human annotation and external reward models.

AI.tldr: SAIL enables diffusion models to self-improve with minimal human preference data by iterative self-labeling and ranked mixup, achieving strong alignment with only 6% of the data used by previous methods.
AI.motivation: Large-scale human preference data is expensive, and external reward models may be impractical. The latent capabilities of diffusion models may be harnessed for self-guided alignment.
AI.method: Start from a small seed of human-annotated preferences; operate in a closed loop where the model generates diverse samples, self-labels preferences, and refines itself with a self-augmented dataset. Employ a ranked preference mixup to balance exploration with adherence to initial human priors to mitigate forgetting.
AI.result: SAIL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across benchmarks while using only ~6% of the preference data required by existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion models possess strong self-improvement capabilities that can substitute for large-scale human annotation and external reward models when guided by appropriate self-supervision and data-augmentation strategies.

title: Advancing Opinion Dynamics Modeling with Neural Diffusion-Convection-Reaction Equation
summary: Advanced opinion dynamics modeling is vital for deciphering social behavior, emphasizing its role in mitigating polarization and securing cyberspace. To synergize mechanistic interpretability with data-driven flexibility, recent studies have explored the integration of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for opinion modeling. Despite this promise, existing methods are tailored to incomplete priors, lacking a comprehensive physical system to integrate dynamics from local, global, and endogenous levels. Moreover, penalty-based constraints adopted in existing methods struggle to deeply encode physical priors, leading to optimization pathologies and discrepancy between latent representations and physical transparency. To this end, we offer a physical view to interpret opinion dynamics via Diffusion-Convection-Reaction (DCR) system inspired by interacting particle theory. Building upon the Neural ODEs, we define the neural opinion dynamics to coordinate neural networks with physical priors, and further present the OPINN, a physics-informed neural framework for opinion dynamics modeling. Evaluated on real-world and synthetic datasets, OPINN achieves state-of-the-art performance in opinion evolution forecasting, offering a promising paradigm for the nexus of cyber, physical, and social systems.

AI.tldr: OPINN: a physics-informed neural framework for opinion dynamics using a diffusion-convection-reaction (DCR) system within Neural ODEs; claims state-of-the-art forecasting on real-world and synthetic data.
AI.motivation: Bridge mechanistic interpretability and data-driven flexibility in opinion dynamics; address incomplete priors and optimization issues of penalty-based PINNs; enable integration across local, global, and endogenous dynamics with increased physical transparency.
AI.method: Introduce a DCR PDE-inspired system from interacting particle theory and embed it in Neural ODEs. OPINN coordinates neural networks with physical priors, forming a physics-informed neural framework for opinion dynamics.
AI.result: Evaluated on real-world and synthetic datasets; OPINN achieves state-of-the-art performance in forecasting opinion evolution.
AI.conclusion: OPINN provides a principled, interpretable, and effective framework at the cyber–physical–social nexus for opinion dynamics, offering deeper physical priors encoding and improved latent–physical consistency.

title: Position: Machine Learning for Heart Transplant Allocation Policy Optimization Should Account for Incentives
summary: The allocation of scarce donor organs constitutes one of the most consequential algorithmic challenges in healthcare. While the field is rapidly transitioning from rigid, rule-based systems to machine learning and data-driven optimization, we argue that current approaches often overlook a fundamental barrier: incentives. In this position paper, we highlight that organ allocation is not merely a static optimization problem, but rather a complex game involving transplant centers, clinicians, and regulators. Focusing on US adult heart transplant allocation, we identify critical incentive misalignments across the decision-making pipeline, and present data showing that they are having adverse consequences today. Our main position is that the next generation of allocation policies should be incentive aware. We outline a research agenda for the machine learning community, calling for the integration of mechanism design, strategic classification, causal inference, and social choice to ensure robustness, efficiency, and fairness in the face of strategic behavior from the various constituent groups.

AI.tldr: Incentive misalignment in US heart transplant allocation; argues for incentive-aware policy design and an ML-driven research agenda.
AI.motivation: Organ allocation is a high-stakes, dynamic game among transplant centers, clinicians, regulators; ignoring incentives risks suboptimal, unfair outcomes and reduced system efficiency.
AI.method: Conceptual, theory-driven analysis of the allocation pipeline to identify incentive misalignments, supported by data; proposes a research agenda uniting mechanism design, strategic classification, causal inference, and social choice.
AI.result: Evidence of incentive misalignments across the transplant decision pipeline with adverse consequences; a clear call for incentive-aware allocation policies.
AI.conclusion: Future organ allocation policies should be incentive-aware; integrate mechanism design, strategic ML methods, and causal inference to address strategic behavior and improve robustness, efficiency, and fairness.

title: VRIQ: Benchmarking and Analyzing Visual-Reasoning IQ of VLMs
summary: Recent progress in Vision Language Models (VLMs) has raised the question of whether they can reliably perform nonverbal reasoning. To this end, we introduce VRIQ (Visual Reasoning IQ), a novel benchmark designed to assess and analyze the visual reasoning ability of VLMs. We evaluate models on two sets of tasks: abstract puzzle-style and natural-image reasoning tasks. We find that on abstract puzzles, performance remains near random with an average accuracy of around 28%, while natural tasks yield better but still weak results with 45% accuracy. We also find that tool-augmented reasoning demonstrates only modest improvements. To uncover the source of this weakness, we introduce diagnostic probes targeting perception and reasoning. Our analysis demonstrates that around 56% of failures arise from perception alone, 43% from both perception and reasoning, and only a mere 1% from reasoning alone. This motivates us to design fine-grained diagnostic probe questions targeting specific perception categories (e.g., shape, count, position, 3D/depth), revealing that certain categories cause more failures than others. Our benchmark and analysis establish that current VLMs, even with visual reasoning tools, remain unreliable abstract reasoners, mostly due to perception limitations, and offer a principled basis for improving visual reasoning in multimodal systems.

AI.tldr: Introduce VRIQ, a benchmark for visual reasoning in VLMs, showing perception is the main bottleneck; abstract puzzles are near random, natural tasks modestly better; tool-augmented reasoning helps little; diagnostic probes separate perception vs reasoning, revealing perceptual limitations dominate across categories.
AI.motivation: Assess whether vision-language models can reliably perform abstract visual reasoning and identify the sources of failure to guide improvements.
AI.method: Two task sets (abstract puzzle-style, natural-image reasoning) evaluated on multiple VLMs with and without tools; develop diagnostic probes to separate perception and reasoning contributions; analyze failure modes and category-specific perception errors.
AI.result: Abstract puzzle performance ~28% (near random); natural-image tasks ~45%; tool-augmented reasoning yields only modest gains; diagnostic split: ~56% failures due to perception alone, ~43% due to perception plus reasoning, ~1% due to pure reasoning; fine-grained perception probes reveal varied difficulty across perception categories.
AI.conclusion: Current VLMs remain unreliable abstract reasoners; perceptual limitations are the primary bottleneck; the benchmark and probes provide a principled basis for diagnosing and improving visual reasoning in multimodal systems.

title: H-AdminSim: A Multi-Agent Simulator for Realistic Hospital Administrative Workflows with FHIR Integration
summary: Hospital administration departments handle a wide range of operational tasks and, in large hospitals, process over 10,000 requests per day, driving growing interest in LLM-based automation. However, prior work has focused primarily on patient--physician interactions or isolated administrative subtasks, failing to capture the complexity of real administrative workflows. To address this gap, we propose H-AdminSim, a comprehensive end-to-end simulation framework that combines realistic data generation with multi-agent-based simulation of hospital administrative workflows. These tasks are quantitatively evaluated using detailed rubrics, enabling systematic comparison of LLMs. Through FHIR integration, H-AdminSim provides a unified and interoperable environment for testing administrative workflows across heterogeneous hospital settings, serving as a standardized testbed for assessing the feasibility and performance of LLM-driven administrative automation.

AI.tldr: H-AdminSim is an end-to-end hospital administration simulation framework with realistic data generation, multi-agent workflow modeling, and FHIR-based interoperability to benchmark LLM-driven automation.
AI.motivation: Current work largely targets patient–physician interactions or isolated administrative tasks and omits the complexity of real hospital admin workflows; there is a need for a standardized, scalable testbed to evaluate LLM automation in high-volume, heterogeneous hospital settings.
AI.method: Integrates realistic data generation, multi-agent simulation of hospital administrative processes, quantitative rubrics for evaluation, and FHIR-based interoperability to enable cross-site testing.
AI.result: Proposes a comprehensive evaluation framework and testbed that enables systematic comparison of LLMs for hospital administration tasks across diverse settings; emphasizes feasibility and performance assessment of LLM-driven automation.
AI.conclusion: H-AdminSim provides a unified, interoperable platform for testing and benchmarking LLM-driven administrative automation across heterogeneous hospital environments.

title: Learning Rate Matters: Vanilla LoRA May Suffice for LLM Fine-tuning
summary: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is the prevailing approach for efficient large language model (LLM) fine-tuning. Building on this paradigm, recent studies have proposed alternative initialization strategies and architectural modifications, reporting substantial improvements over vanilla LoRA. However, these gains are often demonstrated under fixed or narrowly tuned hyperparameter settings, despite the known sensitivity of neural networks to training configurations. In this work, we systematically re-evaluate four representative LoRA variants alongside vanilla LoRA through extensive hyperparameter searches. Across mathematical and code generation tasks on diverse model scales, we find that different LoRA methods favor distinct learning rate ranges. Crucially, once learning rates are properly tuned, all methods achieve similar peak performance (within 1-2%), with only subtle rank-dependent behaviors. These results suggest that vanilla LoRA remains a competitive baseline and that improvements reported under single training configuration may not reflect consistent methodological advantages. Finally, a second-order analysis attributes the differing optimal learning rate ranges to variations in the largest Hessian eigenvalue, aligning with classical learning theories.

AI.tldr: Systematic re-evaluation of four LoRA variants and vanilla LoRA under extensive hyperparameter searches. Finds that with proper learning rate tuning, all methods achieve similar peak performance (within 1–2%), with only subtle rank-dependent effects; vanilla LoRA remains a strong baseline. A second-order analysis links optimal learning rate ranges to the largest Hessian eigenvalue, aligning with learning theory.
AI.motivation: Assess whether reported gains of alternative LoRA initializations and architectures generalize beyond fixed training configurations, and determine the robustness of LoRA variants to hyperparameters. Explain why different methods favor distinct learning rates and relate findings to theoretical aspects of optimization.
AI.method: Systematic hyperparameter search across four LoRA variants plus vanilla LoRA on mathematical and code generation tasks, across multiple model scales. Evaluate performance across a range of learning rates to identify each method’s optimal LR window. Complement with a second-order analysis of the Hessian to relate LR sensitivity to the largest eigenvalue.
AI.result: When learning rates are properly tuned, all LoRA variants reach similar peak performance (within 1–2%), with only subtle rank-dependent differences. Gains reported under single training configurations do not translate into robust advantages. Different methods exhibit different LR ranges, but vanilla LoRA remains a competitive baseline.
AI.conclusion: Vanilla LoRA remains a competitive baseline when hyperparameters are tuned; claimed improvements from alternative initializations/architectures may reflect configuration sensitivity rather than intrinsic superiority. The LR sensitivity differences align with Hessian eigenvalue magnitudes, offering a theoretical explanation for method-dependent LR windows.

title: Dolphin-v2: Universal Document Parsing via Scalable Anchor Prompting
summary: Document parsing has garnered widespread attention as vision-language models (VLMs) advance OCR capabilities. However, the field remains fragmented across dozens of specialized models with varying strengths, forcing users to navigate complex model selection and limiting system scalability. Moreover, existing two-stage approaches depend on axis-aligned bounding boxes for layout detection, failing to handle distorted or photographed documents effectively. To this end, we present Dolphin-v2, a two-stage document image parsing model that substantially improves upon the original Dolphin. In the first stage, Dolphin-v2 jointly performs document type classification (digital-born versus photographed) alongside layout analysis. For digital-born documents, it conducts finer-grained element detection with reading order prediction. In the second stage, we employ a hybrid parsing strategy: photographed documents are parsed holistically as complete pages to handle geometric distortions, while digital-born documents undergo element-wise parallel parsing guided by the detected layout anchors, enabling efficient content extraction. Compared with the original Dolphin, Dolphin-v2 introduces several crucial enhancements: (1) robust parsing of photographed documents via holistic page-level understanding, (2) finer-grained element detection (21 categories) with semantic attribute extraction such as author information and document metadata, and (3) code block recognition with indentation preservation, which existing systems typically lack. Comprehensive evaluations are conducted on DocPTBench, OmniDocBench, and our self-constructed RealDoc-160 benchmark. The results demonstrate substantial improvements: +14.78 points overall on the challenging OmniDocBench and 91% error reduction on photographed documents, while maintaining efficient inference through parallel processing.

AI.tldr: A two-stage, unified document image parsing model (Dolphin-v2) that handles both photographed and digital-born documents with holistic page parsing for photos, finer-grained element detection (21 categories) and semantic attributes, and substantial accuracy improvements with efficient parallel inference.
AI.motivation: Field remains fragmented with dozens of specialized models and axis-aligned layout detection; existing two-stage approaches struggle with distorted or photographed documents and scalability; need a unified, robust, and efficient parsing framework.
AI.method: Stage 1: joint document type classification (digital-born vs photographed) and layout analysis; for digital-born, fine-grained element detection with reading order prediction. Stage 2: hybrid parsing: photographed documents parsed holistically as complete pages to handle geometric distortions; digital-born documents parsed element-wise guided by detected layout anchors; 21 element categories; semantic attribute extraction (author, metadata); code block recognition with indentation preservation; parallel inference for efficiency.
AI.result: Compared with original Dolphin, Dolphin-v2 achieves substantial gains: +14.78 points on OmniDocBench and 91% error reduction on photographed documents; evaluated on DocPTBench, OmniDocBench, and RealDoc-160; maintains efficient inference via parallel processing.
AI.conclusion: Dolphin-v2 delivers robust, scalable document image parsing by unifying handling of photographed and digital-born documents, enabling richer content extraction (semantic attributes and code blocks) and improved accuracy without sacrificing efficiency.

title: THOR: Inductive Link Prediction over Hyper-Relational Knowledge Graphs
summary: Knowledge graphs (KGs) have become a key ingredient supporting a variety of applications. Beyond the traditional triplet representation of facts where a relation connects two entities, modern KGs observe an increasing number of hyper-relational facts, where an arbitrary number of qualifiers associated with a triplet provide auxiliary information to further describe the rich semantics of the triplet, which can effectively boost the reasoning performance in link prediction tasks. However, existing link prediction techniques over such hyper-relational KGs (HKGs) mostly focus on a transductive setting, where KG embedding models are learned from the specific vocabulary of a given KG and subsequently can only make predictions within the same vocabulary, limiting their generalizability to previously unseen vocabularies. Against this background, we propose THOR, an inducTive link prediction technique for Hyper-relational knOwledge gRaphs. Specifically, we first introduce both relation and entity foundation graphs, modeling their fundamental inter- and intra-fact interactions in HKGs, which are agnostic to any specific relations and entities. Afterward, THOR is designed to learn from the two foundation graphs with two parallel graph encoders followed by a transformer decoder, which supports efficient masked training and fully-inductive inference. We conduct a thorough evaluation of THOR in hyper-relational link prediction tasks on 12 datasets with different settings. Results show that THOR outperforms a sizable collection of baselines, yielding 66.1%, 55.9%, and 20.4% improvement over the best-performing rule-based, semi-inductive, and fully-inductive techniques, respectively. A series of ablation studies also reveals our key design factors capturing the structural invariance transferable across HKGs for inductive tasks.

AI.tldr: THOR is an inductive approach for hyper-relational knowledge graph link prediction that learns from relation- and entity-foundation graphs using dual encoders and a transformer decoder, enabling fully inductive inference and strong generalization across datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing hyper-relational KG link prediction methods are largely transductive and struggle to generalize to unseen vocabularies; there is a need for an inductive, transferable framework that captures invariant structural information across hyper-relational facts.
AI.method: THOR constructs relation and entity foundation graphs capturing fundamental inter- and intra-fact interactions; employs two parallel graph encoders (one per foundation graph) followed by a transformer decoder; uses efficient masked training to enable fully-inductive inference on unseen entities/relations.
AI.result: Evaluated on 12 datasets; THOR outperforms baselines, achieving 66.1% improvement over the best rule-based method, 55.9% over the best semi-inductive method, and 20.4% over the best fully-inductive method; ablation studies identify key design factors that capture transferable structural invariance across HKGs.
AI.conclusion: THOR provides an effective inductive framework for hyper-relational link prediction by leveraging relation/entity foundation graphs and a dual-encoder + transformer architecture, demonstrating strong empirical gains and insight into invariant structural factors for inductive generalization.

title: EntRGi: Entropy Aware Reward Guidance for Diffusion Language Models
summary: Reward guidance has been applied to great success in the test-time adaptation of continuous diffusion models; it updates each denoising step using the gradients from a downstream reward model. We study reward guidance for discrete diffusion language models, where one cannot differentiate through the natural outputs of the model because they are discrete tokens. Existing approaches either replace these discrete tokens with continuous relaxations, or employ techniques like the straight-through estimator. In this work, we show the downsides of both these methods. The former degrades gradient feedback because the reward model has never been trained with continuous inputs. The latter involves incorrect optimization because the gradient evaluated at discrete tokens is used to update continuous logits. Our key innovation is to go beyond this tradeoff by introducing a novel mechanism called EntRGi: Entropy aware Reward Guidance that dynamically regulates the gradients from the reward model. By modulating the continuous relaxation using the model's confidence, our approach substantially improves reward guidance while providing reliable inputs to the reward model. We empirically validate our approach on a 7B-parameter diffusion language model across 3 diverse reward models and 3 multi-skill benchmarks, showing consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: Entropy-aware Reward Guidance (EntRGi) for discrete diffusion language models dynamically regulates reward-model gradients by conditioning the continuous relaxation on the model's confidence, addressing gradient-feedback issues in prior continuous-relaxation and straight-through approaches; shows consistent gains on a 7B LM with multiple rewards and tasks.
AI.motivation: Reward guidance has boosted test-time adaptation of continuous diffusion models, but for discrete LMs the inability to backprop through tokens creates a gap. Existing fixes—continuous relaxations and straight-through estimators—suffer: relaxations mismatch reward model training, while straight-through uses gradients at discrete tokens to update continuous logits, causing mis-optimization. A principled method is needed to provide reliable, informative gradients while preserving realistic reward-model inputs.
AI.method: Introduce EntRGi, which modulates the continuous relaxation based on the model's entropy (confidence) to regulate gradients from the reward model. This entropy-aware mechanism aims to deliver more reliable reward feedback and better-aligned inputs to the reward model, improving optimization stability and performance.
AI.result: Empirical validation on a 7B-parameter diffusion language model across 3 diverse reward models and 3 multi-skill benchmarks shows consistent improvements over state-of-the-art reward-guidance methods.
AI.conclusion: EntRGi offers a principled, entropy-aware solution to reward-guided optimization for discrete diffusion LMs, achieving better gradient quality and stability by adapting the relaxation according to model confidence, with demonstrated gains across multiple rewards and tasks.

title: Parallel Swin Transformer-Enhanced 3D MRI-to-CT Synthesis for MRI-Only Radiotherapy Planning
summary: MRI provides superior soft tissue contrast without ionizing radiation; however, the absence of electron density information limits its direct use for dose calculation. As a result, current radiotherapy workflows rely on combined MRI and CT acquisitions, increasing registration uncertainty and procedural complexity. Synthetic CT generation enables MRI only planning but remains challenging due to nonlinear MRI-CT relationships and anatomical variability. We propose Parallel Swin Transformer-Enhanced Med2Transformer, a 3D architecture that integrates convolutional encoding with dual Swin Transformer branches to model both local anatomical detail and long-range contextual dependencies. Multi-scale shifted window attention with hierarchical feature aggregation improves anatomical fidelity. Experiments on public and clinical datasets demonstrate higher image similarity and improved geometric accuracy compared with baseline methods. Dosimetric evaluation shows clinically acceptable performance, with a mean target dose error of 1.69%. Code is available at: https://github.com/mobaidoctor/med2transformer.

AI.tldr: A 3D parallel Swin Transformer-enhanced Med2Transformer for MRI-to-CT synthesis enabling MRI-only radiotherapy planning, achieving improved image similarity, geometric fidelity, and dosimetric accuracy (mean target dose error ~1.69%).
AI.motivation: MRI provides superior soft-tissue contrast but lacks electron density; CT is needed for dose calculation. MRI-only planning is desirable but challenging due to nonlinear MRI-CT relationships and anatomical variability. A model that captures local detail and long-range context could improve synthetic CT quality and dosimetry.
AI.method: Introduce Parallel Swin Transformer-Enhanced Med2Transformer, a 3D architecture combining convolutional encoding with dual Swin Transformer branches to model local and long-range dependencies. Uses multi-scale shifted window attention with hierarchical feature aggregation to improve anatomical fidelity. Evaluated on public and clinical datasets against baselines.
AI.result: Demonstrates higher image similarity and improved geometric accuracy versus baselines; dosimetric evaluation shows clinically acceptable performance with mean target dose error of 1.69%. Code released (GitHub).
AI.conclusion: The approach advances MRI-to-CT synthesis for MRI-only planning, showing strong image and dosimetric performance; supports potential clinical adoption; code availability facilitates replication.

title: RFM-Pose:Reinforcement-Guided Flow Matching for Fast Category-Level 6D Pose Estimation
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Object pose estimation is a fundamental problem in computer vision and plays a critical role in virtual reality and embodied intelligence, where agents must understand and interact with objects in 3D space. Recently, score based generative models have to some extent solved the rotational symmetry ambiguity problem in category level pose estimation, but their efficiency remains limited by the high sampling cost of score-based diffusion. In this work, we propose a new framework, RFM-Pose, that accelerates category-level 6D object pose generation while actively evaluating sampled hypotheses. To improve sampling efficiency, we adopt a flow-matching generative model and generate pose candidates along an optimal transport path from a simple prior to the pose distribution. To further refine these candidates, we cast the flow-matching sampling process as a Markov decision process and apply proximal policy optimization to fine-tune the sampling policy. In particular, we interpret the flow field as a learnable policy and map an estimator to a value network, enabling joint optimization of pose generation and hypothesis scoring within a reinforcement learning framework. Experiments on the REAL275 benchmark demonstrate that RFM-Pose achieves favorable performance while significantly reducing computational cost. Moreover, similar to prior work, our approach can be readily adapted to object pose tracking and attains competitive results in this setting.

AI.tldr: RFM-Pose accelerates category-level 6D pose generation by integrating flow-matching sampling along an optimal transport path with reinforcement-learning refinement, achieving competitive accuracy at lower computational cost and extending to pose tracking.
AI.motivation: Score-based diffusion methods tackle rotational ambiguity but incur high sampling cost; there is a need for faster, efficient, and actively-evaluated pose hypothesis generation for category-level 6D pose estimation.
AI.method: 1) Use a flow-matching generative model to generate pose candidates along an optimal transport path from a simple prior to the target pose distribution. 2) Cast sampling as an MDP and apply PPO to optimize the sampling policy. 3) Treat the flow field as a learnable policy and connect an estimator to a value network, enabling joint optimization of pose generation and hypothesis scoring in an RL framework.
AI.result: REAL275 experiments show favorable accuracy with substantially reduced computation. The method also adapts to object pose tracking with competitive results.
AI.conclusion: RFM-Pose provides an efficient, RL-augmented framework for category-level 6D pose estimation and tracking by coupling flow-based sampling with hypothesis evaluation, achieving reduced compute without sacrificing performance.

title: M$^2$-Miner: Multi-Agent Enhanced MCTS for Mobile GUI Agent Data Mining
comment: Accepted by ICLR 2026. Supplementary material is included at the end of the main paper (16 pages, 15 figures, 2 tables)
summary: Graphical User Interface (GUI) agent is pivotal to advancing intelligent human-computer interaction paradigms. Constructing powerful GUI agents necessitates the large-scale annotation of high-quality user-behavior trajectory data (i.e., intent-trajectory pairs) for training. However, manual annotation methods and current GUI agent data mining approaches typically face three critical challenges: high construction cost, poor data quality, and low data richness. To address these issues, we propose M$^2$-Miner, the first low-cost and automated mobile GUI agent data-mining framework based on Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS). For better data mining efficiency and quality, we present a collaborative multi-agent framework, comprising InferAgent, OrchestraAgent, and JudgeAgent for guidance, acceleration, and evaluation. To further enhance the efficiency of mining and enrich intent diversity, we design an intent recycling strategy to extract extra valuable interaction trajectories. Additionally, a progressive model-in-the-loop training strategy is introduced to improve the success rate of data mining. Extensive experiments have demonstrated that the GUI agent fine-tuned using our mined data achieves state-of-the-art performance on several commonly used mobile GUI benchmarks. Our work will be released to facilitate the community research.

AI.tldr: A low-cost, automated mobile GUI data-mining framework (M^2-Miner) using Monte Carlo Tree Search and a collaborative multi-agent system to mine diverse intent-trajectory data; includes intent recycling and a progressive model-in-the-loop training strategy, achieving state-of-the-art results on mobile GUI benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To reduce the high cost, low quality, and limited diversity of manually annotated GUI interaction data required to train capable GUI agents.
AI.method: Introduce M^2-Miner, based on Monte Carlo Tree Search, with three agents (InferAgent, OrchestraAgent, JudgeAgent); implement intent recycling to boost data diversity; employ progressive model-in-the-loop training to improve mining success.
AI.result: Empirical results show that GUI agents fine-tuned on mined data attain state-of-the-art performance on multiple mobile GUI benchmarks; the authors also release the data/mining framework to the community.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework reduces data annotation costs while enhancing data quality and diversity, enabling scalable training of GUI agents and advancing intelligent HCI research.

title: Enhanced QKNorm normalization for neural transformers with the Lp norm
summary: The normalization of query and key vectors is an essential part of the Transformer architecture. It ensures that learning is stable regardless of the scale of these vectors. Some normalization approaches are available. In this preliminary work, a generalization of the QKNorm normalization scheme is proposed. The approach is based on the Lp norm, allowing non-Euclidean norms to be employed. Experimental results demonstrate the suitability of the method for a simple problem.

AI.tldr: Generalizes QKNorm normalization to use Lp norms for queries and keys in Transformers, enabling non-Euclidean normalizations; presents preliminary results on a simple task to show feasibility.
AI.motivation: To improve training stability and explore the impact of non-Euclidean normalization in Transformer attention by extending QKNorm with Lp norms.
AI.method: Extend the QKNorm scheme to operate with general Lp norms applied to query and key vectors, replacing the Euclidean (L2) normalization in the attention mechanism; evaluate on a simple benchmark task.
AI.result: Preliminary experiments indicate the proposed Lp-based QKNorm is suitable for a simple problem, suggesting feasibility of the approach.
AI.conclusion: An Lp-generalization of QKNorm is feasible and can be employed in Transformer attention; further experiments on more complex tasks and ablations are warranted to understand the effect of different p-values and normalization dynamics.

title: Dataset Distillation via Relative Distribution Matching and Cognitive Heritage
summary: Dataset distillation seeks to synthesize a highly compact dataset that achieves performance comparable to the original dataset on downstream tasks. For the classification task that use pre-trained self-supervised models as backbones, previous linear gradient matching optimizes synthetic images by encouraging them to mimic the gradient updates induced by real images on the linear classifier. However, this batch-level formulation requires loading thousands of real images and applying multiple rounds of differentiable augmentations to synthetic images at each distillation step, leading to substantial computational and memory overhead. In this paper, we introduce statistical flow matching , a stable and efficient supervised learning framework that optimizes synthetic images by aligning constant statistical flows from target class centers to non-target class centers in the original data. Our approach loads raw statistics only once and performs a single augmentation pass on the synthetic data, achieving performance comparable to or better than the state-of-the-art methods with 10x lower GPU memory usage and 4x shorter runtime. Furthermore, we propose a classifier inheritance strategy that reuses the classifier trained on the original dataset for inference, requiring only an extremely lightweight linear projector and marginal storage while achieving substantial performance gains.

AI.tldr: Introduces statistical flow matching for dataset distillation and a classifier inheritance strategy to reduce computation, memory, and storage, while maintaining or improving performance.
AI.motivation: Overcome the heavy compute and memory demands of gradient-based batch distillation for self-supervised backbones, enabling scalable and practical dataset distillation.
AI.method: Statistical flow matching aligns constant statistical flows from target class centers to non-target class centers in the original data using raw statistics loaded once and a single augmentation pass on synthetic data; additionally, a classifier inheritance strategy reuses the original classifier for inference with a lightweight linear projector.
AI.result: Attains performance comparable to or better than state-of-the-art methods with approximately 10x lower GPU memory usage and 4x shorter runtime.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides an efficient, scalable dataset distillation framework with practical deployment benefits, and the classifier inheritance further reduces inference cost with minimal storage overhead.

title: Formal Synthesis of Certifiably Robust Neural Lyapunov-Barrier Certificates
summary: Neural Lyapunov and barrier certificates have recently been used as powerful tools for verifying the safety and stability properties of deep reinforcement learning (RL) controllers. However, existing methods offer guarantees only under fixed ideal unperturbed dynamics, limiting their reliability in real-world applications where dynamics may deviate due to uncertainties. In this work, we study the problem of synthesizing \emph{robust neural Lyapunov barrier certificates} that maintain their guarantees under perturbations in system dynamics. We formally define a robust Lyapunov barrier function and specify sufficient conditions based on Lipschitz continuity that ensure robustness against bounded perturbations. We propose practical training objectives that enforce these conditions via adversarial training, Lipschitz neighborhood bound, and global Lipschitz regularization. We validate our approach in two practically relevant environments, Inverted Pendulum and 2D Docking. The former is a widely studied benchmark, while the latter is a safety-critical task in autonomous systems. We show that our methods significantly improve both certified robustness bounds (up to $4.6$ times) and empirical success rates under strong perturbations (up to $2.4$ times) compared to the baseline. Our results demonstrate effectiveness of training robust neural certificates for safe RL under perturbations in dynamics.

AI.tldr: This work develops robust neural Lyapunov-barrier certificates for safe RL that remain valid under bounded dynamics perturbations; it introduces Lipschitz-based robustness conditions and training objectives (adversarial training, Lipschitz neighborhood bounds, global Lipschitz regularization) and demonstrates substantial improvements in certified robustness and empirical success under perturbations on Inverted Pendulum and 2D Docking.
AI.motivation: Guaranteeing safety and stability of deep RL controllers under model uncertainty and real-world dynamics deviations, where existing certificates fail under perturbations.
AI.method: Formulate a robust Lyapunov-barrier function and derive sufficient conditions based on Lipschitz continuity to ensure robustness to bounded dynamics perturbations; implement practical training objectives including adversarial training, Lipschitz neighborhood bound, and global Lipschitz regularization to enforce these conditions during neural certificate training.
AI.result: The approach achieves substantial improvements in robustness: certified robustness bounds improve up to 4.6x and empirical success rates under strong perturbations improve up to 2.4x relative to a baseline, demonstrated in Inverted Pendulum and 2D Docking tasks.
AI.conclusion: Robust neural certificates are effective for maintaining safety and stability guarantees in RL under dynamics perturbations; the proposed training objectives enable practical enforcement of Lipschitz-based robustness guarantees, advancing safe RL in uncertain environments.

title: Day-Ahead Electricity Price Forecasting for Volatile Markets Using Foundation Models with Regularization Strategy
comment: Accepted to AI4TS Workshop @ AAAI'26 (Oral and Poster), see https://ai4ts.github.io/aaai2026
summary: Electricity price forecasting (EPF) is essential for energy markets stakeholders (e.g. grid operators, energy traders, policymakers) but remains challenging due to the inherent volatility and nonlinearity of price signals. Traditional statistical and deep learning (DL) models often struggle to capture complex temporal dependencies and integrate heterogeneous data effectively. While time series foundation models (TSFMs) have shown strong performance in general time series forecasting tasks, such as traffic forecasting and weather forecasting. However, their effectiveness in day-ahead EPF, particularly in volatile markets, remains underexplored. This paper presents a spike regularization strategy and evaluates a wide range of TSFMs, including Tiny Time Mixers (TTMs), MOIRAI, MOMENT, and TimesFM, against traditional statistical and DL models such as Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA), Long-short Term Memory (LSTM), and Convolutional Neural Network - LSTM (CNN-LSTM) using half-hourly wholesale market data with volatile trends in Singapore. Exogenous factors (e.g. weather and calendar variables) are also incorporated into models where applicable. Results demonstrate that TSFMs consistently outperform traditional approaches, achieving up to 37.4% improvement in MAPE across various evaluation settings. The findings offer practical guidance for improving forecast accuracy and decision-making in volatile electricity markets.

AI.tldr: TSFMs outperform traditional methods for day-ahead electricity price forecasting in volatile markets; spike regularization and incorporation of exogenous factors improve accuracy, with up to 37.4% MAPE improvement on Singapore half-hourly data.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of volatile, nonlinear electricity prices and the potential of time series foundation models (TSFMs) to capture complex dependencies and integrate heterogeneous data, specifically for day-ahead EPF where TSFM evidence is limited.
AI.method: Evaluate multiple TSFMs (TTMs, MOIRAI, MOMENT, TimesFM) with a spike regularization strategy, against traditional models (ARIMA, LSTM, CNN-LSTM) using half-hourly wholesale market data from Singapore. Include exogenous variables (weather, calendar) where applicable.
AI.result: TSFMs consistently outperform traditional approaches, achieving up to 37.4% improvement in MAPE across various settings.
AI.conclusion: TSFMs offer practical gains for volatile EPF; spike regularization plus exogenous data integration enhances forecast accuracy, providing guidance for practitioners in volatile electricity markets.

title: Private PoEtry: Private In-Context Learning via Product of Experts
summary: In-context learning (ICL) enables Large Language Models (LLMs) to adapt to new tasks with only a small set of examples at inference time, thereby avoiding task-specific fine-tuning. However, in-context examples may contain privacy-sensitive information that should not be revealed through model outputs. Existing differential privacy (DP) approaches to ICL are either computationally expensive or rely on heuristics with limited effectiveness, including context oversampling, synthetic data generation, or unnecessary thresholding. We reformulate private ICL through the lens of a Product-of-Experts model. This gives a theoretically grounded framework, and the algorithm can be trivially parallelized. We evaluate our method across five datasets in text classification, math, and vision-language. We find that our method improves accuracy by more than 30 percentage points on average compared to prior DP-ICL methods, while maintaining strong privacy guarantees.

AI.tldr: Product-of-Experts framework for private in-context learning (DP-ICL) yielding large accuracy gains with strong privacy and parallelism.
AI.motivation: In-context learning raises privacy concerns since in-context examples may reveal sensitive information. Existing DP approaches to ICL are expensive or heuristic with limited effectiveness.
AI.method: Reformulate private ICL as a Product-of-Experts model, providing a theoretically grounded framework and a trivially parallelizable algorithm.
AI.result: Evaluated on five datasets spanning text classification, math, and vision-language; achieved >30 percentage points average accuracy improvement over prior DP-ICL methods, while maintaining strong privacy guarantees.
AI.conclusion: POE-based private ICL is theoretically grounded, scalable, and effective across tasks, enabling practical, privacy-preserving ICL.

title: Explainable Pathomics Feature Visualization via Correlation-aware Conditional Feature Editing
summary: Pathomics is a recent approach that offers rich quantitative features beyond what black-box deep learning can provide, supporting more reproducible and explainable biomarkers in digital pathology. However, many derived features (e.g., "second-order moment") remain difficult to interpret, especially across different clinical contexts, which limits their practical adoption. Conditional diffusion models show promise for explainability through feature editing, but they typically assume feature independence**--**an assumption violated by intrinsically correlated pathomics features. Consequently, editing one feature while fixing others can push the model off the biological manifold and produce unrealistic artifacts. To address this, we propose a Manifold-Aware Diffusion (MAD) framework for controllable and biologically plausible cell nuclei editing. Unlike existing approaches, our method regularizes feature trajectories within a disentangled latent space learned by a variational auto-encoder (VAE). This ensures that manipulating a target feature automatically adjusts correlated attributes to remain within the learned distribution of real cells. These optimized features then guide a conditional diffusion model to synthesize high-fidelity images. Experiments demonstrate that our approach is able to navigate the manifold of pathomics features when editing those features. The proposed method outperforms baseline methods in conditional feature editing while preserving structural coherence.

AI.tldr: MAD: a manifold-aware diffusion framework for controllable editing of pathomics features via a VAE-disentangled latent space, ensuring correlated attributes stay within the real-cell distribution and enabling high-fidelity image synthesis.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge that feature-editing via conditional diffusion assumes feature independence, which is violated by correlated pathomics features; aim to achieve biologically plausible edits and better interpretability.
AI.method: Introduce Manifold-Aware Diffusion (MAD) that regularizes feature trajectories in a disentangled latent space learned by a variational auto-encoder (VAE). Editing a target feature automatically adjusts correlated attributes, keeping edits on the learned biological manifold, and guiding a conditional diffusion model to synthesize high-fidelity images.
AI.result: MAD enables navigation of the pathomics feature manifold during editing, outperforming baselines in conditional feature editing while preserving structural coherence of the generated images.
AI.conclusion: MAD provides a biologically plausible, controllable editing framework for pathomics features, improving explainability and practical applicability of feature-guided image synthesis.

title: MerNav: A Highly Generalizable Memory-Execute-Review Framework for Zero-Shot Object Goal Navigation
summary: Visual Language Navigation (VLN) is one of the fundamental capabilities for embodied intelligence and a critical challenge that urgently needs to be addressed. However, existing methods are still unsatisfactory in terms of both success rate (SR) and generalization: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) approaches typically achieve higher SR, while Training-Free (TF) approaches often generalize better, but it is difficult to obtain both simultaneously. To this end, we propose a Memory-Execute-Review framework. It consists of three parts: a hierarchical memory module for providing information support, an execute module for routine decision-making and actions, and a review module for handling abnormal situations and correcting behavior. We validated the effectiveness of this framework on the Object Goal Navigation task. Across 4 datasets, our average SR achieved absolute improvements of 7% and 5% compared to all baseline methods under TF and Zero-Shot (ZS) settings, respectively. On the most commonly used HM3D_v0.1 and the more challenging open vocabulary dataset HM3D_OVON, the SR improved by 8% and 6%, under ZS settings. Furthermore, on the MP3D and HM3D_OVON datasets, our method not only outperformed all TF methods but also surpassed all SFT methods, achieving comprehensive leadership in both SR (5% and 2%) and generalization.

AI.tldr: A Memory-Execute-Review framework for Visual Language Navigation (VLN) that combines a hierarchical memory module, an execute module, and a review module to improve success rate (SR) and generalization. The approach targets the trade-off between supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and training-free (TF) methods by achieving strong SR while maintaining generalization, validated on Object Goal Navigation across 4 datasets with consistent SR gains and state-of-the-art performance on several benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The field struggles to simultaneously maximize success rate and generalization in VLN. SFT methods achieve higher SR but poor generalization, while TF methods generalize better but lag in SR. A framework that integrates memory, decision execution, and review could balance these aspects.
AI.method: Proposes a three-component framework: (1) hierarchical memory module providing information support, (2) execute module for routine decision-making and actions, (3) review module for handling abnormal situations and correcting behavior. Validation on Object Goal Navigation across four datasets, comparing TF, ZS, and SFT baselines.
AI.result: Across four datasets, average SR improved by 7% (TF) and 5% (ZS) over baselines. On HM3D_v0.1 and HM3D_OVON, SR improvements of 8% and 6% under ZS. On MP3D and HM3D_OVON, the method surpasses all TF methods and even all SFT methods, indicating leadership in SR (5% and 2%) and generalization.
AI.conclusion: The Memory-Execute-Review framework effectively improves both SR and generalization in VLN. The hierarchical memory, routine execution, and review processes collectively address failure modes and abnormal situations, yielding strong empirical gains across multiple datasets and settings, and suggesting a generalizable architecture for embodied navigation tasks.

title: Refine and Purify: Orthogonal Basis Optimization with Null-Space Denoising for Conditional Representation Learning
summary: Conditional representation learning aims to extract criterion-specific features for customized tasks. Recent studies project universal features onto the conditional feature subspace spanned by an LLM-generated text basis to obtain conditional representations. However, such methods face two key limitations: sensitivity to subspace basis and vulnerability to inter-subspace interference. To address these challenges, we propose OD-CRL, a novel framework integrating Adaptive Orthogonal Basis Optimization (AOBO) and Null-Space Denoising Projection (NSDP). Specifically, AOBO constructs orthogonal semantic bases via singular value decomposition with a curvature-based truncation. NSDP suppresses non-target semantic interference by projecting embeddings onto the null space of irrelevant subspaces. Extensive experiments conducted across customized clustering, customized classification, and customized retrieval tasks demonstrate that OD-CRL achieves a new state-of-the-art performance with superior generalization.

AI.tldr: OD-CRL jointly learns robust conditional representations by adaptive orthogonal basis optimization and null-space denoising projection, achieving state-of-the-art on customized tasks.
AI.motivation: To address sensitivity to subspace basis selection and inter-subspace interference in conditional representation learning, aiming for improved generalization across downstream tasks.
AI.method: Adaptive Orthogonal Basis Optimization (AOBO) constructs orthogonal semantic bases via singular value decomposition with curvature-based truncation to select informative components; Null-Space Denoising Projection (NSDP) projects embeddings onto the null space of irrelevant subspaces to suppress non-target interference.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on customized clustering, customization classification, and customized retrieval show state-of-the-art performance and stronger generalization.
AI.conclusion: OD-CRL provides a robust conditional representation learning framework by integrating adaptive basis optimization and null-space denoising, yielding improved robustness and task performance.

title: A Simple Reduction Scheme for Constrained Contextual Bandits with Adversarial Contexts via Regression
summary: We study constrained contextual bandits (CCB) with adversarially chosen contexts, where each action yields a random reward and incurs a random cost. We adopt the standard realizability assumption: conditioned on the observed context, rewards and costs are drawn independently from fixed distributions whose expectations belong to known function classes. We consider the continuing setting, in which the algorithm operates over the entire horizon even after the budget is exhausted. In this setting, the objective is to simultaneously control regret and cumulative constraint violation. Building on the seminal SquareCB framework of Foster et al. (2018), we propose a simple and modular algorithmic scheme that leverages online regression oracles to reduce the constrained problem to a standard unconstrained contextual bandit problem with adaptively defined surrogate reward functions. In contrast to most prior work on CCB, which focuses on stochastic contexts, our reduction yields improved guarantees for the more general adversarial context setting, together with a compact and transparent analysis.

AI.tldr: A reduction-based, modular algorithm for constrained contextual bandits with adversarial contexts, reducing to an unconstrained contextual bandit via adaptively defined surrogate rewards using online regression oracles; builds on SquareCB; provides improved guarantees and transparent analysis in the continuing setting.
AI.motivation: Motivated by the need to handle adversarial contexts in constrained contextual bandits while balancing regret and cumulative constraint violation, and to extend beyond the stochastic-context focus of prior work with a simple, modular, and analyzable approach.
AI.method: Builds on the SquareCB framework and employs online regression oracles to construct adaptive surrogate reward functions, enabling a reduction from constrained to unconstrained contextual bandits. The algorithm operates in the continuing setting and uses the surrogate rewards to guide action selection.
AI.result: Proposes a simple modular scheme that yields improved guarantees for adversarial-context CCB and offers a compact, transparent analysis.
AI.conclusion: The reduction provides a practical, scalable framework for constrained contextual bandits under adversarial contexts, extending SquareCB and delivering stronger theoretical guarantees with a clear analytic approach.

title: TSBOW: Traffic Surveillance Benchmark for Occluded Vehicles Under Various Weather Conditions
comment: This paper has been accepted by the 40th AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence (AAAI-26)
summary: Global warming has intensified the frequency and severity of extreme weather events, which degrade CCTV signal and video quality while disrupting traffic flow, thereby increasing traffic accident rates. Existing datasets, often limited to light haze, rain, and snow, fail to capture extreme weather conditions. To address this gap, this study introduces the Traffic Surveillance Benchmark for Occluded vehicles under various Weather conditions (TSBOW), a comprehensive dataset designed to enhance occluded vehicle detection across diverse annual weather scenarios. Comprising over 32 hours of real-world traffic data from densely populated urban areas, TSBOW includes more than 48,000 manually annotated and 3.2 million semi-labeled frames; bounding boxes spanning eight traffic participant classes from large vehicles to micromobility devices and pedestrians. We establish an object detection benchmark for TSBOW, highlighting challenges posed by occlusions and adverse weather. With its varied road types, scales, and viewpoints, TSBOW serves as a critical resource for advancing Intelligent Transportation Systems. Our findings underscore the potential of CCTV-based traffic monitoring, pave the way for new research and applications. The TSBOW dataset is publicly available at: https://github.com/SKKUAutoLab/TSBOW.

AI.tldr: Introduces TSBOW, a large-scale CCTV-based dataset for occluded vehicle detection under diverse weather conditions, plus a benchmark to evaluate occlusion/weather robustness.
AI.motivation: Address gap: existing datasets cover light haze, rain, and snow; need data representing extreme weather and occlusions to improve Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS).
AI.method: Collected 32 hours of real-world urban traffic data; 48,000 manually annotated frames and 3.2 million semi-labeled frames; eight traffic participant classes; established an object-detection benchmark; varied road types, scales, and viewpoints.
AI.result: Established a benchmark; highlighted challenges posed by occlusions and adverse weather; dataset publicly available for research and applications in CCTV-based traffic monitoring.
AI.conclusion: TSBOW is a valuable resource for advancing Intelligent Transportation Systems and occluded-vehicle detection under varied weather; enables new research directions and practical applications.

title: IndustryShapes: An RGB-D Benchmark dataset for 6D object pose estimation of industrial assembly components and tools
summary: We introduce IndustryShapes, a new RGB-D benchmark dataset of industrial tools and components, designed for both instance-level and novel object 6D pose estimation approaches. The dataset provides a realistic and application-relevant testbed for benchmarking these methods in the context of industrial robotics bridging the gap between lab-based research and deployment in real-world manufacturing scenarios. Unlike many previous datasets that focus on household or consumer products or use synthetic, clean tabletop datasets, or objects captured solely in controlled lab environments, IndustryShapes introduces five new object types with challenging properties, also captured in realistic industrial assembly settings. The dataset has diverse complexity, from simple to more challenging scenes, with single and multiple objects, including scenes with multiple instances of the same object and it is organized in two parts: the classic set and the extended set. The classic set includes a total of 4,6k images and 6k annotated poses. The extended set introduces additional data modalities to support the evaluation of model-free and sequence-based approaches. To the best of our knowledge, IndustryShapes is the first dataset to offer RGB-D static onboarding sequences. We further evaluate the dataset on a representative set of state-of-the art methods for instance-based and novel object 6D pose estimation, including also object detection, segmentation, showing that there is room for improvement in this domain. The dataset page can be found in https://pose-lab.github.io/IndustryShapes.

AI.tldr: IndustryShapes is a new RGB-D benchmark for industrial tools and components, enabling instance-level and novel-object 6D pose estimation. It is organized in two parts: classic (4.6k images, 6k annotated poses) and extended (additional modalities). Five new object types are introduced, captured in realistic industrial assembly settings. Includes RGB-D onboarding sequences and baseline evaluations across state-of-the-art methods, highlighting room for improvement.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between lab-based datasets and real-world industrial deployment by providing a realistic, application-relevant benchmark for 6D pose estimation, object detection, and segmentation in industrial robotics.
AI.method: Data collection in realistic industrial environments; introduction of five challenging industrial object types; two-set dataset (classic and extended) with increasing modalities; extended set adds support for model-free and sequence-based approaches and includes RGB-D onboarding sequences; baseline evaluation using representative state-of-the-art methods across instance-based and novel-object 6D pose estimation tasks.
AI.result: IndustryShapes provides a realistic benchmarking platform with diverse scenes (single/multiple objects, multiple instances) and two data parts, enabling evaluation of both instance-level and novel-object pose estimation, as well as detection and segmentation. Baseline experiments indicate there is room for improvement in this domain.
AI.conclusion: IndustryShapes advances benchmarking for industrial robotics by offering a realistic, multi-modality RGB-D dataset with onboarding sequences, facilitating the transition from lab research to real-world deployment. The dataset page is provided for access.

title: ALIVE: Awakening LLM Reasoning via Adversarial Learning and Instructive Verbal Evaluation
summary: The quest for expert-level reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs) has been hampered by a persistent \textit{reward bottleneck}: traditional reinforcement learning (RL) relies on scalar rewards that are \textbf{costly} to scale, \textbf{brittle} across domains, and \textbf{blind} to the underlying logic of a solution. This reliance on external, impoverished signals prevents models from developing a deep, self-contained understanding of reasoning principles. We introduce \textbf{ALIVE} (\emph{Adversarial Learning with Instructive Verbal Evaluation}), a hands-free alignment framework that moves beyond scalar reward optimization toward intrinsic reasoning acquisition. Grounded in the principle of \emph{Cognitive Synergy}, ALIVE unifies problem posing, solving, and judging within a single policy model to internalize the logic of correctness. By coupling adversarial learning with instructive verbal feedback, ALIVE enables models to internalize evaluative criteria directly from raw corpora, effectively transforming external critiques into an endogenous reasoning faculty. Empirical evaluations across mathematical reasoning, code generation, and general logical inference benchmarks demonstrate that ALIVE consistently mitigates reward signal limitations. With identical data and compute, it achieves accuracy gains, markedly improved cross-domain generalization, and higher self-correction rates. These results indicate that the reasoning trinity fosters a self-sustaining trajectory of capability growth, positioning ALIVE as a scalable foundation for general-purpose reasoning alignment without human-in-the-loop supervision.

AI.tldr: ALIVE replaces scalar RL rewards with an intrinsic, self-contained reasoning framework that unifies posing, solving, and judging under a single policy, leveraging adversarial learning and instructive verbal feedback to internalize correctness criteria from raw data.
AI.motivation: Overcome the reward bottleneck in RL-based reasoning where external scalar signals are costly, brittle across domains, and blind to reasoning structure; enable self-contained improvement of reasoning capabilities without heavy human supervision.
AI.method: Introduce ALIVE (Adversarial Learning with Instructive Verbal Evaluation), a hands-free alignment approach that couples adversarial learning with instructive verbal feedback. It unifies problem posing, solving, and judging within one policy under Cognitive Synergy, training on raw corpora to internalize evaluative criteria and transform external critiques into an endogenous reasoning faculty.
AI.result: Empirically shows consistency in mitigating reward signal limitations across mathematical reasoning, code generation, and general logical inference benchmarks; with identical data/compute, achieves higher accuracy, better cross-domain generalization, and increased self-correction rates.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning trinity fosters a self-sustaining growth trajectory and provides a scalable foundation for general-purpose reasoning alignment without human-in-the-loop supervision.

title: Laws of Learning Dynamics and the Core of Learners
summary: We formulate the fundamental laws governing learning dynamics, namely the conservation law and the decrease of total entropy. Within this framework, we introduce an entropy-based lifelong ensemble learning method. We evaluate its effectiveness by constructing an immunization mechanism to defend against transfer-based adversarial attacks on the CIFAR-10 dataset. Compared with a naive ensemble formed by simply averaging models specialized on clean and adversarial samples, the resulting logifold achieves higher accuracy in most test cases, with particularly large gains under strong perturbations.

AI.tldr: Entropy-based lifelong ensemble learning with an immunization mechanism against transfer-based adversarial attacks on CIFAR-10; improves over naive ensembles, especially under strong perturbations.
AI.motivation: Address learning dynamics and robustness by grounding in conservation and entropy decrease; extend ensemble methods to lifelong learning and defend against transfer-based attacks.
AI.method: Formulate fundamental laws (conservation of learning dynamics, decrease of total entropy). Develop entropy-based lifelong ensemble learning.
