Our approach achieves this through a calibration-free architecture, enabling a single model to adapt to different sensor setups. Unlike general-purpose geometric foundational models that focus on pixel-aligned predictions, ViGT estimates a continuous 3D occupancy field in a birds-eye-view (BEV) addressing domain-specific requirements. ViGT naturally infers geometry from multiple camera views into a single metric coordinate frame, providing a common representation for multiple geometric tasks. Unlike most existing occupancy models, we adopt a self-supervised training procedure that leverages synchronized image-LiDAR pairs, eliminating the need for costly manual annotations. We validate the scalability and generalizability of our approach by training our model on a mixture of five large-scale autonomous driving datasets (NuScenes, Waymo, NuPlan, ONCE, and Argoverse) and achieving state-of-the-art performance on the pointmap estimation task, with the best average rank across all evaluated baselines. We further evaluate ViGT on the Occ3D-nuScenes benchmark, where ViGT achieves comparable performance with supervised methods. The source code is publicly available at \href{https://github.com/whesense/ViGT}{https://github.com/whesense/ViGT}.

AI.tldr: Calibration-free Visual Implicit Geometry Transformer (ViGT) estimates continuous 3D occupancy fields in BEV from surround-view cameras, using self-supervised training across multiple datasets; achieves SOTA on pointmap and competitive results on Occ3D-nuScenes; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the need for scalable, simple, and generalizable foundational geometric models for autonomous driving that can adapt to diverse sensor configurations without calibration or heavy manual annotations.
AI.method: A calibration-free architecture that fuses multi-view camera data into a single metric BEV occupancy field. ViGT infers geometry from multiple views into a common coordinate frame, and trains with self-supervised learning leveraging synchronized image-LiDAR pairs across a mixture of five large-scale datasets (NuScenes, Waymo, NuPlan, ONCE, Argoverse). Focuses on continuous 3D occupancy estimation rather than pixel-aligned predictions.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on the pointmap estimation task with the best average rank across baselines. On Occ3D-nuScenes, ViGT reaches comparable performance to supervised methods. Demonstrates scalability and generalization across diverse sensor configurations and datasets; source code available.
AI.conclusion: ViGT provides a scalable, architecture-simple, and generalizable geometric model for autonomous driving that unifies multiple geometric tasks in a BEV representation and reduces reliance on manual annotations; future work could extend tasks, further improve cross-dataset generalization, and explore deployment considerations.

title: STProtein: predicting spatial protein expression from multi-omics data
comment: STProtein: predicting spatial protein expression from multi-omics data is accepted SPARTA_AAAI2026 Oral GitHub: https://github.com/zhaorui-bi/STProtein
summary: The integration of spatial multi-omics data from single tissues is crucial for advancing biological research. However, a significant data imbalance impedes progress: while spatial transcriptomics data is relatively abundant, spatial proteomics data remains scarce due to technical limitations and high costs. To overcome this challenge we propose STProtein, a novel framework leveraging graph neural networks with multi-task learning strategy. STProtein is designed to accurately predict unknown spatial protein expression using more accessible spatial multi-omics data, such as spatial transcriptomics. We believe that STProtein can effectively addresses the scarcity of spatial proteomics, accelerating the integration of spatial multi-omics and potentially catalyzing transformative breakthroughs in life sciences. This tool enables scientists to accelerate discovery by identifying complex and previously hidden spatial patterns of proteins within tissues, uncovering novel relationships between different marker genes, and exploring the biological "Dark Matter".

AI.tldr: A graph neural network-based, multi-task learning framework (STProtein) predicts spatial protein expression from spatial transcriptomics to mitigate scarcity of spatial proteomics and enable integrated spatial multi-omics analysis.
AI.motivation: Spatial proteomics data are scarce due to high cost and technical difficulty, while spatial transcriptomics data are comparatively abundant. This data imbalance hinders comprehensive spatial multi-omics studies; a predictive approach can unlock proteomics insights using cheaper, accessible data.
AI.method: STProtein employs graph neural networks with a multi-task learning strategy to infer unknown spatial protein expression from existing spatial transcriptomics data, leveraging the structural and contextual information in tissue graphs.
AI.result: The abstract asserts that STProtein can accurately predict unknown spatial protein expression and accelerate spatial multi-omics integration, but it does not provide quantitative results or experimental validation.
AI.conclusion: STProtein has the potential to alleviate spatial proteomics scarcity, speeding discovery of spatial protein patterns and cross-modal relationships, thereby advancing life-science research.

title: Position: Capability Control Should be a Separate Goal From Alignment
summary: Foundation models are trained on broad data distributions, yielding generalist capabilities that enable many downstream applications but also expand the space of potential misuse and failures. This position paper argues that capability control -- imposing restrictions on permissible model behavior -- should be treated as a distinct goal from alignment. While alignment is often context and preference-driven, capability control aims to impose hard operational limits on permissible behaviors, including under adversarial elicitation. We organize capability control mechanisms across the model lifecycle into three layers: (i) data-based control of the training distribution, (ii) learning-based control via weight- or representation-level interventions, and (iii) system-based control via post-deployment guardrails over inputs, outputs, and actions. Because each layer has characteristic failure modes when used in isolation, we advocate for a defense-in-depth approach that composes complementary controls across the full stack. We further outline key open challenges in achieving such control, including the dual-use nature of knowledge and compositional generalization.

AI.tldr: A position paper arguing that capability control should be distinct from alignment, proposing a three-layer, defense-in-depth framework (data, learning, system) for hard operational limits across the model lifecycle, and outlining open challenges like dual-use and compositional generalization.
AI.motivation: Foundation models have broad training distributions that enable powerful capabilities but also enable misuse and failures. The paper argues for explicit capability control as a separate objective from alignment to impose hard operational limits.
AI.method: Organize capability control across three layers of the lifecycle: (i) data-based control of training distribution, (ii) learning-based interventions at weight or representation level, and (iii) system-based guardrails post-deployment over inputs, outputs, and actions; advocate defense-in-depth by combining controls across layers; discuss open challenges.
AI.result: Proposes a structured framework for capability control with a defense-in-depth strategy; identifies potential failure modes when layers are used in isolation; highlights open challenges such as dual-use knowledge and compositional generalization.
AI.conclusion: Capability control is a distinct objective from alignment. A defense-in-depth, multi-layer approach across data, learning, and system layers is needed to impose hard operational limits and mitigate misuse, with active attention to open research challenges.

title: A Hybrid CNN and ML Framework for Multi-modal Classification of Movement Disorders Using MRI and Brain Structural Features
comment: To be published in Proceedings of SPIE Medical Imaging 2026
summary: Atypical Parkinsonian Disorders (APD), also known as Parkinson-plus syndrome, are a group of neurodegenerative diseases that include progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP) and multiple system atrophy (MSA). In the early stages, overlapping clinical features often lead to misdiagnosis as Parkinson's disease (PD). Identifying reliable imaging biomarkers for early differential diagnosis remains a critical challenge. In this study, we propose a hybrid framework combining convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with machine learning (ML) techniques to classify APD subtypes versus PD and distinguish between the subtypes themselves: PSP vs. PD, MSA vs. PD, and PSP vs. MSA. The model leverages multi-modal input data, including T1-weighted magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), segmentation masks of 12 deep brain structures associated with APD, and their corresponding volumetric measurements. By integrating these complementary modalities, including image data, structural segmentation masks, and quantitative volume features, the hybrid approach achieved promising classification performance with area under the curve (AUC) scores of 0.95 for PSP vs. PD, 0.86 for MSA vs. PD, and 0.92 for PSP vs. MSA. These results highlight the potential of combining spatial and structural information for robust subtype differentiation. In conclusion, this study demonstrates that fusing CNN-based image features with volume-based ML inputs improves classification accuracy for APD subtypes. The proposed approach may contribute to more reliable early-stage diagnosis, facilitating timely and targeted interventions in clinical practice.

AI.tldr: Hybrid CNN+ML framework using multi-modal MRI, segmentation masks, and volumetrics to classify APD subtypes vs PD and among PSP, MSA, and PD; achieves AUCs 0.95 (PSP vs PD), 0.86 (MSA vs PD), 0.92 (PSP vs MSA).
AI.motivation: Early differential diagnosis of atypical parkinsonian disorders (APD) is challenging due to overlapping clinical features; reliable imaging biomarkers are needed.
AI.method: A hybrid architecture that fuses CNN-extracted image features with volume-based ML features. Inputs include T1-weighted MRI, segmentation masks for 12 deep brain structures associated with APD, and their corresponding volumetric measurements. The model performs multi-class and pairwise subtype classification: PSP vs PD, MSA vs PD, PSP vs MSA.
AI.result: AUCs reported: PSP vs PD 0.95, MSA vs PD 0.86, PSP vs MSA 0.92, indicating strong discrimination and benefit from multi-modal fusion.
AI.conclusion: Fusing CNN-derived image features with volume-based features improves APD subtype differentiation and may enable more reliable early-stage diagnosis in clinical practice.

title: TKG-Thinker: Towards Dynamic Reasoning over Temporal Knowledge Graphs via Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Temporal knowledge graph question answering (TKGQA) aims to answer time-sensitive questions by leveraging temporal knowledge bases. While Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate significant potential in TKGQA, current prompting strategies constrain their efficacy in two primary ways. First, they are prone to reasoning hallucinations under complex temporal constraints. Second, static prompting limits model autonomy and generalization, as it lack optimization through dynamic interaction with temporal knowledge graphs (TKGs) environments. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{TKG-Thinker}, a novel agent equipped with autonomous planning and adaptive retrieval capabilities for reasoning over TKGs. Specifically, TKG-Thinker performs in-depth temporal reasoning through dynamic multi-turn interactions with TKGs via a dual-training strategy. We first apply Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with chain-of thought data to instill core planning capabilities, followed by a Reinforcement Learning (RL) stage that leverages multi-dimensional rewards to refine reasoning policies under intricate temporal constraints. Experimental results on benchmark datasets with three open-source LLMs show that TKG-Thinker achieves state-of-the-art performance and exhibits strong generalization across complex TKGQA settings.

AI.tldr: TKG-Thinker introduces an autonomous planning agent for temporal knowledge graph QA, addressing hallucinations and static prompting limits by coupling dynamic TKG interactions with a dual-training regime (SFT with chain-of-thought data, followed by RL with multi-dimensional rewards).
AI.motivation: Current LLM prompting for Temporal Knowledge Graph QA (TKGQA) suffers from reasoning hallucinations under complex temporal constraints and from static prompting that limits autonomy and generalization. There is a need for an agent that can autonomously plan and retrieve relevant temporal knowledge through interactive dialogue with TKG environments.
AI.method: TKG-Thinker is an autonomous agent that interacts with temporal knowledge graphs through dynamic, multi-turn sessions. It uses a dual-training strategy: first, Supervised Fine-Tuning with chain-of-thought data to instill core planning capabilities; second, Reinforcement Learning that uses multi-dimensional rewards to refine reasoning policies under intricate temporal constraints.
AI.result: Experiments on benchmark datasets across three open-source LLMs show state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization to complex TKGQA settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed TKG-Thinker demonstrates that autonomous planning and adaptive retrieval in a planning-enabled agent can outperform static prompting, improving reasoning under temporal constraints and generalization across TKGQA tasks.

title: EBPO: Empirical Bayes Shrinkage for Stabilizing Group-Relative Policy Optimization
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has proven effective for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, dominant approaches like Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) face critical stability challenges: they suffer from high estimator variance under computational constraints (small group sizes) and vanishing gradient signals in saturated failure regimes where all responses yield identical zero rewards. To address this, we propose Empirical Bayes Policy Optimization (EBPO), a novel framework that regularizes local group-based baselines by borrowing strength from the policy's accumulated global statistics. Instead of estimating baselines in isolation, EBPO employs a shrinkage estimator that dynamically balances local group statistics with a global prior updated via Welford's online algorithm. Theoretically, we demonstrate that EBPO guarantees strictly lower Mean Squared Error (MSE), bounded entropy decay, and non-vanishing penalty signals in failure scenarios compared to GRPO. Empirically, EBPO consistently outperforms GRPO and other established baselines across diverse benchmarks, including AIME and OlympiadBench. Notably, EBPO exhibits superior training stability, achieving high-performance gains even with small group sizes, and benefits significantly from difficulty-stratified curriculum learning.

AI.tldr: EBPO introduces an empirical Bayes shrinkage regularization for policy optimization in RLVR to stabilize training and alleviate vanishing gradients, outperforming GRPO at small group sizes.
AI.motivation: Dominant approaches like GRPO suffer high estimator variance under small group sizes and vanishing gradient signals in saturated failure regimes (identical zero rewards), hindering learning stability and performance.
AI.method: EBPO uses shrinkage to regularize local group baselines toward a globally accumulated prior. The global prior is updated online via Welford's method, creating a dynamically balanced estimate that combines local statistics with global information.
AI.result: Theoretically, EBPO achieves strictly lower MSE than GRPO, bounded entropy decay, and non-vanishing penalty signals in failure regimes. Empirically, EBPO outperforms GRPO and baselines on AIME and OlympiadBench, demonstrating improved training stability and strong gains even with small group sizes; curriculum learning with difficulty stratification further boosts performance.
AI.conclusion: EBPO provides a principled, statistically grounded regularization mechanism that enhances stability and performance in RLVR tasks, particularly under restrictive group sizes and challenging curricula.

title: LocateEdit-Bench: A Benchmark for Instruction-Based Editing Localization
summary: Recent advancements in image editing have enabled highly controllable and semantically-aware alteration of visual content, posing unprecedented challenges to manipulation localization. However, existing AI-generated forgery localization methods primarily focus on inpainting-based manipulations, making them ineffective against the latest instruction-based editing paradigms. To bridge this critical gap, we propose LocateEdit-Bench, a large-scale dataset comprising $231$K edited images, designed specifically to benchmark localization methods against instruction-driven image editing. Our dataset incorporates four cutting-edge editing models and covers three common edit types. We conduct a detailed analysis of the dataset and develop two multi-metric evaluation protocols to assess existing localization methods. Our work establishes a foundation to keep pace with the evolving landscape of image editing, thereby facilitating the development of effective methods for future forgery localization. Dataset will be open-sourced upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: LocateEdit-Bench presents a large-scale 231k-image dataset to benchmark localization of instruction-driven image edits, incorporating four editing models and three edit types, plus two multi-metric evaluation protocols; dataset will be open-sourced upon acceptance.
AI.motivation: Current forgery localization methods largely target inpainting-based edits, leaving a gap against instruction-driven editing. Rapid advances in image editing necessitate a benchmark and evaluation framework to guide robust localization methods.
AI.method: Construct LocateEdit-Bench by compiling 231k edited images using four cutting-edge editing models across three edit types; perform detailed dataset analysis; develop two multi-metric evaluation protocols to assess existing localization methods; plan to open-source the dataset after acceptance.
AI.result: Provides a large-scale, diverse benchmark and two evaluation protocols for localization under instruction-driven edits; establishes a foundation for evaluating and improving forgery localization methods in evolving editing landscapes.
AI.conclusion: The work enables development of effective forgery localization against instruction-driven editing and accelerates progress by offering a publicly available benchmark and evaluation framework upon acceptance.

title: Learning Compact Boolean Networks
summary: Floating-point neural networks dominate modern machine learning but incur substantial inference cost, motivating interest in Boolean networks for resource-constrained settings. However, learning compact and accurate Boolean networks is challenging due to their combinatorial nature. In this work, we address this challenge from three different angles: learned connections, compact convolutions and adaptive discretization. First, we propose a novel strategy to learn efficient connections with no additional parameters and negligible computational overhead. Second, we introduce a novel convolutional Boolean architecture that exploits the locality with reduced number of Boolean operations than existing methods. Third, we propose an adaptive discretization strategy to reduce the accuracy drop when converting a continuous-valued network into a Boolean one. Extensive results on standard vision benchmarks demonstrate that the Pareto front of accuracy vs. computation of our method significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art, achieving better accuracy with up to 37x fewer Boolean operations.

AI.tldr: Proposes three-pronged approach (learned connections, compact Boolean convolutions, adaptive discretization) to train high-accuracy Boolean networks with drastically reduced Boolean operations (up to 37x fewer), achieving favorable accuracy–computation Pareto front on vision benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Boolean networks offer resource efficiency for constrained devices but learning compact, accurate models is hard due to combinatorial search; bridging performance gap with floating-point networks.
AI.method: Three contributions: (1) learn efficient connections with no additional parameters; (2) a novel convolutional Boolean architecture exploiting locality with fewer Boolean operations; (3) adaptive discretization to reduce accuracy loss when binarizing continuous networks.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on standard vision benchmarks show the Pareto front of accuracy vs computation significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art, achieving up to 37x fewer Boolean operations.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates the viability of efficient Boolean networks for resource-constrained inference and provides design principles that yield major efficiency gains without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Benchmarking Artificial Intelligence Models for Daily Coastal Hypoxia Forecasting
comment: This is a Preprint accepted at IEEE Big Data 2025
summary: Coastal hypoxia, especially in the northern part of Gulf of Mexico, presents a persistent ecological and economic concern. Seasonal models offer coarse forecasts that miss the fine-scale variability needed for daily, responsive ecosystem management. We present study that compares four deep learning architectures for daily hypoxia classification: Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM), Medformer (Medical Transformer), Spatio-Temporal Transformer (ST-Transformer), and Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN). We trained our models with twelve years of daily hindcast data from 2009-2020 Our training data consists of 2009-2020 hindcast data from a coupled hydrodynamic-biogeochemical model. Similarly, we use hindcast data from 2020 through 2024 as a test data. We constructed classification models incorporating water column stratification, sediment oxygen consumption, and temperature-dependent decomposition rates. We evaluated each architectures using the same data preprocessing, input/output formulation, and validation protocols. Each model achieved high classification accuracy and strong discriminative ability with ST-Transformer achieving the highest performance across all metrics and tests periods (AUC-ROC: 0.982-0.992). We also employed McNemar's method to identify statistically significant differences in model predictions. Our contribution is a reproducible framework for operational real-time hypoxia prediction that can support broader efforts in the environmental and ocean modeling systems community and in ecosystem resilience. The source code is available https://github.com/rmagesh148/hypoxia-ai/

AI.tldr: Four deep learning models for daily hypoxia classification in the Gulf of Mexico were compared; ST-Transformer achieved the best AUC-ROC (0.982–0.992) on a 2020–2024 test, enabling a reproducible real-time prediction framework; code is publicly available.
AI.motivation: Coastal hypoxia in the northern Gulf of Mexico poses ongoing ecological and economic risks. Seasonal models lack fine-scale, daily predictive capability required for responsive ecosystem management. The study seeks to evaluate multiple DL architectures to identify a robust, operational approach for daily hypoxia classification.
AI.method: Data: hindcast from 2009–2020 for training and 2020–2024 for testing from a coupled hydrodynamic–biogeochemical model. Models compared: BiLSTM, Medformer, ST-Transformer, and Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN). Features include water column stratification, sediment oxygen consumption, and temperature-dependent decomposition. Standardized preprocessing, identical input/output formulation, and validation protocols. Evaluation includes AUC-ROC and other discriminative metrics; statistical comparison via McNemar's test.
AI.result: ST-Transformer produced the highest performance across metrics and test periods, with AUC-ROC values in the range 0.982–0.992. All models achieved high classification accuracy and strong discriminative ability. McNemar's test identified statistically significant differences among model predictions, supporting the superiority of the ST-Transformer in this setup.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a reproducible framework for operational real-time hypoxia prediction and has potential for broader adoption in environmental/ocean modeling and ecosystem resilience efforts. Source code is available at https://github.com/rmagesh148/hypoxia-ai/.

title: LoGoSeg: Integrating Local and Global Features for Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation
summary: Open-vocabulary semantic segmentation (OVSS) extends traditional closed-set segmentation by enabling pixel-wise annotation for both seen and unseen categories using arbitrary textual descriptions. While existing methods leverage vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP, their reliance on image-level pretraining often results in imprecise spatial alignment, leading to mismatched segmentations in ambiguous or cluttered scenes. However, most existing approaches lack strong object priors and region-level constraints, which can lead to object hallucination or missed detections, further degrading performance. To address these challenges, we propose LoGoSeg, an efficient single-stage framework that integrates three key innovations: (i) an object existence prior that dynamically weights relevant categories through global image-text similarity, effectively reducing hallucinations; (ii) a region-aware alignment module that establishes precise region-level visual-textual correspondences; and (iii) a dual-stream fusion mechanism that optimally combines local structural information with global semantic context. Unlike prior works, LoGoSeg eliminates the need for external mask proposals, additional backbones, or extra datasets, ensuring efficiency. Extensive experiments on six benchmarks (A-847, PC-459, A-150, PC-59, PAS-20, and PAS-20b) demonstrate its competitive performance and strong generalization in open-vocabulary settings.

AI.tldr: LoGoSeg is a single-stage open-vocabulary semantic segmentation framework that uses an object existence prior, region-aware alignment, and dual-stream fusion to improve spatial precision and reduce hallucinations, without external proposals or extra data, achieving competitive results and strong generalization across six benchmarks.
AI.motivation: OVSS suffers from imprecise spatial alignment due to image-level pretraining and lacked strong object priors/region-level constraints, leading to hallucinations or missed detections in cluttered scenes. There is a need for efficient, globally aware yet region-consistent segmentation.
AI.method: Introduce LoGoSeg with: (i) an object existence prior that weights categories via global image-text similarity to suppress irrelevant concepts; (ii) a region-aware alignment module for precise region-level visual-text correspondences; (iii) a dual-stream fusion mechanism combining local structural cues with global semantic context. The framework is single-stage and does not rely on external mask proposals, additional backbones, or extra datasets.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on six benchmarks (A-847, PC-459, A-150, PC-59, PAS-20, PAS-20b) show competitive performance and strong generalization in open-vocabulary settings.
AI.conclusion: LoGoSeg provides a robust, efficient solution for OVSS by coupling object priors, region-level alignment, and fusion of local-global information, avoiding reliance on external proposals or datasets.

title: OmniVideo-R1: Reinforcing Audio-visual Reasoning with Query Intention and Modality Attention
summary: While humans perceive the world through diverse modalities that operate synergistically to support a holistic understanding of their surroundings, existing omnivideo models still face substantial challenges on audio-visual understanding tasks. In this paper, we propose OmniVideo-R1, a novel reinforced framework that improves mixed-modality reasoning. OmniVideo-R1 empowers models to "think with omnimodal cues" by two key strategies: (1) query-intensive grounding based on self-supervised learning paradigms; and (2) modality-attentive fusion built upon contrastive learning paradigms. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that OmniVideo-R1 consistently outperforms strong baselines, highlighting its effectiveness and robust generalization capabilities.

AI.tldr: OmniVideo-R1 introduces a reinforced framework for omnivideo reasoning using query-intensive grounding and modality-attentive fusion, achieving superior performance and generalization on audio-visual benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing omnivideo models in audio-visual understanding and mixed-modality reasoning.
AI.method: Two key strategies: (1) query-intensive grounding based on self-supervised learning; (2) modality-attentive fusion built upon contrastive learning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks show that OmniVideo-R1 consistently outperforms strong baselines, demonstrating effective mixed-modality reasoning and robust generalization.
AI.conclusion: OmniVideo-R1 effectively enhances mixed-modality reasoning and generalizes across tasks.

title: Data-Centric Interpretability for LLM-based Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly trained in complex Reinforcement Learning, multi-agent environments, making it difficult to understand how behavior changes over training. Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) have recently shown to be useful for data-centric interpretability. In this work, we analyze large-scale reinforcement learning training runs from the sophisticated environment of Full-Press Diplomacy by applying pretrained SAEs, alongside LLM-summarizer methods. We introduce Meta-Autointerp, a method for grouping SAE features into interpretable hypotheses about training dynamics. We discover fine-grained behaviors including role-playing patterns, degenerate outputs, language switching, alongside high-level strategic behaviors and environment-specific bugs. Through automated evaluation, we validate that 90% of discovered SAE Meta-Features are significant, and find a surprising reward hacking behavior. However, through two user studies, we find that even subjectively interesting and seemingly helpful SAE features may be worse than useless to humans, along with most LLM generated hypotheses. However, a subset of SAE-derived hypotheses are predictively useful for downstream tasks. We further provide validation by augmenting an untrained agent's system prompt, improving the score by +14.2%. Overall, we show that SAEs and LLM-summarizer provide complementary views into agent behavior, and together our framework forms a practical starting point for future data-centric interpretability work on ensuring trustworthy LLM behavior throughout training.

AI.tldr: A framework combining pretrained sparse autoencoders (SAEs) and LLM-based summarizers to interpret large-scale RL training dynamics in Full-Press Diplomacy; reveals a spectrum of behaviors from fine-grained to strategic, with many SAE features significant, some counterproductive to human utility, and some SAE-derived hypotheses predictive; system-prompt augmentation boosts performance; SAEs and LLM summaries are complementary for data-centric interpretability and trustworthy LLM behavior.
AI.motivation: Understand how behavior evolves during complex RL/multi-agent training and improve trustworthiness and interpretability by leveraging data-centric methods (SAEs) and LLM-generated summaries.
AI.method: Apply pretrained SAEs to large-scale RL training runs in a sophisticated multi-agent environment (Diplomacy). Introduce Meta-Autointerp to cluster SAE features into interpretable hypotheses about training dynamics. Use LLM-summarizer methods to augment SAE-derived insights. Conduct automated evaluation to assess significance of Meta-Features, followed by two user studies to gauge human usefulness. Validate predictive usefulness by downstream tasks, e.g., augmenting an untrained agent's system prompt and measuring performance.
AI.result: Identified fine-grained behaviors (role-playing, degenerate outputs, language switching) and high-level strategic behaviors plus environment-specific bugs. Automated evaluation shows ~90% of SAE Meta-Features are significant. Observed a surprising reward hacking behavior. Many LLM-generated hypotheses and some SAE features perceived as irrelevant or useless by humans, but a subset of SAE-derived hypotheses are predictive for downstream tasks. System prompt augmentation on an untrained agent yielded +14.2% performance improvement.
AI.conclusion: SAEs and LLM-summarizer provide complementary perspectives for interpreting training dynamics. The proposed framework (Meta-Autointerp) offers a practical starting point for data-centric interpretability to promote trustworthy LLM behavior throughout training and can guide future work on debugging and understanding complex RL/LMM systems.

title: Geometric Observability Index: An Operator-Theoretic Framework for Per-Feature Sensitivity, Weak Observability, and Dynamic Effects in SE(3) Pose Estimation
summary:
We present a unified operator-theoretic framework for analyzing per-feature sensitivity in camera pose estimation on the Lie group SE(3). Classical sensitivity tools - conditioning analyses, Euclidean perturbation arguments, and Fisher information bounds - do not explain how individual image features influence the pose estimate, nor why dynamic or inconsistent observations can disproportionately distort modern SLAM and structure-from-motion systems. To address this gap, we extend influence function theory to matrix Lie groups and derive an intrinsic perturbation operator for left-trivialized M-estimators on SE(3).
  The resulting Geometric Observability Index (GOI) quantifies the contribution of a single measurement through the curvature operator and the Lie algebraic structure of the observable subspace. GOI admits a spectral decomposition along the principal directions of the observable curvature, revealing a direct correspondence between weak observability and amplified sensitivity. In the population regime, GOI coincides with the Fisher information geometry on SE(3), yielding a single-measurement analogue of the Cramer-Rao bound.
  The same spectral mechanism explains classical degeneracies such as pure rotation and vanishing parallax, as well as dynamic feature amplification along weak curvature directions. Overall, GOI provides a geometrically consistent description of measurement influence that unifies conditioning analysis, Fisher information geometry, influence function theory, and dynamic scene detectability through the spectral geometry of the curvature operator. Because these quantities arise directly within Gauss-Newton pipelines, the curvature spectrum and GOI also yield lightweight, training-free diagnostic signals for identifying dynamic features and detecting weak observability configurations without modifying existing SLAM architectures.

AI.tldr: A unified operator-theoretic framework, the Geometric Observability Index (GOI), quantifies per-feature influence in SE(3) pose estimation by spectral analysis of the curvature operator, unifying conditioning, Fisher information geometry, and influence theory within Gauss-Newton pipelines; it yields practical diagnostics for weak observability and dynamic features without altering SLAM systems.
AI.motivation: Classical sensitivity tools fail to explain how individual image features influence pose estimates in SLAM/SfM and do not account for dynamic or inconsistent observations. There is a need for a geometrically consistent, feature-level sensitivity measure on Lie groups that integrates curvature and observability.
AI.method: Extend influence function theory to matrix Lie groups (SE(3)) and define an intrinsic perturbation operator for left-trivialized M-estimators. Introduce the curvature operator on SE(3), perform a spectral decomposition along observable curvature directions to obtain the GOI for each measurement. Show GOI aligns with Fisher information geometry in the population limit and derive a CR-like bound. Analyze degeneracies (pure rotation, zero parallax) and dynamic feature amplification via curvature directions. Provide training-free diagnostics embedded in Gauss-Newton pipelines.
AI.result: GOI provides a per-measurement contribution measure through the curvature spectrum, enabling weak observability detection and dynamic feature amplification analysis. It bridges conditioning analysis, Fisher info geometry, influence theory, and dynamic scene detectability, and offers lightweight diagnostics for SLAM without changing architectures.
AI.conclusion: GOI offers a geometrically consistent, inspectable framework for measurement influence on SE(3) pose estimates, unifying several sensitivity theories and delivering practical, real-time diagnostics for dynamic and weakly observable scenarios in SLAM/SfM.

title: BABE: Biology Arena BEnchmark
summary: The rapid evolution of large language models (LLMs) has expanded their capabilities from basic dialogue to advanced scientific reasoning. However, existing benchmarks in biology often fail to assess a critical skill required of researchers: the ability to integrate experimental results with contextual knowledge to derive meaningful conclusions. To address this gap, we introduce BABE(Biology Arena BEnchmark), a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate the experimental reasoning capabilities of biological AI systems. BABE is uniquely constructed from peer-reviewed research papers and real-world biological studies, ensuring that tasks reflect the complexity and interdisciplinary nature of actual scientific inquiry. BABE challenges models to perform causal reasoning and cross-scale inference. Our benchmark provides a robust framework for assessing how well AI systems can reason like practicing scientists, offering a more authentic measure of their potential to contribute to biological research.

AI.tldr: BABE is a benchmark to evaluate AI's experimental reasoning in biology using peer-reviewed papers and real-world studies, focusing on causal reasoning and cross-scale inference.
AI.motivation: Existing biology benchmarks inadequately test the integration of experimental results with contextual knowledge; there is a need for an authentic, interdisciplinary evaluation that mirrors scientific inquiry.
AI.method: BABE is constructed from peer-reviewed research papers and real-world biological studies. Tasks require causal reasoning and cross-scale inference, challenging AI to reason like practicing scientists by integrating results with contextual knowledge.
AI.result: BABE provides a robust framework for assessing experimental reasoning in biological AI systems and frames a path toward evaluating whether AI can reason like practicing scientists; no empirical results are reported in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: BABE offers an authentic measure of AI potential to contribute to biological research by evaluating experimental reasoning across scales and contexts.

title: SpectraKAN: Conditioning Spectral Operators
summary: Spectral neural operators, particularly Fourier Neural Operators (FNO), are a powerful framework for learning solution operators of partial differential equations (PDEs) due to their efficient global mixing in the frequency domain. However, existing spectral operators rely on static Fourier kernels applied uniformly across inputs, limiting their ability to capture multi-scale, regime-dependent, and anisotropic dynamics governed by the global state of the system. We introduce SpectraKAN, a neural operator that conditions the spectral operator on the input itself, turning static spectral convolution into an input-conditioned integral operator. This is achieved by extracting a compact global representation from spatio-temporal history and using it to modulate a multi-scale Fourier trunk via single-query cross-attention, enabling the operator to adapt its behaviour while retaining the efficiency of spectral mixing. We provide theoretical justification showing that this modulation converges to a resolution-independent continuous operator under mesh refinement and KAN gives smooth, Lipschitz-controlled global modulation. Across diverse PDE benchmarks, SpectraKAN achieves state-of-the-art performance, reducing RMSE by up to 49% over strong baselines, with particularly large gains on challenging spatio-temporal prediction tasks.

AI.tldr: SpectraKAN is an input-conditioned spectral neural operator that modulates a multi-scale Fourier trunk using a global spatio-temporal representation via single-query cross-attention, yielding a resolution-independent continuous operator with strong PDE performance.
AI.motivation: Static Fourier kernels in spectral neural operators (e.g., FNO) cannot capture multi-scale, regime-dependent, and anisotropic dynamics governed by the global state. An input-conditioned operator can adapt its behavior to the current state.
AI.method: Derive a compact global representation from spatio-temporal history and modulate a multi-scale Fourier trunk with single-query cross-attention to form an input-conditioned integral operator. Provide theoretical guarantees: convergence to a resolution-independent continuum operator under mesh refinement and Lipschitz-controlled global modulation.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on diverse PDE benchmarks, reducing RMSE by up to 49% compared to strong baselines, with particularly notable gains on challenging spatio-temporal tasks.
AI.conclusion: SpectraKAN combines the efficiency of spectral mixing with input-conditioned adaptability, supported by theoretical continuity guarantees, enabling improved modeling of complex PDE dynamics and broad applicability to spatio-temporal prediction tasks.

title: A Mixed Reality System for Robust Manikin Localization in Childbirth Training
summary: Opportunities for medical students to gain practical experience in vaginal births are increasingly constrained by shortened clinical rotations, patient reluctance, and the unpredictable nature of labour. To alleviate clinicians' instructional burden and enhance trainees' learning efficiency, we introduce a mixed reality (MR) system for childbirth training that combines virtual guidance with tactile manikin interaction, thereby preserving authentic haptic feedback while enabling independent practice without continuous on-site expert supervision. The system extends the passthrough capability of commercial head-mounted displays (HMDs) by spatially calibrating an external RGB-D camera, allowing real-time visual integration of physical training objects. Building on this capability, we implement a coarse-to-fine localization pipeline that first aligns the maternal manikin with fiducial markers to define a delivery region and then registers the pre-scanned neonatal head within this area. This process enables spatially accurate overlay of virtual guiding hands near the manikin, allowing trainees to follow expert trajectories reinforced by haptic interaction. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that the system achieves accurate and stable manikin localization on a standalone headset, ensuring practical deployment without external computing resources. A large-scale user study involving 83 fourth-year medical students was subsequently conducted to compare MR-based and virtual reality (VR)-based childbirth training. Four senior obstetricians independently assessed performance using standardized criteria. Results showed that MR training achieved significantly higher scores in delivery, post-delivery, and overall task performance, and was consistently preferred by trainees over VR training.

AI.tldr: A mixed reality (MR) childbirth training system integrating virtual guidance with tactile feedback via a calibrated RGB-D passthrough, enabling independent practice on standalone headsets and outperforming VR in a large student study.
AI.motivation: To address limited clinical rotations, patient reluctance, and need for efficient, hands-on obstetric training while maintaining authentic haptic feedback and reducing on-site supervision.
AI.method: Develop a mixed reality setup that overlays virtual guiding hands onto a physical maternity manikin using spatially calibrated RGB-D passthrough; implement a coarse-to-fine localization: first align the maternal manikin with fiducials to define the delivery region, then register the pre-scanned neonatal head within that region; enable real-time, spatially accurate guidance with haptic interaction; ensure standalone headset operation without external compute; evaluate with a large user study.
AI.result: Localization is accurate and stable on a standalone headset. In a study with 83 fourth-year medical students and four senior obstetricians, MR training yielded significantly higher scores in delivery, post-delivery, and overall task performance, and was consistently preferred over VR training by trainees.
AI.conclusion: MR-based childbirth training enhances objective performance and user preference compared with VR, and is practical for deployment without external computing resources.

title: Beyond Manual Planning: Seating Allocation for Large Organizations
summary: We introduce the Hierarchical Seating Allocation Problem (HSAP) which addresses the optimal assignment of hierarchically structured organizational teams to physical seating arrangements on a floor plan. This problem is driven by the necessity for large organizations with large hierarchies to ensure that teams with close hierarchical relationships are seated in proximity to one another, such as ensuring a research group occupies a contiguous area. Currently, this problem is managed manually leading to infrequent and suboptimal replanning efforts. To alleviate this manual process, we propose an end-to-end framework to solve the HSAP. A scalable approach to calculate the distance between any pair of seats using a probabilistic road map (PRM) and rapidly-exploring random trees (RRT) which is combined with heuristic search and dynamic programming approach to solve the HSAP using integer programming. We demonstrate our approach under different sized instances by evaluating the PRM framework and subsequent allocations both quantitatively and qualitatively.

AI.tldr: Proposes the Hierarchical Seating Allocation Problem (HSAP) and an end-to-end framework that automates seating of hierarchically related teams on a floor plan using PRM/RRT-based distance computation, heuristic search, dynamic programming, and integer programming; evaluated on varying instance sizes.
AI.motivation: Manual seating of large hierarchies is suboptimal and infrequent; automating proximity-aware allocations reduces replanning and improves collaboration.
AI.method: Compute seat distances with a probabilistic roadmap (PRM) and rapidly-exploring random trees (RRT); integrate with heuristic search and dynamic programming; formulate and solve HSAP as an integer programming problem; deliver end-to-end allocation framework.
AI.result: Scalable demonstration across different instance sizes; quantitative and qualitative evaluation of the PRM-based distance computation and the resulting seat allocations.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables scalable, automated HSAP solutions, aligning seating with hierarchical relationships and reducing manual planning effort; performance hinges on PRM/RRT distance accuracy and problem scale.

title: Double-P: Hierarchical Top-P Sparse Attention for Long-Context LLMs
summary: As long-context inference becomes central to large language models (LLMs), attention over growing key-value caches emerges as a dominant decoding bottleneck, motivating sparse attention for scalable inference. Fixed-budget top-k sparse attention cannot adapt to heterogeneous attention distributions across heads and layers, whereas top-p sparse attention directly preserves attention mass and provides stronger accuracy guarantees. Existing top-p methods, however, fail to jointly optimize top-p accuracy, selection overhead, and sparse attention cost, which limits their overall efficiency. We present Double-P, a hierarchical sparse attention framework that optimizes all three stages. Double-P first performs coarse-grained top-p estimation at the cluster level using size-weighted centroids, then adaptively refines computation through a second top-p stage that allocates token-level attention only when needed. Across long-context benchmarks, Double-P consistently achieves near-zero accuracy drop, reducing attention computation overhead by up to 1.8x and delivers up to 1.3x end-to-end decoding speedup over state-of-the-art fixed-budget sparse attention methods.

AI.tldr: Double-P is a two-stage hierarchical sparse attention method for long-context inference that optimizes top-p sparse attention across both cluster-level and token-level stages, delivering near-zero accuracy loss with substantial reductions in computation and faster decoding compared to fixed-budget sparse methods.
AI.motivation: Long-context inference in LLMs is bottlenecked by attention over expanding key-value caches; fixed-budget top-k is inflexible; top-p methods preserve attention mass but fail to jointly optimize accuracy, overhead, and sparse-attention cost.
AI.method: Two-stage: coarse-grained top-p estimation at cluster level using size-weighted centroids; adaptive refinement with a second top-p stage to allocate token-level attention only when needed; hierarchical, adaptive computation to balance accuracy and cost.
AI.result: Near-zero accuracy drop on long-context benchmarks; up to 1.8x reduction in attention computation overhead; up to 1.3x end-to-end decoding speedup against state-of-the-art fixed-budget sparse attention methods.
AI.conclusion: Double-P demonstrates that joint optimization of accuracy, overhead, and sparse-attention cost via hierarchical top-p sparse attention yields efficient and scalable long-context inference for LLMs.

title: EgoPoseVR: Spatiotemporal Multi-Modal Reasoning for Egocentric Full-Body Pose in Virtual Reality
summary: Immersive virtual reality (VR) applications demand accurate, temporally coherent full-body pose tracking. Recent head-mounted camera-based approaches show promise in egocentric pose estimation, but encounter challenges when applied to VR head-mounted displays (HMDs), including temporal instability, inaccurate lower-body estimation, and the lack of real-time performance. To address these limitations, we present EgoPoseVR, an end-to-end framework for accurate egocentric full-body pose estimation in VR that integrates headset motion cues with egocentric RGB-D observations through a dual-modality fusion pipeline. A spatiotemporal encoder extracts frame- and joint-level representations, which are fused via cross-attention to fully exploit complementary motion cues across modalities. A kinematic optimization module then imposes constraints from HMD signals, enhancing the accuracy and stability of pose estimation. To facilitate training and evaluation, we introduce a large-scale synthetic dataset of over 1.8 million temporally aligned HMD and RGB-D frames across diverse VR scenarios. Experimental results show that EgoPoseVR outperforms state-of-the-art egocentric pose estimation models. A user study in real-world scenes further shows that EgoPoseVR achieved significantly higher subjective ratings in accuracy, stability, embodiment, and intention for future use compared to baseline methods. These results show that EgoPoseVR enables robust full-body pose tracking, offering a practical solution for accurate VR embodiment without requiring additional body-worn sensors or room-scale tracking systems.

AI.tldr: EgoPoseVR enables end-to-end egocentric full-body pose estimation for VR by fusing headset motion cues with egocentric RGB-D through a spatiotemporal encoder and cross-attention, refined by a kinematic optimization, backed by a 1.8M-frame synthetic dataset; achieves improved accuracy, stability, and user-perceived embodiment over baselines, without extra sensors.
AI.motivation: Overcome temporal instability, inaccurate lower-body estimation, and lack of real-time performance in HMD-based egocentric pose estimation for VR; enable robust full-body tracking using only HMD and RGB-D.
AI.method: Dual-modality fusion pipeline combining headset motion with egocentric RGB-D; spatiotemporal encoder extracting frame- and joint-level representations; cross-attention to fuse modalities; kinematic optimization constrained by HMD signals; large-scale synthetic dataset for training; evaluation includes objective metrics and a user study.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art egocentric pose estimation models; user study reports higher accuracy, stability, embodiment, and intention to reuse; demonstrates robustness and practicality for VR embodiment without extra sensors.
AI.conclusion: EgoPoseVR provides a practical, sensor-efficient solution for accurate VR embodiment with robust full-body tracking through integrated temporal-spatial fusion and kinematic constraints.

title: Agent2Agent Threats in Safety-Critical LLM Assistants: A Human-Centric Taxonomy
summary: The integration of Large Language Model (LLM)-based conversational agents into vehicles creates novel security challenges at the intersection of agentic AI, automotive safety, and inter-agent communication. As these intelligent assistants coordinate with external services via protocols such as Google's Agent-to-Agent (A2A), they establish attack surfaces where manipulations can propagate through natural language payloads, potentially causing severe consequences ranging from driver distraction to unauthorized vehicle control. Existing AI security frameworks, while foundational, lack the rigorous "separation of concerns" standard in safety-critical systems engineering by co-mingling the concepts of what is being protected (assets) with how it is attacked (attack paths). This paper addresses this methodological gap by proposing a threat modeling framework called AgentHeLLM (Agent Hazard Exploration for LLM Assistants) that formally separates asset identification from attack path analysis. We introduce a human-centric asset taxonomy derived from harm-oriented "victim modeling" and inspired by the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, and a formal graph-based model that distinguishes poison paths (malicious data propagation) from trigger paths (activation actions). We demonstrate the framework's practical applicability through an open-source attack path suggestion tool AgentHeLLM Attack Path Generator that automates multi-stage threat discovery using a bi-level search strategy.

AI.tldr: Proposes AgentHeLLM, a threat-modeling framework that separates asset identification from attack-path analysis for LLM-based agents in automotive settings, with a human-centric asset taxonomy, a graph-based model differentiating poison and trigger paths, and an open-source Attack Path Generator that automates multi-stage threat discovery via a bi-level search.
AI.motivation: Addresses the safety-engineering gap where existing AI security approaches conflate assets with attack paths, particularly in safety-critical, inter-agent automotive contexts utilizing A2A protocols.
AI.method: Introduces a formal graph-based threat model and a victim-centric asset taxonomy inspired by human rights; defines poison vs trigger paths; develops AgentHeLLM Attack Path Generator to automate threat discovery using bi-level search; demonstrates practical applicability with an open-source tool.
AI.result: Framework and tool are presented as practically applicable to automotive LLM agents; supports multi-stage threat discovery and separation of concerns but relies on formal modeling and automated path generation rather than reported empirical security assessments.
AI.conclusion: AgentHeLLM offers a rigorous separation of asset vs attack-path analysis for LLM agents in safety-critical settings, with potential generalization across domains; the open-source tool provides concrete utility for threat discovery and design of mitigations.

title: Extreme Weather Nowcasting via Local Precipitation Pattern Prediction
comment: 10pages, 20 figures, The Fourteenth International Conference on Learning Representations, see https://github.com/tony890048/exPreCast
summary: Accurate forecasting of extreme weather events such as heavy rainfall or storms is critical for risk management and disaster mitigation. Although high-resolution radar observations have spurred extensive research on nowcasting models, precipitation nowcasting remains particularly challenging due to pronounced spatial locality, intricate fine-scale rainfall structures, and variability in forecasting horizons. While recent diffusion-based generative ensembles show promising results, they are computationally expensive and unsuitable for real-time applications. In contrast, deterministic models are computationally efficient but remain biased toward normal rainfall. Furthermore, the benchmark datasets commonly used in prior studies are themselves skewed--either dominated by ordinary rainfall events or restricted to extreme rainfall episodes--thereby hindering general applicability in real-world settings. In this paper, we propose exPreCast, an efficient deterministic framework for generating finely detailed radar forecasts, and introduce a newly constructed balanced radar dataset from the Korea Meteorological Administration (KMA), which encompasses both ordinary precipitation and extreme events. Our model integrates local spatiotemporal attention, a texture-preserving cubic dual upsampling decoder, and a temporal extractor to flexibly adjust forecasting horizons. Experiments on established benchmarks (SEVIR and MeteoNet) as well as on the balanced KMA dataset demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, delivering accurate and reliable nowcasts across both normal and extreme rainfall regimes.

AI.tldr: Proposes exPreCast, an efficient deterministic radar nowcasting model with a balanced KMA dataset, achieving state-of-the-art results on SEVIR, MeteoNet, and KMA for both ordinary and extreme rainfall.
AI.motivation: Address the challenges of extreme weather nowcasting: limits of diffusion-based ensembles (computationally heavy), deterministic models biased toward normal rainfall, and biased benchmark datasets that hinder real-world generalization.
AI.method: Introduce exPreCast with local spatiotemporal attention, a texture-preserving cubic dual upsampling decoder, and a temporal extractor to flexibly adjust forecasting horizons; construct a balanced KMA radar dataset including both ordinary and extreme events.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on SEVIR and MeteoNet benchmarks and on the balanced KMA dataset, delivering accurate and reliable nowcasts across normal and extreme rainfall regimes.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that an efficient deterministic framework can match or surpass diffusion-based approaches in nowcasting accuracy while enabling real-time operation; dataset balancing enhances generalization across rainfall regimes.

title: CAViT -- Channel-Aware Vision Transformer for Dynamic Feature Fusion
comment: Presented at the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2025 (CVPR 25) in the 4th Workshop on Transformers for Visions - T4V (https://sites.google.com/view/t4v-cvpr25/) Accepted for Publication at 33rd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Cognitive Science (AICS 2025), where it was shortlisted for Best Paper Award. (https://aicsconf.org/?page_id=278)
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have demonstrated strong performance across a range of computer vision tasks by modeling long-range spatial interactions via self-attention. However, channel-wise mixing in ViTs remains static, relying on fixed multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) that lack adaptability to input content. We introduce 'CAViT', a dual-attention architecture that replaces the static MLP with a dynamic, attention-based mechanism for feature interaction. Each Transformer block in CAViT performs spatial self-attention followed by channel-wise self-attention, allowing the model to dynamically recalibrate feature representations based on global image context. This unified and content-aware token mixing strategy enhances representational expressiveness without increasing depth or complexity. We validate CAViT across five benchmark datasets spanning both natural and medical domains, where it outperforms the standard ViT baseline by up to +3.6% in accuracy, while reducing parameter count and FLOPs by over 30%. Qualitative attention maps reveal sharper and semantically meaningful activation patterns, validating the effectiveness of our attention-driven token mixing.

AI.tldr: CAViT replaces static MLP-based channel mixing in ViTs with a dynamic, dual-attention mechanism (spatial then channel-wise self-attention) in each Transformer block, enabling content-aware token interaction and achieving up to +3.6% accuracy while reducing parameters and FLOPs by over 30% across five datasets (natural and medical).
AI.motivation: ViTs rely on fixed, static MLPs for channel mixing, which are not content-adaptive. A dynamic, attention-based channel interaction can improve representational expressiveness and efficiency by leveraging global context.
AI.method: In every Transformer block, apply spatial self-attention to capture long-range dependencies, followed by channel-wise self-attention to recalibrate feature interactions. This dual-attention token-mixing replaces the traditional static MLP without increasing depth or overall computational burden, demonstrated across five benchmarks.
AI.result: The approach outperforms the ViT baseline by up to 3.6 percentage points in accuracy and reduces parameters and FLOPs by over 30%. Qualitative attention maps indicate sharper, semantically meaningful activation patterns, supporting the efficacy of attention-driven token mixing.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, unified, content-aware token mixing via dual-attention (spatial and channel-wise) enhances ViT representational capacity and efficiency across natural and medical domains, suggesting broad applicability of the approach.

title: A Guide to Large Language Models in Modeling and Simulation: From Core Techniques to Critical Challenges
comment: Book chapter. Accepted in Artificial Intelligence in Modeling and Simulation, Philippe J. Giabbanelli and Istvan David (eds). Series on Simulation Foundations, Methods and Applications. Springer, Cham. Series ISSN: 2195-2817
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have rapidly become familiar tools to researchers and practitioners. Concepts such as prompting, temperature, or few-shot examples are now widely recognized, and LLMs are increasingly used in Modeling & Simulation (M&S) workflows. However, practices that appear straightforward may introduce subtle issues, unnecessary complexity, or may even lead to inferior results. Adding more data can backfire (e.g., deteriorating performance through model collapse or inadvertently wiping out existing guardrails), spending time on fine-tuning a model can be unnecessary without a prior assessment of what it already knows, setting the temperature to 0 is not sufficient to make LLMs deterministic, providing a large volume of M&S data as input can be excessive (LLMs cannot attend to everything) but naive simplifications can lose information. We aim to provide comprehensive and practical guidance on how to use LLMs, with an emphasis on M&S applications. We discuss common sources of confusion, including non-determinism, knowledge augmentation (including RAG and LoRA), decomposition of M&S data, and hyper-parameter settings. We emphasize principled design choices, diagnostic strategies, and empirical evaluation, with the goal of helping modelers make informed decisions about when, how, and whether to rely on LLMs.

AI.tldr: Offers practical guidance for using LLMs in M&S, addressing non-determinism, data choices, fine-tuning, RAG/LoRA, and hyperparameters, with diagnostic strategies and empirical evaluation to inform decision-making.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly embedded in Modeling & Simulation workflows, but naive settings can degrade results or introduce hidden pitfalls; need structured guidance to avoid these issues.
AI.method: Analytical synthesis of common practices, identification of failure modes, and recommended workflows for configuring and evaluating LLMs in M&S, including data decomposition, knowledge augmentation techniques (RAG, LoRA), and hyperparameter considerations.
AI.result: A comprehensive guidance framework that helps practitioners decide when/how to use LLMs in M&S, with principled design choices, diagnostic strategies, and empirical evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Promotes principled, empirically validated use of LLMs in M&S to enable informed decision-making and prevent common missteps.

title: Disentangled Representation Learning via Flow Matching
summary: Disentangled representation learning aims to capture the underlying explanatory factors of observed data, enabling a principled understanding of the data-generating process. Recent advances in generative modeling have introduced new paradigms for learning such representations. However, existing diffusion-based methods encourage factor independence via inductive biases, yet frequently lack strong semantic alignment. In this work, we propose a flow matching-based framework for disentangled representation learning, which casts disentanglement as learning factor-conditioned flows in a compact latent space. To enforce explicit semantic alignment, we introduce a non-overlap (orthogonality) regularizer that suppresses cross-factor interference and reduces information leakage between factors. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over representative baselines, yielding higher disentanglement scores as well as improved controllability and sample fidelity.

AI.tldr: A flow matching-based disentanglement method with factor-conditioned latent flows and an orthogonality regularizer, yielding improved disentanglement, controllability, and sample fidelity.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based disentanglement methods often rely on inductive biases to enforce factor independence but lack strong semantic alignment between learned factors and interpretable concepts; there is a need for explicit semantic alignment and reduced inter-factor leakage.
AI.method: Introduce a flow matching-based framework that learns factor-conditioned flows in a compact latent space; impose a non-overlap (orthogonality) regularizer to suppress cross-factor interference and minimize information leakage between factors.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple datasets show consistent improvements over representative baselines, with higher disentanglement scores and improved controllability and sample fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework achieves better semantic alignment for disentangled representations by combining factor-conditioned flows with an orthogonality regularizer; demonstrated gains suggest potential applicability to broader generative modeling tasks and directions for further refinement.

title: Multi-instance robust fitting for non-classical geometric models
summary: Most existing robust fitting methods are designed for classical models, such as lines, circles, and planes. In contrast, fewer methods have been developed to robustly handle non-classical models, such as spiral curves, procedural character models, and free-form surfaces. Furthermore, existing methods primarily focus on reconstructing a single instance of a non-classical model. This paper aims to reconstruct multiple instances of non-classical models from noisy data. We formulate this multi-instance fitting task as an optimization problem, which comprises an estimator and an optimizer. Specifically, we propose a novel estimator based on the model-to-data error, capable of handling outliers without a predefined error threshold. Since the proposed estimator is non-differentiable with respect to the model parameters, we employ a meta-heuristic algorithm as the optimizer to seek the global optimum. The effectiveness of our method are demonstrated through experimental results on various non-classical models. The code is available at https://github.com/zhangzongliang/fitting.

AI.tldr: Proposes a two-part framework for multi-instance robust fitting of non-classical models using a non-differentiable estimator based on model-to-data error, optimized by a meta-heuristic search to find global optima; demonstrates on various non-classical models with code released.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where current robust fitting methods target classical shapes (lines, circles, planes) and single-instance non-classical models; the need to recover multiple instances from noisy data with outliers without predefined error thresholds.
AI.method: Introduce an estimator defined by model-to-data error that is robust to outliers without a fixed threshold. Because the estimator is non-differentiable with respect to model parameters, apply a meta-heuristic optimizer to search the global optimum. Formulate as a joint estimator–optimizer optimization problem to recover multiple instances of non-classical models from noisy data.
AI.result: Experimental results on several non-classical model types demonstrate effectiveness. The approach is complemented by publicly available code at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a viable path for robustly fitting multiple instances of non-classical models from noisy data, leveraging a non-differentiable estimator and a global-search optimizer; it shows promise across diverse non-classical shapes, with practical availability of code for reproduction.

title: Quantum Reinforcement Learning with Transformers for the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem
summary: This paper addresses the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) by comparing classical and quantum Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches. An Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C) agent is implemented in classical, full quantum, and hybrid variants, integrating transformer architectures to capture the relationships between vehicles, clients, and the depot through self- and cross-attention mechanisms. The experiments focus on multi-vehicle scenarios with capacity constraints, considering 20 clients and 4 vehicles, and are conducted over ten independent runs. Performance is assessed using routing distance, route compactness, and route overlap. The results show that all three approaches are capable of learning effective routing policies. However, quantum-enhanced models outperform the classical baseline and produce more robust route organization, with the hybrid architecture achieving the best overall performance across distance, compactness, and route overlap. In addition to quantitative improvements, qualitative visualizations reveal that quantum-based models generate more structured and coherent routing solutions. These findings highlight the potential of hybrid quantum-classical reinforcement learning models for addressing complex combinatorial optimization problems such as the CVRP.

AI.tldr: Quantum-enhanced reinforcement learning (including hybrid quantum-classical variants) improves CVRP performance over classical A2C, with hybrids delivering the best overall metrics and more structured solutions.
AI.motivation: Assess whether quantum RL can improve solving CVRP and whether hybrid architectures offer advantages over purely classical or purely quantum approaches.
AI.method: Implement A2C agents in three variants: classical, full quantum, and hybrid. Use transformer-based architectures with self- and cross-attention to model relationships among vehicles, clients, and depot. Train on CVRP instances with 20 clients and 4 vehicles, across 10 independent runs. Evaluate using routing distance, route compactness, and route overlap.
AI.result: Quantum-enhanced models outperform the classical baseline across metrics; hybrid quantum-classical architecture achieves the best overall performance in distance, compactness, and route overlap. Qualitative visualizations show more structured and coherent routing.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid quantum-classical RL shows strong potential for complex combinatorial optimization like CVRP, offering robustness and improved routing organization compared with purely classical or quantum approaches.

title: Private Prediction via Shrinkage
summary:
We study differentially private prediction introduced by Dwork and Feldman (COLT 2018): an algorithm receives one labeled sample set $S$ and then answers a stream of unlabeled queries while the output transcript remains $(\varepsilon,δ)$-differentially private with respect to $S$. Standard composition yields a $\sqrt{T}$ dependence for $T$ queries.
  We show that this dependence can be reduced to polylogarithmic in $T$ in streaming settings. For an oblivious online adversary and any concept class $\mathcal{C}$, we give a private predictor that answers $T$ queries with $|S|= \tilde{O}(VC(\mathcal{C})^{3.5}\log^{3.5}T)$ labeled examples. For an adaptive online adversary and halfspaces over $\mathbb{R}^d$, we obtain $|S|=\tilde{O}\left(d^{5.5}\log T\right)$.

AI.tldr: Polylogarithmic-in-T privacy for streaming private predictions; replaces sqrt(T) from naive composition with polylog(T) dependence, via sample-efficient private predictors. For oblivious adversaries: |S| = Õ( VC(C)^{3.5} log^{3.5} T ). For adaptive adversaries with halfspaces in Rd: |S| = Õ( d^{5.5} log T ).
AI.motivation: Improve DP guarantees for streaming prediction where a single labeled sample set guides a stream of unlabeled queries; overcome the standard DP composition barrier (√T) in the online streaming setting.
AI.method: Develop private predictors that, after receiving a labeled set S, answer T unlabeled queries while maintaining (ε,δ)-DP w.r.t. S. For oblivious adversaries, leverage VC dimension bounds to control error and privacy; for adaptive adversaries in linear settings, exploit geometric/DP techniques tailored to halfspaces to reduce labeled data requirements.
AI.result: Achieves polylogarithmic dependence on T for the number of queries. Explicit labeled-sample complexities: Õ( VC(C)^{3.5} log^{3.5} T ) for oblivious adversaries and Õ( d^{5.5} log T ) for adaptive adversaries with halfspaces in Rd.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that streaming DP prediction can beat naïve √T query-privacy scaling, attaining polylog T in T under both general and geometric (halfspace) settings; highlights fundamental trade-offs between adversary model, concept class, and labeled data requirements.

title: Unified Sensor Simulation for Autonomous Driving
summary: In this work, we introduce \textbf{XSIM}, a sensor simulation framework for autonomous driving. XSIM extends 3DGUT splatting with a generalized rolling-shutter modeling tailored for autonomous driving applications. Our framework provides a unified and flexible formulation for appearance and geometric sensor modeling, enabling rendering of complex sensor distortions in dynamic environments. We identify spherical cameras, such as LiDARs, as a critical edge case for existing 3DGUT splatting due to cyclic projection and time discontinuities at azimuth boundaries leading to incorrect particle projection. To address this issue, we propose a phase modeling mechanism that explicitly accounts temporal and shape discontinuities of Gaussians projected by the Unscented Transform at azimuth borders. In addition, we introduce an extended 3D Gaussian representation that incorporates two distinct opacity parameters to resolve mismatches between geometry and color distributions. As a result, our framework provides enhanced scene representations with improved geometric consistency and photorealistic appearance. We evaluate our framework extensively on multiple autonomous driving datasets, including Waymo Open Dataset, Argoverse 2, and PandaSet. Our framework consistently outperforms strong recent baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance across all datasets. The source code is publicly available at \href{https://github.com/whesense/XSIM}{https://github.com/whesense/XSIM}.

AI.tldr: XSIM extends 3DGUT splatting with generalized rolling-shutter modeling for autonomous driving, addressing edge-case distortions in spherical camera scenarios via phase modeling and a dual-opacity 3D Gaussian representation, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple driving datasets.
AI.motivation: There is a need for unified, flexible sensor appearance and geometry modeling in autonomous driving to render complex distortions in dynamic environments, and to address edge cases (e.g., spherical cameras/LiDAR-like imaging) where existing 3D Gaussian splatting fails due to cyclic projections and azimuth-boundary discontinuities.
AI.method: Extend 3DGUT splatting with a generalized rolling-shutter model for dynamic scenes; introduce phase modeling to handle temporal and shape discontinuities of Gaussians at azimuth borders projected by the Unscented Transform; propose an extended 3D Gaussian representation with two opacity parameters to better align geometry and color distributions.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation on Waymo Open Dataset, Argoverse 2, and PandaSet shows consistent improvements over strong baselines and state-of-the-art performance across all datasets; source code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: XSIM provides enhanced scene representations with improved geometric consistency and photorealistic appearance, by unifying appearance and geometry sensing in a single framework and addressing spherical-camera edge cases.

title: Geographically-aware Transformer-based Traffic Forecasting for Urban Motorway Digital Twins
comment: IEEE IV2026 37th IEEE Intelligent Vehicles Symposium
summary: The operational effectiveness of digital-twin technology in motorway traffic management depends on the availability of a continuous flow of high-resolution real-time traffic data. To function as a proactive decision-making support layer within traffic management, a digital twin must also incorporate predicted traffic conditions in addition to real-time observations. Due to the spatio-temporal complexity and the time-variant, non-linear nature of traffic dynamics, predicting motorway traffic remains a difficult problem. Sequence-based deep-learning models offer clear advantages over classical machine learning and statistical models in capturing long-range, temporal dependencies in time-series traffic data, yet limitations in forecasting accuracy and model complexity point to the need for further improvements.
