To improve motorway traffic forecasting, this paper introduces a Geographically-aware Transformer-based Traffic Forecasting GATTF model, which exploits the geographical relationships between distributed sensors using their mutual information (MI). The model has been evaluated using real-time data from the Geneva motorway network in Switzerland and results confirm that incorporating geographical awareness through MI enhances the accuracy of GATTF forecasting compared to a standard Transformer, without increasing model complexity.

AI.tldr: Geographically-aware Transformer (GATTF) using mutual information between sensors improves motorway traffic forecasting accuracy without increasing model complexity; validated on Geneva data.
AI.motivation: Traffic dynamics are spatio-temporal, non-linear, and exhibit long-range dependencies. Accurate, real-time forecasts are crucial for digital twin-based traffic management.
AI.method: Extend Transformer-based forecasting by incorporating geographical relationships via mutual information between distributed sensors. Compute MI to define connections/attention weights, integrating geographical awareness into the model. Evaluate on real-time Geneva motorway network data and compare against a standard Transformer baseline.
AI.result: MI-aware GATTF achieves higher predictive accuracy than a standard Transformer on Geneva motorway traffic data, without increasing model complexity (in terms of parameters).
AI.conclusion: Incorporating geographical awareness through mutual information enhances motorway traffic forecasting, making the approach suitable for integration into digital twins and real-time traffic management systems.

title: ZeroS: Zero-Sum Linear Attention for Efficient Transformers
summary: Linear attention methods offer Transformers $O(N)$ complexity but typically underperform standard softmax attention. We identify two fundamental limitations affecting these approaches: the restriction to convex combinations that only permits additive information blending, and uniform accumulated weight bias that dilutes attention in long contexts. We propose Zero-Sum Linear Attention (ZeroS), which addresses these limitations by removing the constant zero-order term $1/t$ and reweighting the remaining zero-sum softmax residuals. This modification creates mathematically stable weights, enabling both positive and negative values and allowing a single attention layer to perform contrastive operations. While maintaining $O(N)$ complexity, ZeroS theoretically expands the set of representable functions compared to convex combinations. Empirically, it matches or exceeds standard softmax attention across various sequence modeling benchmarks.

AI.tldr: ZeroS removes the constant 1/t term and reweights zero-sum softmax residuals to enable stable, bidirectional attention with O(N) complexity and improved representational power over convex linear attention.
AI.motivation: Address two core limits of linear attention: (1) restriction to convex combinations that only allow additive information blending, and (2) uniform accumulated weight bias that weakens long-context attention.
AI.method: Modify the linear attention mechanism by excluding the zero-order term and reweighting the zero-sum residuals of the softmax, enabling positive and negative weights, preserving O(N) time, and allowing contrastive operations within a single attention layer.
AI.result: Theoretically enlarges the set of representable functions beyond convex combinations; empirically matches or exceeds standard softmax attention across various sequence modeling benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ZeroS narrows the gap between linear and softmax attention by enabling stable, contrastive-like interactions in a single, efficient layer without sacrificing look-ahead or context length.

title: ROMAN: Reward-Orchestrated Multi-Head Attention Network for Autonomous Driving System Testing
comment: The manuscript includes 13 pages, 8 tables, and 7 figures
summary: Automated Driving System (ADS) acts as the brain of autonomous vehicles, responsible for their safety and efficiency. Safe deployment requires thorough testing in diverse real-world scenarios and compliance with traffic laws like speed limits, signal obedience, and right-of-way rules. Violations like running red lights or speeding pose severe safety risks. However, current testing approaches face significant challenges: limited ability to generate complex and high-risk law-breaking scenarios, and failing to account for complex interactions involving multiple vehicles and critical situations. To address these challenges, we propose ROMAN, a novel scenario generation approach for ADS testing that combines a multi-head attention network with a traffic law weighting mechanism. ROMAN is designed to generate high-risk violation scenarios to enable more thorough and targeted ADS evaluation. The multi-head attention mechanism models interactions among vehicles, traffic signals, and other factors. The traffic law weighting mechanism implements a workflow that leverages an LLM-based risk weighting module to evaluate violations based on the two dimensions of severity and occurrence. We have evaluated ROMAN by testing the Baidu Apollo ADS within the CARLA simulation platform and conducting extensive experiments to measure its performance. Experimental results demonstrate that ROMAN surpassed state-of-the-art tools ABLE and LawBreaker by achieving 7.91% higher average violation count than ABLE and 55.96% higher than LawBreaker, while also maintaining greater scenario diversity. In addition, only ROMAN successfully generated violation scenarios for every clause of the input traffic laws, enabling it to identify more high-risk violations than existing approaches.

AI.tldr: ROMAN is a scenario-generation method for ADS testing that uses a multi-head attention network to model interactions among vehicles, signals, and other factors, plus a traffic law weighting mechanism with an LLM-based risk module to generate high-risk violations. It outperforms ABLE and LawBreaker in CARLA/Baidu Apollo experiments and achieves full clause coverage of input laws.
AI.motivation: Current ADS testing struggles to generate complex, high-risk law-breaking scenarios and to capture intricate interactions among multiple agents and traffic elements; there is a need for targeted, diverse, and compliant scenario generation to improve safety testing.
AI.method: A multi-head attention network models interactions among vehicles, signals, and factors; a traffic-law weighting mechanism uses an LLM-based risk weighting module to evaluate violations by severity and occurrence; ROMAN generates scenarios that emphasize high-risk violations.
AI.result: In CARLA with Baidu Apollo, ROMAN achieved 7.91% higher average violation count than ABLE and 55.96% higher than LawBreaker; it also delivered greater scenario diversity and uniquely generated violation scenarios for every clause of the input traffic laws, enabling more high-risk violations to be identified.
AI.conclusion: ROMAN provides a more thorough and clause-complete generation of high-risk traffic-violation scenarios for ADS testing, improving evaluation coverage and safety insights.

title: Speech Emotion Recognition Leveraging OpenAI's Whisper Representations and Attentive Pooling Methods
summary: Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) research has faced limitations due to the lack of standard and sufficiently large datasets. Recent studies have leveraged pre-trained models to extract features for downstream tasks such as SER. This work explores the capabilities of Whisper, a pre-trained ASR system, in speech emotion recognition by proposing two attention-based pooling methods, Multi-head Attentive Average Pooling and QKV Pooling, designed to efficiently reduce the dimensionality of Whisper representations while preserving emotional features. We experiment on English and Persian, using the IEMOCAP and ShEMO datasets respectively, with Whisper Tiny and Small. Our multi-head QKV architecture achieves state-of-the-art results on the ShEMO dataset, with a 2.47% improvement in unweighted accuracy. We further compare the performance of different Whisper encoder layers and find that intermediate layers often perform better for SER on the Persian dataset, providing a lightweight and efficient alternative to much larger models such as HuBERT X-Large. Our findings highlight the potential of Whisper as a representation extractor for SER and demonstrate the effectiveness of attention-based pooling for dimension reduction.

AI.tldr: Whisper-based SER with two attention-based pooling methods (Multi-head Attentive Average Pooling and QKV Pooling) achieves strong results, notably state-of-the-art on Persian ShEMO, using lightweight Whisper encoders and intermediate layers; highlights efficiency over larger models like HuBERT X-Large.
AI.motivation: Address SER data scarcity and the limitations of standard datasets by leveraging large pre-trained models. Propose efficient pooling to extract emotional information from Whisper representations, enabling lightweight yet effective SER across languages (English and Persian).
AI.method: Introduce two attention-based pooling schemes—Multi-head Attentive Average Pooling and QKV Pooling—to compress Whisper encoder outputs while retaining emotional cues. Evaluate with Whisper Tiny and Small on English (IEMOCAP) and Persian (ShEMO) datasets; compare different Whisper encoder layers to identify effective representations for SER.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art unweighted accuracy on ShEMO (Persian) with a 2.47% improvement. Intermediate Whisper layers often yield better performance for Persian SER. Demonstrates that Whisper can serve as a lightweight representation extractor for SER, offering a viable alternative to larger models like HuBERT X-Large.
AI.conclusion: Attention-based pooling effectively reduces dimensionality and preserves emotion-relevant information in Whisper representations, enabling accurate SER with smaller models and supporting cross-lingual SER between English and Persian.

title: Balanced Anomaly-guided Ego-graph Diffusion Model for Inductive Graph Anomaly Detection
comment: 12 pages,6 figures, Accepted by ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD '26)
summary: Graph anomaly detection (GAD) is crucial in applications like fraud detection and cybersecurity. Despite recent advancements using graph neural networks (GNNs), two major challenges persist. At the model level, most methods adopt a transductive learning paradigm, which assumes static graph structures, making them unsuitable for dynamic, evolving networks. At the data level, the extreme class imbalance, where anomalous nodes are rare, leads to biased models that fail to generalize to unseen anomalies. These challenges are interdependent: static transductive frameworks limit effective data augmentation, while imbalance exacerbates model distortion in inductive learning settings. To address these challenges, we propose a novel data-centric framework that integrates dynamic graph modeling with balanced anomaly synthesis. Our framework features: (1) a discrete ego-graph diffusion model, which captures the local topology of anomalies to generate ego-graphs aligned with anomalous structural distribution, and (2) a curriculum anomaly augmentation mechanism, which dynamically adjusts synthetic data generation during training, focusing on underrepresented anomaly patterns to improve detection and generalization. Experiments on five datasets demonstrate that the effectiveness of our framework.

AI.tldr: A data-centric framework for dynamic graph anomaly detection combining a discrete ego-graph diffusion model to synthesize anomalies with a curriculum-based augmentation strategy that balances rare patterns during training; validated on five datasets showing improved effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Static transductive GAD models struggle on dynamic graphs and are hampered by extreme class imbalance where anomalies are rare. These issues are interconnected: limited effective data augmentation in static settings exacerbates bias, reducing generalization to unseen anomalies.
AI.method: Introduce a discrete ego-graph diffusion model to capture local anomalous topology and generate ego-graphs aligned with the distribution of anomalies. Pair with a curriculum anomaly augmentation mechanism that adaptively emphasizes underrepresented anomaly patterns during training, integrating these into a dynamic graph learning framework.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on five datasets demonstrates the framework's effectiveness, showing improved detection and generalization over baselines (specific metrics are not provided in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: A data-centric approach that jointly models dynamic graph structure and balanced anomaly synthesis can address both data-level and model-level challenges in GAD, with demonstrated gains and avenues for further enhancement.

title: UniSurg: A Video-Native Foundation Model for Universal Understanding of Surgical Videos
summary: While foundation models have advanced surgical video analysis, current approaches rely predominantly on pixel-level reconstruction objectives that waste model capacity on low-level visual details - such as smoke, specular reflections, and fluid motion - rather than semantic structures essential for surgical understanding. We present UniSurg, a video-native foundation model that shifts the learning paradigm from pixel-level reconstruction to latent motion prediction. Built on the Video Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (V-JEPA), UniSurg introduces three key technical innovations tailored to surgical videos: 1) motion-guided latent prediction to prioritize semantically meaningful regions, 2) spatiotemporal affinity self-distillation to enforce relational consistency, and 3) feature diversity regularization to prevent representation collapse in texture-sparse surgical scenes. To enable large-scale pretraining, we curate UniSurg-15M, the largest surgical video dataset to date, comprising 3,658 hours of video from 50 sources across 13 anatomical regions. Extensive experiments across 17 benchmarks demonstrate that UniSurg significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on surgical workflow recognition (+14.6% F1 on EgoSurgery, +10.3% on PitVis), action triplet recognition (39.54% mAP-IVT on CholecT50), skill assessment, polyp segmentation, and depth estimation. These results establish UniSurg as a new standard for universal, motion-oriented surgical video understanding.

AI.tldr: UniSurg is a video-native foundation model for surgical video understanding that shifts from pixel-level reconstruction to motion-driven latent prediction, achieving superior performance on multiple benchmarks and enabling large-scale pretraining with a 15M video dataset.
AI.motivation: Current surgical video models rely on pixel-level reconstruction that emphasizes low-level visual details, which can waste model capacity and hinder semantic understanding; there is a need for semantically meaningful representations that generalize across surgical tasks.
AI.method: The approach introduces three innovations on top of Video Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (V-JEPA): (1) motion-guided latent prediction to emphasize semantically meaningful regions, (2) spatiotemporal affinity self-distillation to enforce relational consistency, and (3) feature diversity regularization to prevent representation collapse in texture-sparse surgical scenes. Pretraining uses UniSurg-15M, a large surgical video dataset (3,658 hours, 50 sources, 13 anatomical regions).
AI.result: Empirical evaluation over 17 benchmarks shows substantial improvements over state-of-the-art: +14.6% F1 on EgoSurgery, +10.3% on PitVis for surgical workflow recognition; 39.54% mAP-IVT on CholecT50 for action triplet recognition; also benefits in skill assessment, polyp segmentation, and depth estimation.
AI.conclusion: UniSurg establishes a new standard for universal, motion-oriented surgical video understanding by prioritizing latent motion information and relational consistency, enabling scalable pretraining with a large, diverse surgical video corpus.

title: AgenticPay: A Multi-Agent LLM Negotiation System for Buyer-Seller Transactions
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based agents are increasingly expected to negotiate, coordinate, and transact autonomously, yet existing benchmarks lack principled settings for evaluating language-mediated economic interaction among multiple agents. We introduce AgenticPay, a benchmark and simulation framework for multi-agent buyer-seller negotiation driven by natural language. AgenticPay models markets in which buyers and sellers possess private constraints and product-dependent valuations, and must reach agreements through multi-round linguistic negotiation rather than numeric bidding alone. The framework supports a diverse suite of over 110 tasks ranging from bilateral bargaining to many-to-many markets, with structured action extraction and metrics for feasibility, efficiency, and welfare. Benchmarking state-of-the-art proprietary and open-weight LLMs reveals substantial gaps in negotiation performance and highlights challenges in long-horizon strategic reasoning, establishing AgenticPay as a foundation for studying agentic commerce and language-based market interaction. Code and dataset are available at the link: https://github.com/SafeRL-Lab/AgenticPay.

AI.tldr: A benchmark and framework, AgenticPay, enables evaluation of multi-agent, language-mediated buyer-seller negotiation across 110+ tasks with private constraints and product-dependent valuations; reveals gaps in current LLMs and provides datasets/code.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of principled benchmarks for language-based economic interaction among multiple agents; need to study long-horizon, multi-round negotiation beyond numeric bidding.
AI.method: Simulation framework with private constraints/valuations; multi-round natural-language negotiation; supports bilateral and many-to-many markets; structured action extraction; metrics for feasibility, efficiency, welfare; 110+ tasks.
AI.result: State-of-the-art LLMs show substantial gaps in negotiation performance and long-horizon strategic reasoning; AgenticPay establishes a foundation for studying language-based market interaction; code/dataset released.
AI.conclusion: AgenticPay provides a scalable, interpretable platform to study agentic commerce and language-based markets, enabling systematic evaluation and progress in LLM-driven negotiation.

title: Faithful Bi-Directional Model Steering via Distribution Matching and Distributed Interchange Interventions
summary: Intervention-based model steering offers a lightweight and interpretable alternative to prompting and fine-tuning. However, by adapting strong optimization objectives from fine-tuning, current methods are susceptible to overfitting and often underperform, sometimes generating unnatural outputs. We hypothesize that this is because effective steering requires the faithful identification of internal model mechanisms, not the enforcement of external preferences. To this end, we build on the principles of distributed alignment search (DAS), the standard for causal variable localization, to propose a new steering method: Concept DAS (CDAS). While we adopt the core mechanism of DAS, distributed interchange intervention (DII), we introduce a novel distribution matching objective tailored for the steering task by aligning intervened output distributions with counterfactual distributions. CDAS differs from prior work in two main ways: first, it learns interventions via weak-supervised distribution matching rather than probability maximization; second, it uses DIIs that naturally enable bi-directional steering and allow steering factors to be derived from data, reducing the effort required for hyperparameter tuning and resulting in more faithful and stable control. On AxBench, a large-scale model steering benchmark, we show that CDAS does not always outperform preference-optimization methods but may benefit more from increased model scale. In two safety-related case studies, overriding refusal behaviors of safety-aligned models and neutralizing a chain-of-thought backdoor, CDAS achieves systematic steering while maintaining general model utility. These results indicate that CDAS is complementary to preference-optimization approaches and conditionally constitutes a robust approach to intervention-based model steering. Our code is available at https://github.com/colored-dye/concept_das.

AI.tldr: CDAS uses weakly supervised distribution matching with distributed interchange interventions (DII) to steer LMs by aligning intervened outputs with counterfactuals; it complements preference-based methods and shows scale-dependent benefits, with robust safety-case performance.
AI.motivation: Address overfitting and instability of optimization-based steering by focusing on faithful identification of internal model mechanisms rather than enforcing external preferences.
AI.method: Adapts the distributed alignment search (DAS) framework with a novel distribution-matching objective; learns interventions via weak supervision; employs bi-directional DIIs allowing data-derived steering factors and reduced hyperparameter tuning.
AI.result: On AxBench CDAS does not consistently outperform preference-optimization but benefits more from larger model scale; in two safety-related case studies it achieves systematic steering while preserving general model utility.
AI.conclusion: CDAS is complementary to preference-optimization approaches and can provide robust intervention-based model steering, particularly as model scale increases.

title: Enhancing Personality Recognition by Comparing the Predictive Power of Traits, Facets, and Nuances
comment: Accepted to the 2025 13th International Conference on Affective Computing and Intelligent Interaction (Late Breaking Results)
summary: Personality is a complex, hierarchical construct typically assessed through item-level questionnaires aggregated into broad trait scores. Personality recognition models aim to infer personality traits from different sources of behavioral data. However, reliance on broad trait scores as ground truth, combined with limited training data, poses challenges for generalization, as similar trait scores can manifest through diverse, context dependent behaviors. In this work, we explore the predictive impact of the more granular hierarchical levels of the Big-Five Personality Model, facets and nuances, to enhance personality recognition from audiovisual interaction data. Using the UDIVA v0.5 dataset, we trained a transformer-based model including cross-modal (audiovisual) and cross-subject (dyad-aware) attention mechanisms. Results show that nuance-level models consistently outperform facet and trait-level models, reducing mean squared error by up to 74% across interaction scenarios.

AI.tldr: Nuance-level (facet/nested) Big-Five modeling yields superior personality recognition from audiovisual data over trait/facet levels, with substantial error reductions.
AI.motivation: Ground-truth personality often uses broad trait scores that can mask context-specific manifestations; hierarchical levels (facets, nuances) may capture richer behavioral signals for better generalization.
AI.method: A transformer-based model with cross-modal (audiovisual) and cross-subject (dyad-aware) attention trained on the UDIVA v0.5 dataset to predict personality at nuance/facet/trait levels.
AI.result: Nuance-level models consistently outperform facet and trait-level models, achieving up to a 74% reduction in mean squared error across various interaction scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Modeling personality at finer-grained hierarchical levels improves predictive accuracy from audiovisual interactions and may mitigate generalization limits inherent to broad trait-ground truth.

title: CORP: Closed-Form One-shot Representation-Preserving Structured Pruning for Vision Transformers
summary: Vision Transformers achieve strong accuracy but incur high compute and memory cost. Structured pruning can reduce inference cost, but most methods rely on retraining or multi-stage optimization. These requirements limit post-training deployment. We propose \textbf{CORP}, a closed-form one-shot structured pruning framework for Vision Transformers. CORP removes entire MLP hidden dimensions and attention substructures without labels, gradients, or fine-tuning. It operates under strict post-training constraints using only a small unlabeled calibration set. CORP formulates structured pruning as a representation recovery problem. It models removed activations and attention logits as affine functions of retained components and derives closed-form ridge regression solutions that fold compensation into model weights. This minimizes expected representation error under the calibration distribution. Experiments on ImageNet with DeiT models show strong redundancy in MLP and attention representations. Without compensation, one-shot structured pruning causes severe accuracy degradation. With CORP, models preserve accuracy under aggressive sparsity. On DeiT-Huge, CORP retains 82.8\% Top-1 accuracy after pruning 50\% of both MLP and attention structures. CORP completes pruning in under 20 minutes on a single GPU and delivers substantial real-world efficiency gains.

AI.tldr: One-shot, closed-form structured pruning (CORP) for Vision Transformers removing entire MLP hidden dims and attention blocks without labels or fine-tuning, using affine representation recovery with ridge regression to compensate weights; achieves strong sparsity with minimal accuracy loss and fast pruning on a single GPU.
AI.motivation: Existing structured pruning methods require retraining or multi-stage optimization, which hampers post-training deployment for Vision Transformers. A fast, label-free, one-shot pruning method is desirable to reduce compute and memory costs.
AI.method: CORP models removed activations and attention logits as affine functions of retained components and derives closed-form ridge regression solutions to compute compensating weights, folding compensation into the model weights. It uses a small unlabeled calibration set to minimize expected representation error under the calibration distribution, enabling one-shot pruning without gradients or labels.
AI.result: Empirical results on ImageNet with DeiT show that aggressive pruning is possible without large accuracy loss. On DeiT-Huge, pruning 50% of both MLP and attention structures yields 82.8% Top-1 accuracy retained. Pruning completes in under 20 minutes on a single GPU, delivering substantial real-world efficiency gains.
AI.conclusion: CORP demonstrates effective one-shot, post-training structured pruning for Vision Transformers by formulating a representation-recovery objective and providing closed-form ridge-regression solutions, enabling fast, label-free pruning with preserved accuracy under high sparsity.

title: ShapeUP: Scalable Image-Conditioned 3D Editing
summary: Recent advancements in 3D foundation models have enabled the generation of high-fidelity assets, yet precise 3D manipulation remains a significant challenge. Existing 3D editing frameworks often face a difficult trade-off between visual controllability, geometric consistency, and scalability. Specifically, optimization-based methods are prohibitively slow, multi-view 2D propagation techniques suffer from visual drift, and training-free latent manipulation methods are inherently bound by frozen priors and cannot directly benefit from scaling. In this work, we present ShapeUP, a scalable, image-conditioned 3D editing framework that formulates editing as a supervised latent-to-latent translation within a native 3D representation. This formulation allows ShapeUP to build on a pretrained 3D foundation model, leveraging its strong generative prior while adapting it to editing through supervised training. In practice, ShapeUP is trained on triplets consisting of a source 3D shape, an edited 2D image, and the corresponding edited 3D shape, and learns a direct mapping using a 3D Diffusion Transformer (DiT). This image-as-prompt approach enables fine-grained visual control over both local and global edits and achieves implicit, mask-free localization, while maintaining strict structural consistency with the original asset. Our extensive evaluations demonstrate that ShapeUP consistently outperforms current trained and training-free baselines in both identity preservation and edit fidelity, offering a robust and scalable paradigm for native 3D content creation.

AI.tldr: ShapeUP introduces a scalable image-conditioned 3D editing framework that uses supervised latent-to-latent translation within a native 3D representation, trained on triplets (source shape, edited 2D image, edited shape) via a 3D Diffusion Transformer to achieve precise, geometry-preserving edits with local/global control.
AI.motivation: To overcome trade-offs among visual control, geometric consistency, and scalability in 3D editing; previous optimization-based methods are slow, 2D multi-view propagation drifts, and latent-manipulation methods are limited by frozen priors and lack of scalability.
AI.method: Train a 3D Diffusion Transformer to map from latent representations conditioned on the edited 2D image to edited 3D shapes, using triplets; image-as-prompt enables fine-grained control and implicit mask-free localization; operates on a 3D foundation model's latent space; maintains geometric consistency.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show ShapeUP outperforms both trained and training-free baselines in identity preservation and edit fidelity, offering a robust and scalable approach for native 3D content creation.
AI.conclusion: ShapeUP provides a scalable, image-conditioned 3D editing paradigm that leverages a 3D foundation model through supervised latent translation to enable precise edits with strong structural fidelity.

title: DyTopo: Dynamic Topology Routing for Multi-Agent Reasoning via Semantic Matching
summary: Multi-agent systems built from prompted large language models can improve multi-round reasoning, yet most existing pipelines rely on fixed, trajectory-wide communication patterns that are poorly matched to the stage-dependent needs of iterative problem solving. We introduce DyTopo, a manager-guided multi-agent framework that reconstructs a sparse directed communication graph at each round. Conditioned on the manager's round goal, each agent outputs lightweight natural-language query (need) and \key (offer) descriptors; DyTopo embeds these descriptors and performs semantic matching, routing private messages only along the induced edges. Across code generation and mathematical reasoning benchmarks and four LLM backbones, DyTopo consistently outperforms over the strongest baseline (avg. +6.2). Beyond accuracy, DyTopo yields an interpretable coordination trace via the evolving graphs, enabling qualitative inspection of how communication pathways reconfigure across rounds.

AI.tldr: DyTopo introduces a manager-guided, dynamic sparse communication graph for multi-agent LLM systems. Each round, agents emit need (query) and key (offer) descriptors; a manager guides the round goal, enabling semantic matching to route private messages along induced edges. Across codegen and math reasoning tasks with four backbones, it achieves about +6.2 average improvement over strong baselines and provides interpretable, evolving coordination graphs.
AI.motivation: Current multi-agent LLM pipelines use fixed, trajectory-wide communication patterns that fail to adapt to stage-specific needs in iterative problem solving. A dynamic, goal-driven routing mechanism could improve accuracy and interpretability by aligning messaging with current tasks and rounds.
AI.method: In each round, a manager defines a round goal. Each agent outputs lightweight natural-language queries (needs) and offers (keys). DyTopo embeds these descriptors and performs semantic matching to determine directed edges; private messages are routed only along these edges. The communication graph is reconstructed at every round, producing an interpretable trace of coordination as the graph evolves across rounds.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on code generation and mathematical reasoning benchmarks across four LLM backbones shows DyTopo consistently outperforming the strongest baseline by an average of 6.2 points. In addition to improved accuracy, the framework yields an interpretable coordination trace via evolving graphs that can be qualitatively inspected to understand dynamic communication pathways.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, manager-guided sparse graphs enable stage-aware, interpretable coordination in multi-agent LLM systems and generalize across tasks and backbones. DyTopo’s per-round graph reconstruction and semantic routing offer both performance gains and insights into the communication structure of collaborative reasoning.

title: TADS: Task-Aware Data Selection for Multi-Task Multimodal Pre-Training
summary: Large-scale multimodal pre-trained models like CLIP rely heavily on high-quality training data, yet raw web-crawled datasets are often noisy, misaligned, and redundant, leading to inefficient training and suboptimal generalization. Existing data selection methods are either heuristic-based, suffering from bias and limited diversity, or data-driven but task-agnostic, failing to optimize for multi-task scenarios. To address these gaps, we introduce TADS (Task-Aware Data Selection), a novel framework for multi-task multimodal pre-training that integrates Intrinsic Quality, Task Relevance, and Distributional Diversity into a learnable value function. TADS employs a comprehensive quality assessment system with unimodal and cross-modal operators, quantifies task relevance via interpretable similarity vectors, and optimizes diversity through cluster-based weighting. A feedback-driven meta-learning mechanism adaptively refines the selection strategy based on proxy model performance across multiple downstream tasks. Experiments on CC12M demonstrate that TADS achieves superior zero-shot performance on benchmarks like ImageNet, CIFAR-100, MS-COCO, and Flickr30K, using only 36% of the data while outperforming baselines by an average of 1.0%. This highlights that TADS significantly enhances data efficiency by curating a high-utility subset that yields a much higher performance ceiling within the same computational constraints.

AI.tldr: Task-Aware Data Selection (TADS): a framework for multi-task multimodal pre-training that jointly optimizes intrinsic data quality, task relevance, and distributional diversity via a learnable value function, refined by meta-learning, achieving data-efficient improvements on CC12M across several benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Web-scale multimodal data are noisy, misaligned, and redundant, and existing data-selection methods are either heuristic and biased or task-agnostic. There is a need for a task-aware, multi-task data selection strategy to improve data efficiency and generalization.
AI.method: TADS computes Intrinsic Quality using unimodal and cross-modal operators; estimates Task Relevance with interpretable similarity vectors; enforces Distributional Diversity via cluster-based weighting; uses a feedback-driven meta-learning loop to adapt the selection policy using proxy-task performance across multiple downstream tasks.
AI.result: On CC12M, TADS yields superior zero-shot performance on ImageNet, CIFAR-100, MS-COCO, and Flickr30K, using only 36% of the data and outperforming baselines by about 1.0% on average, indicating a notable increase in data efficiency and a higher performance ceiling under the same computational budget.
AI.conclusion: Task-aware data curation via TADS significantly improves data efficiency for multi-task multimodal pre-training, enabling higher performance with substantially less data and highlighting the value of integrating quality, relevance, and diversity through a learnable, feedback-driven framework.

title: Poster: Camera Tampering Detection for Outdoor IoT Systems
comment: Proceedings of the 2024 INTERNATIONAL CONFERENCE ON EMBEDDED WIRELESS SYSTEMS AND NETWORKS (EWSN)
summary: Recently, the use of smart cameras in outdoor settings has grown to improve surveillance and security. Nonetheless, these systems are susceptible to tampering, whether from deliberate vandalism or harsh environmental conditions, which can undermine their monitoring effectiveness. In this context, detecting camera tampering is more challenging when a camera is capturing still images rather than video as there is no sequence of continuous frames over time. In this study, we propose two approaches for detecting tampered images: a rule-based method and a deep-learning-based method. The aim is to evaluate how each method performs in terms of accuracy, computational demands, and the data required for training when applied to real-world scenarios. Our results show that the deep-learning model provides higher accuracy, while the rule-based method is more appropriate for scenarios where resources are limited and a prolonged calibration phase is impractical. We also offer publicly available datasets with normal, blurred, and rotated images to support the development and evaluation of camera tampering detection methods, addressing the need for such resources.

AI.tldr: Two approaches for detecting tampered still images from outdoor cameras: a rule-based method and a deep-learning method. Deep learning achieves higher accuracy; rule-based is more resource-efficient and better when calibration is limited. Public datasets (normal/blurred/rotated) are released to support benchmarking.
AI.motivation: Outdoor smart cameras are vulnerable to tampering (vandalism, harsh environments). Detecting tampering is harder with still images due to lack of temporal frames. The work aims to compare approaches in accuracy, computational cost, and data requirements, and to provide datasets to support evaluation.
AI.method: Two detection approaches are developed: (1) a rule-based method using handcrafted features/heuristics; (2) a deep-learning-based method (likely CNN/NN). The study evaluates both on real-world scenarios focusing on accuracy, computational demands, and training data requirements. Public datasets containing normal, blurred, and rotated images are released.
AI.result: Deep-learning model achieves higher accuracy in tampering detection on still images; rule-based method is more suitable for resource-constrained scenarios and when long calibration is impractical due to lower computational demands and simpler setup. Public datasets with normal, blurred, and rotated images are provided to facilitate benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: There is a trade-off between accuracy and resource use. Deep learning offers better performance for tamper detection in still images, while rule-based approaches remain viable for low-resource deployments. The released datasets will help standardize evaluation and spur further research.

title: Denoising diffusion networks for normative modeling in neuroimaging
summary: Normative modeling estimates reference distributions of biological measures conditional on covariates, enabling centiles and clinically interpretable deviation scores to be derived. Most neuroimaging pipelines fit one model per imaging-derived phenotype (IDP), which scales well but discards multivariate dependence that may encode coordinated patterns. We propose denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) as a unified conditional density estimator for tabular IDPs, from which univariate centiles and deviation scores are derived by sampling. We utilise two denoiser backbones: (i) a feature-wise linear modulation (FiLM) conditioned multilayer perceptron (MLP) and (ii) a tabular transformer with feature self-attention and intersample attention (SAINT), conditioning covariates through learned embeddings. We evaluate on a synthetic benchmark with heteroscedastic and multimodal age effects and on UK Biobank FreeSurfer phenotypes, scaling from dimension of 2 to 200. Our evaluation suite includes centile calibration (absolute centile error, empirical coverage, and the probability integral transform), distributional fidelity (Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests), multivariate dependence diagnostics, and nearest-neighbour memorisation analysis. For low dimensions, diffusion models deliver well-calibrated per-IDP outputs comparable to traditional baselines while jointly modeling realistic dependence structure. At higher dimensions, the transformer backbone remains substantially better calibrated than the MLP and better preserves higher-order dependence, enabling scalable joint normative models that remain compatible with standard per-IDP pipelines. These results support diffusion-based normative modeling as a practical route to calibrated multivariate deviation profiles in neuroimaging.

AI.tldr: Diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) as a unified conditional density estimator for tabular neuroimaging phenotypes (IDPs), enabling multivariate normative modeling that preserves dependence; compares FiLM-MLP and SAINT backbones; transformer-based model better calibrates at higher dimensions; supports centile-based deviation scores.
AI.motivation: Standard normative modeling fits one model per IDP, losing multivariate dependencies; need scalable multivariate conditional density modeling for neuroimaging data.
AI.method: Train DDPMs conditioned on covariates using two backbones: (i) FiLM-conditioned MLP; (ii) SAINT transformer with feature self-attention and intersample attention; embeddings encode covariates. Evaluate on synthetic data (heteroscedastic/multimodal age effects) and UK Biobank FreeSurfer phenotypes (dim 2-200). Derive centiles and deviation scores via sampling. Assess centile calibration (absolute CE, empirical coverage, pIT), distributional fidelity (KS tests), multivariate dependence diagnostics, and nearest-neighbour memorisation.
AI.result: DDPMs yield well-calibrated per-IDP outputs at low dimensions comparable to baselines; at higher dimensions, SAINT backbone yields substantially better calibration and preserves higher-order dependence, enabling scalable joint normative models compatible with standard pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based normative modeling offers a practical path to calibrated multivariate deviation profiles in neuroimaging; transformer-backed DDPMs scale to higher dimensions and maintain dependence structure, supporting integrated, multivariate normative analyses.

title: Hybrid Gated Flow (HGF): Stabilizing 1.58-bit LLMs via Selective Low-Rank Correction
comment: 21 pages, 4 figures, 6 tables. Code and models will be released at opencores.ai
summary:
The deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) on edge devices is fundamentally constrained by the "Memory Wall" -- a hardware limitation where memory bandwidth, not compute, becomes the bottleneck. Recent 1.58-bit quantization techniques (e.g., BitNet b1.58) dramatically reduce memory footprint but typically incur a perplexity degradation of 20-25% compared to FP16 baselines. In this work, we introduce Hybrid Gated Flow (HGF), a dual-stream architecture that couples a 1.58-bit ternary backbone with a learnable, low-rank FP16 correction path controlled by adaptive gates.
  Through extensive experiments on the TinyStories dataset across two training regimes (2500 and 3500 steps), we demonstrate that HGF 5.4 achieves a validation loss of 0.9306 compared to BitNet's 1.0294, recovering approximately 55% of the quality gap between pure ternary quantization and the FP16 baseline (0.8490). This recovery is achieved with only ~12-15% memory overhead beyond the ternary backbone.
  Furthermore, we provide empirical evidence for an emergent phenomenon: quantization as structural regularization. While a full-precision differential attention baseline (Diff_Only) exhibited training instability with validation loss exceeding 1.68, the ternary-anchored HGF maintained robust convergence throughout training. Finally, we report preliminary results extending this architecture to 1.2B and 3B parameter models trained on SlimPajama and FineWeb-Edu. These larger-scale experiments confirm that the architectural stability and quality recovery observed in small-scale proxies scale linearly to production-grade language modeling regimes.

AI.tldr: Hybrid Gated Flow (HGF) combines a 1.58-bit ternary backbone with a learnable FP16 correction path via adaptive gates to cut memory while recovering part of the quantization loss. It shows improved validation loss on TinyStories over BitNet, with modest memory overhead and evidence that quantization acts as structural regularization, scaling to 1.2B–3B models.
AI.motivation: Memory bandwidth, not compute, is the bottleneck for edge deployment of LLMs; 1.58-bit quantization saves memory but hurts perplexity. A method that preserves quality with low overhead is highly desired.
AI.method: Proposes a dual-stream architecture: a 1.58-bit ternary backbone plus a learnable, low-rank FP16 correction path guided by adaptive gates (Hybrid Gated Flow). Trains and evaluates on TinyStories with two regimes (2500/3500 steps) and extends to 1.2B and 3B models on SlimPajama and FineWeb-Edu.
AI.result: HGF 5.4 achieves validation loss 0.9306 vs BitNet 1.0294, recovering ~55% of the quality gap between ternary quantization and FP16 baseline (0.8490). Memory overhead is ~12–15% beyond the ternary backbone. Evidence of quantization as structural regularization; Diff_Only baseline unstable, HGF converges robustly. Scale-up experiments to 1.2B and 3B parameters show similar stability and quality gains.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid Gated Flow stabilizes training and improves quality for aggressive quantization, with modest memory overhead; supports the view of quantization as a form of regularization and appears scalable to production-scale LLMs.

title: Exploring the Temporal Consistency for Point-Level Weakly-Supervised Temporal Action Localization
summary: Point-supervised Temporal Action Localization (PTAL) adopts a lightly frame-annotated paradigm (\textit{i.e.}, labeling only a single frame per action instance) to train a model to effectively locate action instances within untrimmed videos. Most existing approaches design the task head of models with only a point-supervised snippet-level classification, without explicit modeling of understanding temporal relationships among frames of an action. However, understanding the temporal relationships of frames is crucial because it can help a model understand how an action is defined and therefore benefits localizing the full frames of an action. To this end, in this paper, we design a multi-task learning framework that fully utilizes point supervision to boost the model's temporal understanding capability for action localization. Specifically, we design three self-supervised temporal understanding tasks: (i) Action Completion, (ii) Action Order Understanding, and (iii) Action Regularity Understanding. These tasks help a model understand the temporal consistency of actions across videos. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first attempt to explicitly explore temporal consistency for point supervision action localization. Extensive experimental results on four benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method compared to several state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: A multi-task, point-supervised temporal action localization framework that uses self-supervised temporal tasks to enforce temporal consistency and improve localization accuracy.
AI.motivation: Temporal relationships among frames are crucial for defining and localizing actions, but point-supervised (PTAL) methods often neglect explicit temporal modeling. The paper introduces self-supervised tasks to enhance temporal understanding under weak supervision.
AI.method: Introduce three self-supervised temporal tasks—(i) Action Completion, (ii) Action Order Understanding, (iii) Action Regularity Understanding—within a multi-task learning framework that shares representations and leverages point annotations to train a model capable of coherent temporal reasoning for localizing full action instances.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets show that incorporating temporal-consistency tasks improves localization performance, outperforming several state-of-the-art PTAL methods.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling temporal consistency via auxiliary self-supervised tasks under point supervision is effective for PTAL and represents a novel contribution to temporal reasoning in weakly supervised action localization.

title: Back to Basics: Revisiting Exploration in Reinforcement Learning for LLM Reasoning via Generative Probabilities
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as an indispensable paradigm for enhancing reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs). However, standard policy optimization methods, such as Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), often converge to low-entropy policies, leading to severe mode collapse and limited output diversity. We analyze this issue from the perspective of sampling probability dynamics, identifying that the standard objective disproportionately reinforces the highest-likelihood paths, thereby suppressing valid alternative reasoning chains. To address this, we propose a novel Advantage Re-weighting Mechanism (ARM) designed to equilibrate the confidence levels across all correct responses. By incorporating Prompt Perplexity and Answer Confidence into the advantage estimation, our method dynamically reshapes the reward signal to attenuate the gradient updates of over-confident reasoning paths, while redistributing probability mass toward under-explored correct solutions. Empirical results demonstrate that our approach significantly enhances generative diversity and response entropy while maintaining competitive accuracy, effectively achieving a superior trade-off between exploration and exploitation in reasoning tasks. Empirical results on Qwen2.5 and DeepSeek models across mathematical and coding benchmarks show that ProGRPO significantly mitigates entropy collapse. Specifically, on Qwen2.5-7B, our method outperforms GRPO by 5.7% in Pass@1 and, notably, by 13.9% in Pass@32, highlighting its superior capability in generating diverse correct reasoning paths.

AI.tldr: An entropy-collapse problem in policy optimization for LLM reasoning is identified and tackled with an Advantage Re-weighting Mechanism (ARM) that balances confidence across correct responses, improving response diversity while maintaining accuracy; empirical gains on Qwen2.5-7B and DeepSeek demonstrate superior Pass@1 and Pass@32 over GRPO.
AI.motivation: Mitigate mode collapse and low entropy in policy optimization (GRPO) when eliciting chain-of-thought-like reasoning from LLMs, by encouraging exploration of diverse correct reasoning paths rather than overemphasizing the top paths.
AI.method: Introduce Advantage Re-weighting Mechanism (ARM) within the ProGRPO framework. ARM incorporates Prompt Perplexity and Answer Confidence into the advantage estimation to damp over-confident paths and redistribute probability mass toward under-explored correct solutions, thereby balancing exploration and exploitation.
AI.result: Empirical results show increased generative diversity and response entropy with competitive accuracy. On Qwen2.5-7B, ProGRPO with ARM outperforms GRPO by 5.7% in Pass@1 and 13.9% in Pass@32, indicating enhanced diversity of correct reasoning paths.
AI.conclusion: ARM effectively mitigates entropy collapse in GRPO-based optimization, offering a better trade-off between exploration and exploitation in LLM reasoning tasks without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Adaptive Global and Fine-Grained Perceptual Fusion for MLLM Embeddings Compatible with Hard Negative Amplification
summary: Multimodal embeddings serve as a bridge for aligning vision and language, with the two primary implementations -- CLIP-based and MLLM-based embedding models -- both limited to capturing only global semantic information. Although numerous studies have focused on fine-grained understanding, we observe that complex scenarios currently targeted by MLLM embeddings often involve a hybrid perceptual pattern of both global and fine-grained elements, thus necessitating a compatible fusion mechanism. In this paper, we propose Adaptive Global and Fine-grained perceptual Fusion for MLLM Embeddings (AGFF-Embed), a method that prompts the MLLM to generate multiple embeddings focusing on different dimensions of semantic information, which are then adaptively and smoothly aggregated. Furthermore, we adapt AGFF-Embed with the Explicit Gradient Amplification (EGA) technique to achieve in-batch hard negatives enhancement without requiring fine-grained editing of the dataset. Evaluation on the MMEB and MMVP-VLM benchmarks shows that AGFF-Embed comprehensively achieves state-of-the-art performance in both general and fine-grained understanding compared to other multimodal embedding models.

AI.tldr: AGFF-Embed prompts an MLLM to produce multiple embeddings that capture different semantic dimensions, then adaptively fuses them to combine global and fine-grained information; enhanced with Explicit Gradient Amplification (EGA) for hard negatives. It achieves state-of-the-art results on MMEB and MMVP-VLM for both general and fine-grained tasks.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal embeddings (CLIP-based and MLLM-based) mostly capture global semantic information. Real-world understanding often requires balanced global and fine-grained cues, necessitating a fusion mechanism that can align multi-scale information.
AI.method: Generate multiple, dimension-specific embeddings from an MLLM via prompts; adaptively and smoothly fuse these embeddings into a joint representation; apply Explicit Gradient Amplification (EGA) to boost in-batch hard negatives without dataset edits.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on MMEB and MMVP-VLM benchmarks for both general and fine-grained understanding, outperforming other multimodal embedding models.
AI.conclusion: AGFF-Embed provides a flexible, scalable fusion framework that leverages MLLMs for multi-dimensional semantic representations and improves retrieval/recognition through adaptive fusion and hard-negative enhancement.

title: Momentum Attention: The Physics of In-Context Learning and Spectral Forensics for Mechanistic Interpretability
comment: 15 pages, 5 figures, 299 pages total with supplementary material (21 appendices, 27 Jupyter notebooks with embedded results)
summary: The Mechanistic Interpretability (MI) program has mapped the Transformer as a precise computational graph. We extend this graph with a conservation law and time-varying AC dynamics, viewing it as a physical circuit. We introduce Momentum Attention, a symplectic augmentation embedding physical priors via the kinematic difference operator $p_t = q_t - q_{t-1}$, implementing the symplectic shear $\hat{q}_t = q_t + γp_t$ on queries and keys. We identify a fundamental Symplectic-Filter Duality: the physical shear is mathematically equivalent to a High-Pass Filter. This duality is our cornerstone contribution -- by injecting kinematic momentum, we sidestep the topological depth constraint ($L \geq 2$) for induction head formation. While standard architectures require two layers for induction from static positions, our extension grants direct access to velocity, enabling Single-Layer Induction and Spectral Forensics via Bode Plots. We formalize an Orthogonality Theorem proving that DC (semantic) and AC (mechanistic) signals segregate into orthogonal frequency bands when Low-Pass RoPE interacts with High-Pass Momentum. Validated through 5,100+ controlled experiments (documented in Supplementary Appendices A--R and 27 Jupyter notebooks), our 125M Momentum model exceeds expectations on induction-heavy tasks while tracking a 350M baseline within $\sim$2.9% validation loss. Dedicated associative recall experiments reveal a scaling law $γ^* = 4.17 \times N^{-0.74}$ establishing momentum-depth fungibility. We offer this framework as a complementary analytical toolkit connecting Generative AI, Hamiltonian Physics, and Signal Processing.

AI.tldr: Introduces Momentum Attention, a symplectic, momentum-based augmentation to Transformer graphs, positing a Symplectic-Filter Duality that equates physical shear with a high-pass filter; claims single-layer induction, spectral forensics, and scalable momentum-depth fungibility, validated by thousands of experiments with a 125M model near a 350M baseline (~2.9% diff).
AI.motivation: Bridge mechanistic interpretability with physical priors and signal-processing perspectives; overcome depth constraints on induction heads and enable direct access to velocity information within Transformers.
AI.method: Extend the Transformer computational graph with momentum p_t = q_t - q_{t-1}; apply a symplectic shear on queries/keys (q̂_t = q_t + γ p_t); formalize a Symplectic-Filter Duality (physical shear ≡ high-pass filter); propose an Orthogonality Theorem that DC (semantic) and AC (mechanistic) signals occupy orthogonal frequency bands when Low-Pass RoPE interacts with High-Pass Momentum; validate via 5,100+ controlled experiments, 125M Momentum model vs 350M baseline; derive scaling law γ* = 4.17 × N^(-0.74) for momentum-depth fungibility; present as a framework linking Generative AI, Hamiltonian physics, and signal processing.
AI.result: Demonstrated access to velocity enabling Single-Layer Induction and spectral forensics; competitive performance on induction-heavy tasks; momentum augmentation reduces depth constraints; strong empirical support across large experiments; scaling law with momentum-depth fungibility.
AI.conclusion: Offers a complementary analytical toolkit aligning Generative AI with Hamiltonian physics and signal processing; momentum-based symplectic augmentation provides novel interpretability and potential efficiency gains, broadening the mechanistic interpretability program.

title: Robust Inference-Time Steering of Protein Diffusion Models via Embedding Optimization
summary: In many biophysical inverse problems, the goal is to generate biomolecular conformations that are both physically plausible and consistent with experimental measurements. As recent sequence-to-structure diffusion models provide powerful data-driven priors, posterior sampling has emerged as a popular framework by guiding atomic coordinates to target conformations using experimental likelihoods. However, when the target lies in a low-density region of the prior, posterior sampling requires aggressive and brittle weighting of the likelihood guidance. Motivated by this limitation, we propose EmbedOpt, an alternative inference-time approach for steering diffusion models to optimize experimental likelihoods in the conditional embedding space. As this space encodes rich sequence and coevolutionary signals, optimizing over it effectively shifts the diffusion prior to align with experimental constraints. We validate EmbedOpt on two benchmarks simulating cryo-electron microscopy map fitting and experimental distance constraints. We show that EmbedOpt outperforms the coordinate-based posterior sampling method in map fitting tasks, matches performance on distance constraint tasks, and exhibits superior engineering robustness across hyperparameters spanning two orders of magnitude. Moreover, its smooth optimization behavior enables a significant reduction in the number of diffusion steps required for inference, leading to better efficiency.

AI.tldr: EmbedOpt introduces an inference-time method that optimizes experimental likelihoods in the conditional embedding space of diffusion models for biomolecular structure inference, achieving better map-fitting accuracy, comparable distance-constraint performance, greater hyperparameter robustness, and higher efficiency due to fewer diffusion steps.
AI.motivation: Biophysical inverse problems require conformations that are both physically plausible and consistent with experimental data. Posterior sampling using data-driven priors struggles when targets lie in low-density regions, needing brittle, high-variance likelihood weighting.
AI.method: Develop EmbedOpt, an inference-time technique that steers diffusion models in the conditional embedding space, leveraging rich sequence and coevolutionary signals to align priors with experimental constraints. Validate on cryo-EM map fitting and distance constraints benchmarks; compare to coordinate-based posterior sampling; assess robustness across hyperparameters and inference efficiency.
AI.result: EmbedOpt outperforms coordinate-based posterior sampling for map fitting; matches distance-constraint performance; exhibits strong robustness across hyperparameters (two orders of magnitude); reduces required diffusion steps, improving efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Operating in embedding space enables effective alignment of the diffusion prior with experimental constraints, delivering robust, efficient posterior inference for biomolecular structure tasks.

title: Depth as Prior Knowledge for Object Detection
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Detecting small and distant objects remains challenging for object detectors due to scale variation, low resolution, and background clutter. Safety-critical applications require reliable detection of these objects for safe planning. Depth information can improve detection, but existing approaches require complex, model-specific architectural modifications. We provide a theoretical analysis followed by an empirical investigation of the depth-detection relationship. Together, they explain how depth causes systematic performance degradation and why depth-informed supervision mitigates it. We introduce DepthPrior, a framework that uses depth as prior knowledge rather than as a fused feature, providing comparable benefits without modifying detector architectures. DepthPrior consists of Depth-Based Loss Weighting (DLW) and Depth-Based Loss Stratification (DLS) during training, and Depth-Aware Confidence Thresholding (DCT) during inference. The only overhead is the initial cost of depth estimation. Experiments across four benchmarks (KITTI, MS COCO, VisDrone, SUN RGB-D) and two detectors (YOLOv11, EfficientDet) demonstrate the effectiveness of DepthPrior, achieving up to +9% mAP$_S$ and +7% mAR$_S$ for small objects, with inference recovery rates as high as 95:1 (true vs. false detections). DepthPrior offers these benefits without additional sensors, architectural changes, or performance costs. Code is available at https://github.com/mos-ks/DepthPrior.

AI.tldr: DepthPrior treats depth as prior knowledge rather than a fused feature to improve small-object detection without architectural changes. It uses depth-based loss weighting and stratification during training and depth-aware confidence thresholding during inference, with depth estimation overhead only.
AI.motivation: Small and distant objects suffer from scale variation, low resolution, and clutter. Depth information can help, but previous methods require complex, model-specific architecture changes. A theory + empirical study clarifies depth–detection dynamics and enables a practical, architecture-agnostic solution.
AI.method: DepthPrior comprises three components: (1) Depth-Based Loss Weighting (DLW) that weights training losses by depth; (2) Depth-Based Loss Stratification (DLS) that stratifies samples by depth for tailored optimization; (3) Depth-Aware Confidence Thresholding (DCT) at inference to adjust detections using depth cues. Depth estimation is the only additional cost.
AI.result: On KITTI, MS COCO, VisDrone, and SUN RGB-D using YOLOv11 and EfficientDet, DepthPrior achieves up to +9% mAP_S and +7% mAR_S for small objects, with inference recovery rates up to 95:1 (true vs. false detections). It provides these gains without sensor upgrades, architectural changes, or extra inference cost beyond depth estimation, and code is released.
AI.conclusion: DepthPrior confirms that depth can be leveraged as a prior to mitigate depth-induced degradation in small-object detection, delivering substantial improvements across diverse datasets and detectors without modifying detector architectures.

title: HealthMamba: An Uncertainty-aware Spatiotemporal Graph State Space Model for Effective and Reliable Healthcare Facility Visit Prediction
summary: Healthcare facility visit prediction is essential for optimizing healthcare resource allocation and informing public health policy. Despite advanced machine learning methods being employed for better prediction performance, existing works usually formulate this task as a time-series forecasting problem without considering the intrinsic spatial dependencies of different types of healthcare facilities, and they also fail to provide reliable predictions under abnormal situations such as public emergencies. To advance existing research, we propose HealthMamba, an uncertainty-aware spatiotemporal framework for accurate and reliable healthcare facility visit prediction. HealthMamba comprises three key components: (i) a Unified Spatiotemporal Context Encoder that fuses heterogeneous static and dynamic information, (ii) a novel Graph State Space Model called GraphMamba for hierarchical spatiotemporal modeling, and (iii) a comprehensive uncertainty quantification module integrating three uncertainty quantification mechanisms for reliable prediction. We evaluate HealthMamba on four large-scale real-world datasets from California, New York, Texas, and Florida. Results show HealthMamba achieves around 6.0% improvement in prediction accuracy and 3.5% improvement in uncertainty quantification over state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: HealthMamba: an uncertainty-aware spatiotemporal framework for predicting healthcare facility visits, integrating a unified context encoder, GraphMamba, and a three-source uncertainty quantification module; reports ~6% accuracy gain and ~3.5% better UQ on four large real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: To address spatial dependencies among facility types and unreliable predictions during abnormal situations; bridging gaps in time-series forecasting approaches for healthcare visits.
AI.method: Unified Spatiotemporal Context Encoder; GraphMamba Graph State Space Model for hierarchical spatiotemporal modeling; uncertainty quantification module integrating three mechanisms.
AI.result: On California, New York, Texas, Florida datasets, HealthMamba achieves ~6.0% improvement in prediction accuracy and ~3.5% improvement in uncertainty quantification over state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: HealthMamba yields more accurate and reliable visit predictions, supporting efficient resource allocation and public health decision making; suitable for deployment in real-world heterogeneous settings.

title: Neuro-Inspired Visual Pattern Recognition via Biological Reservoir Computing
summary: In this paper, we present a neuro-inspired approach to reservoir computing (RC) in which a network of in vitro cultured cortical neurons serves as the physical reservoir. Rather than relying on artificial recurrent models to approximate neural dynamics, our biological reservoir computing (BRC) system leverages the spontaneous and stimulus-evoked activity of living neural circuits as its computational substrate. A high-density multi-electrode array (HD-MEA) provides simultaneous stimulation and readout across hundreds of channels: input patterns are delivered through selected electrodes, while the remaining ones capture the resulting high-dimensional neural responses, yielding a biologically grounded feature representation. A linear readout layer (single-layer perceptron) is then trained to classify these reservoir states, enabling the living neural network to perform static visual pattern-recognition tasks within a computer-vision framework. We evaluate the system across a sequence of tasks of increasing difficulty, ranging from pointwise stimuli to oriented bars, clock-digit-like shapes, and handwritten digits from the MNIST dataset. Despite the inherent variability of biological neural responses-arising from noise, spontaneous activity, and inter-session differences-the system consistently generates high-dimensional representations that support accurate classification. These results demonstrate that in vitro cortical networks can function as effective reservoirs for static visual pattern recognition, opening new avenues for integrating living neural substrates into neuromorphic computing frameworks. More broadly, this work contributes to the effort to incorporate biological principles into machine learning and supports the goals of neuro-inspired vision by illustrating how living neural systems can inform the design of efficient and biologically grounded computational models.

AI.tldr: Biologically grounded reservoir computing: living cortical networks act as the computational reservoir for static visual pattern recognition, using a high-density multi-electrode array for input/output and a linear readout; achieves accurate classification on tasks from simple stimuli to MNIST despite biological variability.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether living neural tissue can serve as a computational reservoir, leveraging intrinsic dynamics for neuromorphic vision and integrating biological principles into ML.
AI.method: In vitro cultured cortical neurons form a reservoir. Input patterns are delivered via selected electrodes on a dense MEA; hundreds of channels capture the resultant high-dimensional reservoir states. A linear readout (single-layer perceptron) is trained to classify these states for static visual tasks, progressing from pointwise stimuli to oriented bars, clock-digit-like shapes, and MNIST digits.
AI.result: The living neural reservoir produces high-dimensional representations that enable accurate classification across tasks, maintaining performance despite biological variability such as noise, spontaneous activity, and inter-session differences.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that in vitro cortical networks can function as effective reservoirs for static visual pattern recognition, supporting the integration of living substrates into neuromorphic computing and informing biologically grounded machine vision models.

title: FMPose3D: monocular 3D pose estimation via flow matching
summary: Monocular 3D pose estimation is fundamentally ill-posed due to depth ambiguity and occlusions, thereby motivating probabilistic methods that generate multiple plausible 3D pose hypotheses. In particular, diffusion-based models have recently demonstrated strong performance, but their iterative denoising process typically requires many timesteps for each prediction, making inference computationally expensive. In contrast, we leverage Flow Matching (FM) to learn a velocity field defined by an Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE), enabling efficient generation of 3D pose samples with only a few integration steps. We propose a novel generative pose estimation framework, FMPose3D, that formulates 3D pose estimation as a conditional distribution transport problem. It continuously transports samples from a standard Gaussian prior to the distribution of plausible 3D poses conditioned only on 2D inputs. Although ODE trajectories are deterministic, FMPose3D naturally generates various pose hypotheses by sampling different noise seeds. To obtain a single accurate prediction from those hypotheses, we further introduce a Reprojection-based Posterior Expectation Aggregation (RPEA) module, which approximates the Bayesian posterior expectation over 3D hypotheses. FMPose3D surpasses existing methods on the widely used human pose estimation benchmarks Human3.6M and MPI-INF-3DHP, and further achieves state-of-the-art performance on the 3D animal pose datasets Animal3D and CtrlAni3D, demonstrating strong performance across both 3D pose domains. The code is available at https://github.com/AdaptiveMotorControlLab/FMPose3D.

AI.tldr: FMPose3D uses Flow Matching to learn an ODE-based velocity field that transports Gaussian samples into a conditioned 3D-pose distribution, enabling fast, multi-hypothesis monocular 3D pose estimation; it introduces a Reprojection-based Posterior Expectation Aggregation (RPEA) to fuse hypotheses, achieving state-of-the-art results on both human and animal pose datasets; code released.
AI.motivation: Monocular 3D pose estimation is inherently ill-posed due to depth ambiguity and occlusions, which motivates probabilistic approaches that produce multiple plausible 3D pose hypotheses. Diffusion-based methods, while strong, are computationally expensive due to iterative denoising steps; there is a need for faster generation of diverse poses.
AI.method: Formulates 3D pose estimation as a conditional distribution transport using Flow Matching to define a velocity field via an ODE. Samples are transported from a standard Gaussian prior to the distribution of plausible 3D poses conditioned on 2D inputs. Sampling noise seeds yields diverse pose hypotheses; a Reprojection-based Posterior Expectation Aggregation (RPEA) module approximates the Bayesian posterior expectation over these hypotheses to produce a single accurate prediction.
AI.result: FMPose3D outperforms existing methods on Human3.6M and MPI-INF-3DHP, and achieves state-of-the-art results on Animal3D and CtrlAni3D, demonstrating strong performance across both human and animal 3D pose domains.
AI.conclusion: The paper introduces an efficient, probabilistic 3D pose estimation framework that combines flow-matching–based velocity-field transport with an RPEA aggregation to produce accurate predictions from multiple hypotheses, offering faster inference than diffusion-based approaches and strong cross-domain performance.

title: ReText: Text Boosts Generalization in Image-Based Person Re-identification
summary: Generalizable image-based person re-identification (Re-ID) aims to recognize individuals across cameras in unseen domains without retraining. While multiple existing approaches address the domain gap through complex architectures, recent findings indicate that better generalization can be achieved by stylistically diverse single-camera data. Although this data is easy to collect, it lacks complexity due to minimal cross-view variation. We propose ReText, a novel method trained on a mixture of multi-camera Re-ID data and single-camera data, where the latter is complemented by textual descriptions to enrich semantic cues. During training, ReText jointly optimizes three tasks: (1) Re-ID on multi-camera data, (2) image-text matching, and (3) image reconstruction guided by text on single-camera data. Experiments demonstrate that ReText achieves strong generalization and significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on cross-domain Re-ID benchmarks. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to explore multimodal joint learning on a mixture of multi-camera and single-camera data in image-based person Re-ID.

AI.tldr: ReText combines multi-camera Re-ID data with single-camera data augmented by textual descriptions; jointly optimizes Re-ID, image-text matching, and text-guided image reconstruction to improve cross-domain generalization.
AI.motivation: Generalization in image-based person Re-ID across unseen domains is hampered by domain gaps. Existing approaches rely on complex architectures; leveraging stylistically diverse single-camera data with rich textual semantics can enrich representations and enhance cross-domain performance.
AI.method: Train on a mixture of multi-camera Re-ID data and single-camera data complemented with text descriptions. Jointly optimize three tasks: (1) Re-ID on multi-camera data, (2) image-text matching, (3) text-guided image reconstruction on single-camera data.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate strong cross-domain generalization, with ReText outperforming state-of-the-art methods on cross-domain Re-ID benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: This work introduces multimodal joint learning on a mixture of multi-camera and single-camera data for image-based Re-ID, establishing a first step toward leveraging textual semantics to boost generalization in cross-camera settings.

title: Accelerated Sequential Flow Matching: A Bayesian Filtering Perspective
summary: Sequential prediction from streaming observations is a fundamental problem in stochastic dynamical systems, where inherent uncertainty often leads to multiple plausible futures. While diffusion and flow-matching models are capable of modeling complex, multi-modal trajectories, their deployment in real-time streaming environments typically relies on repeated sampling from a non-informative initial distribution, incurring substantial inference latency and potential system backlogs. In this work, we introduce Sequential Flow Matching, a principled framework grounded in Bayesian filtering. By treating streaming inference as learning a probability flow that transports the predictive distribution from one time step to the next, our approach naturally aligns with the recursive structure of Bayesian belief updates. We provide theoretical justification that initializing generation from the previous posterior offers a principled warm start that can accelerate sampling compared to naïve re-sampling. Across a wide range of forecasting, decision-making and state estimation tasks, our method achieves performance competitive with full-step diffusion while requiring only one or very few sampling steps, therefore with faster sampling. It suggests that framing sequential inference via Bayesian filtering provides a new and principled perspective towards efficient real-time deployment of flow-based models.

AI.tldr: Bayesian-filtering–inspired Sequential Flow Matching enables fast, near-diffusion-quality sequential forecasting by learning a probability flow that updates the predictive distribution from step to step, with a principled warm start from the previous posterior.
AI.motivation: In streaming settings, diffusion/flow-matching models require repeated sampling from a non-informative initial distribution, causing high inference latency and potential backlogs in real-time systems dealing with multi-modal futures.
AI.method: Proposes Sequential Flow Matching, a framework that treats streaming inference as learning a probability flow to transport the predictive distribution between consecutive time steps, aligned with Bayesian belief updates. Provides theoretical justification that starting from the previous posterior is a principled warm start.
AI.result: Empirically, the method achieves performance competitive with full-step diffusion while requiring only one or very few sampling steps, across forecasting, decision-making, and state estimation tasks, enabling faster sampling in real-time settings.
AI.conclusion: Framing sequential inference via Bayesian filtering offers a new, principled perspective for efficient real-time deployment of flow-based models, enabling fast, multi-step forecasting with minimal sampling.

title: Allocentric Perceiver: Disentangling Allocentric Reasoning from Egocentric Visual Priors via Frame Instantiation
summary: With the rising need for spatially grounded tasks such as Vision-Language Navigation/Action, allocentric perception capabilities in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are receiving growing focus.
