It provides near-tight characterizations (up to polylog factors) of tail probabilities for the gradient norm of the best iterate, highlighting regimes where strong long-horizon guarantees hold. The results rely on large deviations techniques and clarify how noise structure (bounded vs heavy-tailed) affects tail behavior; practical implications include improved confidence in long-run SGD performance, though constants and model assumptions warrant careful empirical validation.

title: Probabilistic Multi-Regional Solar Power Forecasting with Any-Quantile Recurrent Neural Networks
summary:
The increasing penetration of photovoltaic (PV) generation introduces significant uncertainty into power system operation, necessitating forecasting approaches that extend beyond deterministic point predictions. This paper proposes an any-quantile probabilistic forecasting framework for multi-regional PV power generation based on the Any-Quantile Recurrent Neural Network (AQ-RNN). The model integrates an any-quantile forecasting paradigm with a dual-track recurrent architecture that jointly processes series-specific and cross-regional contextual information, supported by dilated recurrent cells, patch-based temporal modeling, and a dynamic ensemble mechanism.
  The proposed framework enables the estimation of calibrated conditional quantiles at arbitrary probability levels within a single trained model and effectively exploits spatial dependencies to enhance robustness at the system level. The approach is evaluated using 30 years of hourly PV generation data from 259 European regions and compared against established statistical and neural probabilistic baselines. The results demonstrate consistent improvements in forecast accuracy, calibration, and prediction interval quality, underscoring the suitability of the proposed method for uncertainty-aware energy management and operational decision-making in renewable-dominated power systems.

AI.tldr: AQ-RNN-based any-quantile probabilistic forecasting for multi-regional PV; dual-track RNN with dilated cells, patch-based temporal modeling, and dynamic ensembles; single model yields calibrated quantiles across probabilities; evaluated on 30 years, 259 European regions; shows improved accuracy, calibration, and interval quality over baselines; suitable for uncertainty-aware energy management.
AI.motivation: PV expansion increases uncertainty; need probabilistic forecasts beyond point estimates; multi-regional context exploits spatial dependencies to improve system-level robustness.
AI.method: Any-Quantile Recurrent Neural Network (AQ-RNN) with dual-track architecture processing series-specific and cross-regional context; dilated recurrent cells; patch-based temporal modeling; dynamic ensemble; single trained model provides conditional quantiles at arbitrary probability levels.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in forecast accuracy, calibration, and prediction interval quality compared to statistical and neural probabilistic baselines; validated on 30-year hourly PV data from 259 European regions.
AI.conclusion: Framework is suitable for uncertainty-aware energy management in renewable-dominated systems; demonstrates effective exploitation of spatial dependencies; scalable for regional forecasting under uncertainty.

title: Accelerating Benchmarking of Functional Connectivity Modeling via Structure-aware Core-set Selection
summary: Benchmarking the hundreds of functional connectivity (FC) modeling methods on large-scale fMRI datasets is critical for reproducible neuroscience. However, the combinatorial explosion of model-data pairings makes exhaustive evaluation computationally prohibitive, preventing such assessments from becoming a routine pre-analysis step. To break this bottleneck, we reframe the challenge of FC benchmarking by selecting a small, representative core-set whose sole purpose is to preserve the relative performance ranking of FC operators. We formalize this as a ranking-preserving subset selection problem and propose Structure-aware Contrastive Learning for Core-set Selection (SCLCS), a self-supervised framework to select these core-sets. SCLCS first uses an adaptive Transformer to learn each sample's unique FC structure. It then introduces a novel Structural Perturbation Score (SPS) to quantify the stability of these learned structures during training, identifying samples that represent foundational connectivity archetypes. Finally, while SCLCS identifies stable samples via a top-k ranking, we further introduce a density-balanced sampling strategy as a necessary correction to promote diversity, ensuring the final core-set is both structurally robust and distributionally representative. On the large-scale REST-meta-MDD dataset, SCLCS preserves the ground-truth model ranking with just 10% of the data, outperforming state-of-the-art (SOTA) core-set selection methods by up to 23.2% in ranking consistency (nDCG@k). To our knowledge, this is the first work to formalize core-set selection for FC operator benchmarking, thereby making large-scale operators comparisons a feasible and integral part of computational neuroscience. Code is publicly available on https://github.com/lzhan94swu/SCLCS

AI.tldr: Introduces SCLCS, a self-supervised method to select a small core-set of fMRI functional connectivity samples that preserves the relative ranking of FC operators. Uses an adaptive Transformer to encode structure, a Structural Perturbation Score to gauge stability, and density-balanced sampling to ensure diversity. Achieves ranking preservation with only 10% data on REST-meta-MDD, outperforming SOTA by up to 23.2% in ranking consistency (nDCG@k); enables scalable benchmarking with public code.
AI.motivation: Benchmarking hundreds of FC modeling methods on large-scale fMRI data is computationally prohibitive. A small, representative core-set that preserves the relative performance ranking would make large-scale operator benchmarking feasible and reproducible.
AI.method: A self-supervised framework (SCLCS): (1) adaptive Transformer to learn each sample's unique FC structure; (2) Structural Perturbation Score (SPS) to measure stability of learned structures during training; (3) top-k ranking to identify stable samples; (4) density-balanced sampling to promote diversity, forming a representative core-set; (5) evaluation on REST-meta-MDD dataset to check ranking preservation vs full-data ground-truth.
AI.result: Core-set of ~10% of data preserves ground-truth operator ranking; achieves up to 23.2% improvement in ranking consistency (nDCG@k) over state-of-the-art core-set methods on REST-meta-MDD.
AI.conclusion: Formalizes core-set selection for FC operator benchmarking for large-scale comparisons; demonstrates reproducible, cost-efficient benchmarking; code released publicly.

title: Stable but Wrong: When More Data Degrades Scientific Conclusions
summary: Modern science increasingly relies on ever-growing observational datasets and automated inference pipelines, under the implicit belief that accumulating more data makes scientific conclusions more reliable. Here we show that this belief can fail in a fundamental and irreversible way. We identify a structural regime in which standard inference procedures converge smoothly, remain well calibrated, and pass conventional diagnostic checks, yet systematically converge to incorrect conclusions. This failure arises when the reliability of observations degrades in a manner that is intrinsically unobservable to the inference process itself. Using minimal synthetic experiments, we demonstrate that in this regime additional data do not correct error but instead amplify it, while residual-based and goodness-of-fit diagnostics remain misleadingly normal. These results reveal an intrinsic limit of data-driven science: stability, convergence, and confidence are not sufficient indicators of epistemic validity. We argue that inference cannot be treated as an unconditional consequence of data availability, but must instead be governed by explicit constraints on the integrity of the observational process.

AI.tldr: More data can worsen conclusions in a regime where observation reliability degrades in a way unobservable to inference; standard diagnostics appear normal, yet inferences are epistemically invalid.
AI.motivation: Challenge the assumption that increasing data always improves reliability; identify a structural regime where data abundance systematically leads to incorrect conclusions and is undetectable by standard checks.
AI.method: Use minimal synthetic experiments to show that, in this regime, adding data amplifies existing error rather than correcting it; residuals and goodness-of-fit diagnostics remain misleadingly normal.
AI.result: Inference procedures can converge to incorrect conclusions despite stability and calibration; additional data amplify the error instead of correcting it; conventional diagnostics do not reveal the underlying bias—revealing an intrinsic limit of data-driven inference.
AI.conclusion: Inference must be constrained by explicit assumptions about the integrity of the observational process; data availability alone does not guarantee epistemic validity, and models should incorporate constraints on data-generation mechanisms to ensure reliable conclusions.

title: GAS: Enhancing Reward-Cost Balance of Generative Model-assisted Offline Safe RL
summary: Offline Safe Reinforcement Learning (OSRL) aims to learn a policy to achieve high performance in sequential decision-making while satisfying constraints, using only pre-collected datasets. Recent works, inspired by the strong capabilities of Generative Models (GMs), reformulate decision-making in OSRL as a conditional generative process, where GMs generate desirable actions conditioned on predefined reward and cost values. However, GM-assisted methods face two major challenges in OSRL: (1) lacking the ability to "stitch" optimal transitions from suboptimal trajectories within the dataset, and (2) struggling to balance reward targets with cost targets, particularly when they are conflict. To address these issues, we propose Goal-Assisted Stitching (GAS), a novel algorithm designed to enhance stitching capabilities while effectively balancing reward maximization and constraint satisfaction. To enhance the stitching ability, GAS first augments and relabels the dataset at the transition level, enabling the construction of high-quality trajectories from suboptimal ones. GAS also introduces novel goal functions, which estimate the optimal achievable reward and cost goals from the dataset. These goal functions, trained using expectile regression on the relabeled and augmented dataset, allow GAS to accommodate a broader range of reward-cost return pairs and achieve a better tradeoff between reward maximization and constraint satisfaction compared to human-specified values. The estimated goals then guide policy training, ensuring robust performance under constrained settings. Furthermore, to improve training stability and efficiency, we reshape the dataset to achieve a more uniform reward-cost return distribution. Empirical results validate the effectiveness of GAS, demonstrating superior performance in balancing reward maximization and constraint satisfaction compared to existing methods.

AI.tldr: GAS: a transition-level augmented relabeling offline safe RL method that learns goal functions via expectile regression to stitch suboptimal trajectories and balance reward and cost, yielding improved tradeoffs and stability.
AI.motivation: OSRL with generative-model-based planning suffers from inability to stitch optimal transitions from suboptimal datasets and difficulty balancing conflicting reward and cost targets; there is a need for robust, data-efficient policy under constraints.
AI.method: Augment and relabel dataset at transition level; construct high-quality trajectories; introduce goal functions estimating optimal achievable reward and cost from data; train these goals with expectile regression on relabeled data; reshape dataset to uniform reward-cost distribution; use goals to guide policy optimization.
AI.result: Empirical results show GAS outperforms baselines in balancing reward maximization and constraint satisfaction, with improved training stability and efficiency, and robust performance under constrained settings.
AI.conclusion: GAS effectively improves OSRL via stitching and goal-guided training by combining data augmentation, relabeling, and expectile-based goal estimation, demonstrating potential as a general framework for constrained offline RL.

title: Perception-Based Beliefs for POMDPs with Visual Observations
summary: Partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) are a principled planning model for sequential decision-making under uncertainty. Yet, real-world problems with high-dimensional observations, such as camera images, remain intractable for traditional belief- and filtering-based solvers. To tackle this problem, we introduce the Perception-based Beliefs for POMDPs framework (PBP), which complements such solvers with a perception model. This model takes the form of an image classifier which maps visual observations to probability distributions over states. PBP incorporates these distributions directly into belief updates, so the underlying solver does not need to reason explicitly over high-dimensional observation spaces. We show that the belief update of PBP coincides with the standard belief update if the image classifier is exact. Moreover, to handle classifier imprecision, we incorporate uncertainty quantification and introduce two methods to adjust the belief update accordingly. We implement PBP using two traditional POMDP solvers and empirically show that (1) it outperforms existing end-to-end deep RL methods and (2) uncertainty quantification improves robustness of PBP against visual corruption.

AI.tldr: PBP: a framework that uses a perception model to map images to state distributions for POMDP belief updates, with uncertainty handling; achieves better performance than end-to-end RL and improves robustness to visual noise.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional observations (e.g., images) make traditional belief-based POMDP solvers intractable; leverage perception to condense observations into state distributions and integrate with existing solvers.
AI.method: Introduce a perception-based belief update (PBP) that feeds image-derived state distributions into standard POMDP belief updates. When the classifier is exact, updates align with standard belief updates. To account for classifier imprecision, incorporate uncertainty quantification and two methods to adjust updates. Implement and test with two traditional POMDP solvers.
AI.result: Empirically, PBP outperforms existing end-to-end deep RL methods; uncertainty quantification enhances robustness to visual corruption.
AI.conclusion: PBP effectively integrates perception into POMDP planning, reducing reliance on high-dimensional observations and improving robustness via uncertainty-aware updates.

title: Pseudo-Invertible Neural Networks
summary: The Moore-Penrose Pseudo-inverse (PInv) serves as the fundamental solution for linear systems. In this paper, we propose a natural generalization of PInv to the nonlinear regime in general and to neural networks in particular. We introduce Surjective Pseudo-invertible Neural Networks (SPNN), a class of architectures explicitly designed to admit a tractable non-linear PInv. The proposed non-linear PInv and its implementation in SPNN satisfy fundamental geometric properties. One such property is null-space projection or "Back-Projection", $x' = x + A^\dagger(y-Ax)$, which moves a sample $x$ to its closest consistent state $x'$ satisfying $Ax=y$. We formalize Non-Linear Back-Projection (NLBP), a method that guarantees the same consistency constraint for non-linear mappings $f(x)=y$ via our defined PInv. We leverage SPNNs to expand the scope of zero-shot inverse problems. Diffusion-based null-space projection has revolutionized zero-shot solving for linear inverse problems by exploiting closed-form back-projection. We extend this method to non-linear degradations. Here, "degradation" is broadly generalized to include any non-linear loss of information, spanning from optical distortions to semantic abstractions like classification. This approach enables zero-shot inversion of complex degradations and allows precise semantic control over generative outputs without retraining the diffusion prior.

AI.tldr: Nonlinear generalization of Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse via Surjective PInv Neural Networks (SPNN); introduces Non-Linear Back-Projection (NLBP); extends diffusion-based back-projection to nonlinear degradations for zero-shot inverse problems with semantic control.
AI.motivation: Linear PInv is inadequate for nonlinear mappings; need tractable, principled nonlinear inverse mappings with guarantees like back-projection; expand zero-shot inversion capabilities to nonlinear degradations and semantic control.
AI.method: Propose SPNN architectures that admit a tractable nonlinear PInv; formalize Non-Linear Back-Projection (NLBP) ensuring consistency for nonlinear mappings f(x)=y; extend diffusion-based null-space projection to nonlinear degradations; enable zero-shot inversion and semantic control without retraining diffusion priors.
AI.result: Framework proposing theoretical guarantees (null-space projection/consistency) and practical mechanisms to perform zero-shot inversion of nonlinear degradations; extends diffusion-based projection to nonlinear regime; supports semantic control without retraining diffusion priors.
AI.conclusion: SPNN and NLBP offer a principled approach to nonlinear inverse problems, broadening zero-shot capabilities and enabling semantic-controlled generative inversion without retraining diffusion priors.

title: Mining Generalizable Activation Functions
summary: The choice of activation function is an active area of research, with different proposals aimed at improving optimization, while maintaining expressivity. Additionally, the activation function can significantly alter the implicit inductive bias of the architecture, controlling its non-linear behavior. In this paper, in line with previous work, we argue that evolutionary search provides a useful framework for finding new activation functions, while we also make two novel observations. The first is that modern pipelines, such as AlphaEvolve, which relies on frontier LLMs as a mutator operator, allows for a much wider and flexible search space; e.g., over all possible python functions within a certain FLOP budget, eliminating the need for manually constructed search spaces. In addition, these pipelines will be biased towards meaningful activation functions, given their ability to represent common knowledge, leading to a potentially more efficient search of the space. The second observation is that, through this framework, one can target not only performance improvements but also activation functions that encode particular inductive biases. This can be done by using performance on out-of-distribution data as a fitness function, reflecting the degree to which the architecture respects the inherent structure in the data in a manner independent of distribution shifts. We carry an empirical exploration of this proposal and show that relatively small scale synthetic datasets can be sufficient for AlphaEvolve to discover meaningful activations.

AI.tldr: Evolutionary search with AlphaEvolve broadens activation-function discovery by using frontier LLMs as mutators to explore Python functions within a FLOP-budget, enabling inductive-bias-aware activations discovered from small synthetic data.
AI.motivation: Activation functions shape optimization, expressivity, and inductive bias. Manual search spaces are limited; a flexible, knowledge-informed search can expand the set of viable activations and allow encoding of inductive biases via fitness objectives.
AI.method: Use the AlphaEvolve evolutionary framework with frontier LLMs as the mutator to generate candidate activations, exploring the space of all Python functions within a given FLOP budget rather than a hand-crafted search space. Assess candidates on small synthetic datasets, including out-of-distribution fitness to induce desired biases.
AI.result: Empirical exploration shows AlphaEvolve can discover meaningful, useful activations. The search is biased toward known, effective activations due to the mutator's knowledge, and small synthetic datasets suffice for initial discovery of activations that encode inductive biases through OOD fitness.
AI.conclusion: Evolutionary search with flexible mutators like AlphaEvolve is a promising path for discovering new activation functions and encoding inductive biases. The approach expands the search space beyond manual designs and can use OOD fitness to induce desirable properties, with preliminary success on small synthetic datasets. Further validation on real-world tasks is recommended.

title: Shared LoRA Subspaces for almost Strict Continual Learning
summary: Adapting large pretrained models to new tasks efficiently and continually is crucial for real-world deployment but remains challenging due to catastrophic forgetting and the high cost of retraining. While parameter-efficient tuning methods like low rank adaptation (LoRA) reduce computational demands, they lack mechanisms for strict continual learning and knowledge integration, without relying on data replay, or multiple adapters. We propose Share, a novel approach to parameter efficient continual finetuning that learns and dynamically updates a single, shared low-rank subspace, enabling seamless adaptation across multiple tasks and modalities. Share constructs a foundational subspace that extracts core knowledge from past tasks and incrementally integrates new information by identifying essential subspace directions. Knowledge from each new task is incorporated into this evolving subspace, facilitating forward knowledge transfer, while minimizing catastrophic interference. This approach achieves up to 100x parameter reduction and 281x memory savings over traditional LoRA methods, maintaining performance comparable to jointly trained models. A single Share model can replace hundreds of task-specific LoRA adapters, supporting scalable, asynchronous continual learning. Experiments across image classification, natural language understanding, 3D pose estimation, and text-to-image generation validate its effectiveness, making Share a practical and scalable solution for lifelong learning in large-scale AI systems.

AI.tldr: Share learns a single evolving low-rank subspace to parameter-efficiently finetune large models across tasks and modalities, enabling continual learning with up to 100x parameter reduction and 281x memory savings compared with LoRA while maintaining joint-training-level performance.
AI.motivation: To address catastrophic forgetting and the high retraining cost of adapting large pretrained models to new tasks, without relying on data replay or multiple adapters.
AI.method: Construct a foundational shared subspace that captures core knowledge from past tasks and incrementally integrate new task information by identifying essential subspace directions. Update the subspace with each new task, enabling forward knowledge transfer while minimizing interference. A single Share model replaces hundreds of task-specific LoRA adapters, supporting scalable, asynchronous continual learning across modalities.
AI.result: Empirical validation across image classification, natural language understanding, 3D pose estimation, and text-to-image generation shows strong performance with substantial parameter and memory savings (up to 100x parameter reduction and 281x memory savings over traditional LoRA), rivaling jointly trained models.
AI.conclusion: Share provides a practical and scalable solution for lifelong learning in large-scale AI systems, enabling asynchronous continual learning and significant efficiency gains by maintaining a single shared subspace instead of multiple adapters.

title: Almost Asymptotically Optimal Active Clustering Through Pairwise Observations
summary: We propose a new analysis framework for clustering $M$ items into an unknown number of $K$ distinct groups using noisy and actively collected responses. At each time step, an agent is allowed to query pairs of items and observe bandit binary feedback. If the pair of items belongs to the same (resp.\ different) cluster, the observed feedback is $1$ with probability $p>1/2$ (resp.\ $q<1/2$). Leveraging the ubiquitous change-of-measure technique, we establish a fundamental lower bound on the expected number of queries needed to achieve a desired confidence in the clustering accuracy, formulated as a sup-inf optimization problem. Building on this theoretical foundation, we design an asymptotically optimal algorithm in which the stopping criterion involves an empirical version of the inner infimum -- the Generalized Likelihood Ratio (GLR) statistic -- being compared to a threshold. We develop a computationally feasible variant of the GLR statistic and show that its performance gap to the lower bound can be accurately empirically estimated and remains within a constant multiple of the lower bound.

AI.tldr: Establishes a fundamental sample complexity lower bound for active clustering with unknown K under noisy binary pairwise feedback, and delivers an asymptotically optimal algorithm using a GLR-based stopping rule with a computable variant whose empirical gap to the lower bound is a constant factor.
AI.motivation: To understand the minimal number of adaptive pairwise queries required to cluster M items into an unknown number K of clusters with high confidence when responses are noisy (p>1/2 if same cluster, q<1/2 if different).
AI.method: Derive a sup-inf lower bound via change-of-measure techniques for the expected query count. Propose an asymptotically optimal algorithm where the stopping rule compares an empirical GLR statistic (inner infimum over clusterings) to a threshold. Implement a computationally feasible GLR variant and empirically estimate its gap to the lower bound.
AI.result: Proves a fundamental lower bound on required queries and designs a GLR-based stopping rule achieving asymptotic optimality. The feasible GLR variant closely tracks the lower bound with a constant-factor gap that can be estimated empirically.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides tight asymptotic limits for active clustering with noisy pairwise feedback and a practically implementable algorithm whose performance is provably near-optimal with respect to the derived lower bound.

title: FedRandom: Sampling Consistent and Accurate Contribution Values in Federated Learning
summary: Federated Learning is a privacy-preserving decentralized approach for Machine Learning tasks. In industry deployments characterized by a limited number of entities possessing abundant data, the significance of a participant's role in shaping the global model becomes pivotal given that participation in a federation incurs costs, and participants may expect compensation for their involvement. Additionally, the contributions of participants serve as a crucial means to identify and address potential malicious actors and free-riders. However, fairly assessing individual contributions remains a significant hurdle. Recent works have demonstrated a considerable inherent instability in contribution estimations across aggregation strategies. While employing a different strategy may offer convergence benefits, this instability can have potentially harming effects on the willingness of participants in engaging in the federation. In this work, we introduce FedRandom, a novel mitigation technique to the contribution instability problem. Tackling the instability as a statistical estimation problem, FedRandom allows us to generate more samples than when using regular FL strategies. We show that these additional samples provide a more consistent and reliable evaluation of participant contributions. We demonstrate our approach using different data distributions across CIFAR-10, MNIST, CIFAR-100 and FMNIST and show that FedRandom reduces the overall distance to the ground truth by more than a third in half of all evaluated scenarios, and improves stability in more than 90% of cases.

AI.tldr: FedRandom mitigates contribution instability in federated learning by treating contribution estimation as a statistical estimation problem and generating extra samples to stabilize evaluations, improving alignment with ground truth and overall reliability across varied data distributions.
AI.motivation: In settings with few entities that hold ample data, fair and stable attribution of each participant's contribution is critical for compensation, trust, and defense against free-riders or malicious actors. Instability in contribution estimates across aggregation strategies undermines participant willingness to join a federation.
AI.method: Frame contribution estimation as a statistical estimation problem and generate additional samples beyond standard FL, enabling more robust estimation of each participant's contribution. Validate on diverse non-iid data splits of CIFAR-10, MNIST, CIFAR-100, and FMNIST to compare against ground truth.
AI.result: FedRandom reduces the distance to the ground truth by more than one third in half of the evaluated scenarios and improves stability in more than 90% of cases.
AI.conclusion: FedRandom effectively mitigates contribution instability in federated learning, yielding more reliable and consistent attribution of participant contributions across varied data distributions.

title: Limitations of SGD for Multi-Index Models Beyond Statistical Queries
summary: Understanding the limitations of gradient methods, and stochastic gradient descent (SGD) in particular, is a central challenge in learning theory. To that end, a commonly used tool is the Statistical Queries (SQ) framework, which studies performance limits of algorithms based on noisy interaction with the data. However, it is known that the formal connection between the SQ framework and SGD is tenuous: Existing results typically rely on adversarial or specially-structured gradient noise that does not reflect the noise in standard SGD, and (as we point out here) can sometimes lead to incorrect predictions. Moreover, many analyses of SGD for challenging problems rely on non-trivial algorithmic modifications, such as restricting the SGD trajectory to the sphere or using very small learning rates. To address these shortcomings, we develop a new, non-SQ framework to study the limitations of standard vanilla SGD, for single-index and multi-index models (namely, when the target function depends on a low-dimensional projection of the inputs). Our results apply to a broad class of settings and architectures, including (potentially deep) neural networks.

AI.tldr: A non-SQ framework is developed to analyze vanilla SGD; shows SQ-based analyses can misrepresent SGD limitations; applies to single-index and multi-index models and broad architectures including deep nets.
AI.motivation: SQ-based analyses often rely on adversarial or specially structured gradient noise and may mispredict standard SGD behavior; existing SGD analyses require non-trivial algorithmic tweaks. A framework using standard SGD noise and dynamics is needed to characterize learning limits.
AI.method: Introduce a non-SQ theoretical framework for vanilla SGD; analyze single-index and multi-index target functions; extend to broad model classes, including potentially deep neural networks; derive general limitations/lower-bound type results that reflect standard SGD.
AI.result: The framework yields general results about the limitations of vanilla SGD not captured by SQ analyses, applicable to a broad class of problems and architectures, including deep nets; clarifies when SGD cannot efficiently learn certain low-dimensional projections.
AI.conclusion: Provides a non-SQ lens to study SGD limitations, with broad applicability to single-index/multi-index problems and neural networks, aligning theory more closely with practical SGD performance.

title: Fix Representation (Optimally) Before Fairness: Finite-Sample Shrinkage Population Correction and the True Price of Fairness Under Subpopulation Shift
summary: Machine learning practitioners frequently observe tension between predictive accuracy and group fairness constraints -- yet sometimes fairness interventions appear to improve accuracy. We show that both phenomena can be artifacts of training data that misrepresents subgroup proportions. Under subpopulation shift (stable within-group distributions, shifted group proportions), we establish: (i) full importance-weighted correction is asymptotically unbiased but finite-sample suboptimal; (ii) the optimal finite-sample correction is a shrinkage reweighting that interpolates between target and training mixtures; (iii) apparent "fairness helps accuracy" can arise from comparing fairness methods to an improperly-weighted baseline. We provide an actionable evaluation protocol: fix representation (optimally) before fairness -- compare fairness interventions against a shrinkage-corrected baseline to isolate the true, irreducible price of fairness. Experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks (Adult, COMPAS) validate our theoretical predictions and demonstrate that this protocol eliminates spurious tradeoffs, revealing the genuine fairness-utility frontier.

AI.tldr: Under subpopulation shift, observed fairness-utility tradeoffs can be artifacts of misrepresented group proportions. Full importance weighting is asymptotically unbiased but suboptimal with finite data; the finite-sample optimum is a shrinkage reweighting between target and training mixtures; apparent “fairness helps accuracy” can arise from an improperly weighted baseline. An actionable protocol—fix representation and compare fairness interventions to a shrinkage-corrected baseline—reveals the true fairness-utility frontier. Validated on synthetic data, Adult, and COMPAS.
AI.motivation: Address inconsistent observations in ML fairness where fairness interventions sometimes improve accuracy. Show that data misrepresentation of subgroup proportions under subpopulation shift can create spurious tradeoffs.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis under subpopulation shift (stable within-group distributions, shifted group proportions) to derive (i) asymptotic properties of full importance weighting, (ii) finite-sample optimal correction via shrinkage reweighting that blends target and training mixtures, and (iii) an evaluation protocol to distinguish true fairness costs from baseline misweighting. Empirical validation on synthetic data and real benchmarks (Adult, COMPAS).
AI.result: (i) Full importance-weighted correction is asymptotically unbiased but suboptimal with finite samples; (ii) The optimal finite-sample correction is a shrinkage reweighting interpolating between the target distribution and the training mixture; (iii) Apparent fairness-driven accuracy gains can result from comparing fairness methods to an improperly-weighted baseline. The authors propose a protocol—fix a representation (optimally) before applying fairness and compare fairness interventions against a shrinkage-corrected baseline—to isolate the irreducible price of fairness. Experiments on synthetic data, Adult, and COMPAS validate the theory and show the protocol removes spurious tradeoffs, exposing the true fairness-utility frontier.
AI.conclusion: To properly evaluate fairness-utility tradeoffs under distribution shift, fix representation and evaluate fairness interventions against a shrinkage-corrected baseline. This reveals the genuine frontier and prevents misleading conclusions about fairness improving accuracy.

title: Projected Boosting with Fairness Constraints: Quantifying the Cost of Fair Training Distributions
summary: Boosting algorithms enjoy strong theoretical guarantees: when weak learners maintain positive edge, AdaBoost achieves geometric decrease of exponential loss. We study how to incorporate group fairness constraints into boosting while preserving analyzable training dynamics. Our approach, FairBoost, projects the ensemble-induced exponential-weights distribution onto a convex set of distributions satisfying fairness constraints (as a reweighting surrogate), then trains weak learners on this fair distribution. The key theoretical insight is that projecting the training distribution reduces the effective edge of weak learners by a quantity controlled by the KL-divergence of the projection. We prove an exponential-loss bound where the convergence rate depends on weak learner edge minus a "fairness cost" term $δ_t = \sqrt{\mathrm{KL}(w^t \| q^t)/2}$. This directly quantifies the accuracy-fairness tradeoff in boosting dynamics. Experiments on standard benchmarks validate the theoretical predictions and demonstrate competitive fairness-accuracy tradeoffs with stable training curves.

AI.tldr: FairBoost integrates group fairness into boosting by projecting the ensemble's exponential-weights distribution onto a convex fairness set, reweighting training accordingly. The key insight is that the projection reduces a weak learner's effective edge by a KL-divergence-based fairness cost δ_t, yielding an exponential-loss bound where the rate depends on (edge − δ_t). Empirical results show competitive fairness-accuracy tradeoffs and stable training.
AI.motivation: To achieve group fairness within boosting while preserving the tractable training dynamics and providing a clear accuracy–fairness tradeoff quantification.
AI.method: Project the ensemble-induced exponential-weights distribution onto a convex set of fair distributions (reweighting surrogate). Train weak learners on this fair distribution. The decrease in edge is controlled by the KL-divergence between the original and projected distributions, leading to a δ_t = sqrt(KL(w^t || q^t)/2) fairness cost and an exponential-loss bound that accounts for this cost.
AI.result: Derived an exponential-loss bound where the convergence rate depends on the weak-learner edge minus the fairness cost δ_t. Empirical experiments on standard benchmarks show competitive fairness–accuracy tradeoffs and stable training curves, matching the theoretical predictions.
AI.conclusion: FairBoost provides a principled framework to inject group fairness into boosting with interpretable dynamics. The KL-based fairness cost quantifies the accuracy–fairness tradeoff and permits stable, analyzable training with competitive performance.

title: Muon in Associative Memory Learning: Training Dynamics and Scaling Laws
summary: Muon updates matrix parameters via the matrix sign of the gradient and has shown strong empirical gains, yet its dynamics and scaling behavior remain unclear in theory. We study Muon in a linear associative memory model with softmax retrieval and a hierarchical frequency spectrum over query-answer pairs, with and without label noise. In this setting, we show that Gradient Descent (GD) learns frequency components at highly imbalanced rates, leading to slow convergence bottlenecked by low-frequency components. In contrast, the Muon optimizer mitigates this imbalance, leading to faster and more uniform progress. Specifically, in the noiseless case, Muon achieves an exponential speedup over GD; in the noisy case with a power-decay frequency spectrum, we derive Muon's optimization scaling law and demonstrate its superior scaling efficiency over GD. Furthermore, we show that Muon can be interpreted as an implicit matrix preconditioner arising from adaptive task alignment and block-symmetric gradient structure. In contrast, the preconditioner with coordinate-wise sign operator could match Muon under oracle access to unknown task representations, which is infeasible for SignGD in practice. Experiments on synthetic long-tail classification and LLaMA-style pre-training corroborate the theory.

AI.tldr: Muon accelerates learning in a linear associative memory with softmax retrieval under hierarchical frequency spectra, yielding exponential speedup over GD in noiseless settings and favorable scaling under noisy spectra; interpretable as an implicit matrix preconditioner; experiments on synthetic long-tail data and LLaMA-style pretraining corroborate the theory.
AI.motivation: To understand the dynamics and scaling of Muon, which previously showed strong empirical gains but unclear theoretical behavior, particularly regarding how it handles imbalanced frequency components and under label noise.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis within a linear associative memory model using softmax retrieval and a hierarchical (power-law) frequency spectrum for query–answer pairs, both with and without label noise. Derive learning dynamics, convergence rates across frequency components, and scaling laws for Muon vs. gradient descent (GD). Interpret Muon as an implicit adaptive preconditioner arising from task alignment and a block-symmetric gradient structure; compare to a coordinate-wise sign-based preconditioner under oracle task representations; validate with experiments.
AI.result: GD learns frequency components at imbalanced rates, causing slow convergence dominated by low-frequency components. Muon mitigates this imbalance, delivering faster and more uniform progress. In the noiseless case, Muon achieves exponential speedup over GD; with power-decay spectrum under label noise, Muon exhibits superior scaling. Muon can be viewed as an implicit preconditioner formed by adaptive task alignment and block-symmetric gradients; sign-based preconditioner can match Muon only with oracle access to task representations (unfeasible in practice). Experiments on synthetic long-tail classification and LLaMA-style pre-training corroborate the theory.
AI.conclusion: Muon provides faster and more uniform optimization by acting as an adaptive implicit preconditioner that balances learning across frequency components. The theory yields exponential speedups in noiseless settings and favorable scaling under noisy spectra, with practical validation. Future work could explore broader tasks, robustness, and tighter characterizations of the preconditioner.

title: CSRv2: Unlocking Ultra-Sparse Embeddings
summary: In the era of large foundation models, the quality of embeddings has become a central determinant of downstream task performance and overall system capability. Yet widely used dense embeddings are often extremely high-dimensional, incurring substantial costs in storage, memory, and inference latency. To address these, Contrastive Sparse Representation (CSR) is recently proposed as a promising direction, mapping dense embeddings into high-dimensional but k-sparse vectors, in contrast to compact dense embeddings such as Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL). Despite its promise, CSR suffers severe degradation in the ultra-sparse regime, where over 80% of neurons remain inactive, leaving much of its efficiency potential unrealized. In this paper, we introduce CSRv2, a principled training approach designed to make ultra-sparse embeddings viable. CSRv2 stabilizes sparsity learning through progressive k-annealing, enhances representational quality via supervised contrastive objectives, and ensures end-to-end adaptability with full backbone finetuning. CSRv2 reduces dead neurons from 80% to 20% and delivers a 14% accuracy gain at k=2, bringing ultra-sparse embeddings on par with CSR at k=8 and MRL at 32 dimensions, all with only two active features. While maintaining comparable performance, CSRv2 delivers a 7x speedup over MRL, and yields up to 300x improvements in compute and memory efficiency relative to dense embeddings in text representation. Extensive experiments across text and vision demonstrate that CSRv2 makes ultra-sparse embeddings practical without compromising performance, where CSRv2 achieves 7%/4% improvement over CSR when k=4 and further increases this gap to 14%/6% when k=2 in text/vision representation. By making extreme sparsity viable, CSRv2 broadens the design space for real-time and edge-deployable AI systems where both embedding quality and efficiency are critical.

AI.tldr: CSRv2 makes ultra-sparse embeddings viable by progressive k-annealing, supervised contrastive learning, and full finetuning; achieves strong accuracy with only two active features and large efficiency gains.
AI.motivation: Dense high-dimensional embeddings are costly; CSR degrades in ultra-sparse regime with many dead neurons; need efficient real-time/edge-friendly representations.
AI.method: Progressive k-annealing for stable sparsity; supervised contrastive objectives to improve representation quality; end-to-end backbone finetuning.
AI.result: Reduces dead neurons from 80% to 20%; 14% accuracy gain at k=2; matches CSR at k=8 and MRL at 32d with two active features; 7x speedup over MRL; up to 300x compute/memory efficiency vs dense; improvements over CSR at k=4 (7%/4%) and at k=2 (14%/6%) in text/vision.
AI.conclusion: CSRv2 broadens the design space for real-time and edge-deployable AI by making extreme sparsity practical without sacrificing performance.

title: DECO: Decoupled Multimodal Diffusion Transformer for Bimanual Dexterous Manipulation with a Plugin Tactile Adapter
summary: Overview of the Proposed DECO Framework.} DECO is a DiT-based policy that decouples multimodal conditioning. Image and action tokens interact via joint self attention, while proprioceptive states and optional conditions are injected through adaptive layer normalization. Tactile signals are injected via cross attention, while a lightweight LoRA-based adapter is used to efficiently fine-tune the pretrained policy. DECO is also accompanied by DECO-50, a bimanual dexterous manipulation dataset with tactile sensing, consisting of 4 scenarios and 28 sub-tasks, covering more than 50 hours of data, approximately 5 million frames, and 8,000 successful trajectories.

AI.tldr: DECO is a DiT-based multimodal policy that decouples conditioning across modalities using joint self-attention for image and action tokens, adaptive layer normalization for proprioception, and cross-attention for tactile signals, with a LoRA adapter for efficient fine-tuning. It is accompanied by DECO-50, a bimanual dexterous manipulation dataset with tactile sensing, comprising 4 scenarios, 28 sub-tasks, over 50 hours of data, about 5 million frames, and 8,000 successful trajectories.
AI.motivation: To enable robust, flexible multimodal manipulation by decoupling conditioning across vision, proprioception, and tactile inputs, while maintaining parameter efficiency through LoRA-based fine-tuning; and to provide a substantial tactile-enabled benchmark for bimanual manipulation.
AI.method: A DiT-based policy where image and action tokens interact via joint self-attention; proprioceptive states and optional conditions injected through adaptive layer normalization; tactile signals injected via cross-attention; parameter-efficient fine-tuning via a lightweight LoRA adapter; accompanied by the DECO-50 dataset.
AI.result: Introduction of the DECO framework and the DECO-50 dataset; the dataset contains 4 scenarios and 28 sub-tasks, with >50 hours of data, ~5 million frames, and 8,000 successful trajectories, demonstrating a scalable, tactile-enabled multimodal manipulation setup.
AI.conclusion: DECO offers a modular, data-efficient approach to multimodal manipulation by decoupling conditioning across modalities and enabling efficient adaptation to tactile-rich tasks; DECO-50 provides a substantial benchmark for bimanual dexterous manipulation with tactile sensing.

title: Learning to Inject: Automated Prompt Injection via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Prompt injection is one of the most critical vulnerabilities in LLM agents; yet, effective automated attacks remain largely unexplored from an optimization perspective. Existing methods heavily depend on human red-teamers and hand-crafted prompts, limiting their scalability and adaptability. We propose AutoInject, a reinforcement learning framework that generates universal, transferable adversarial suffixes while jointly optimizing for attack success and utility preservation on benign tasks. Our black-box method supports both query-based optimization and transfer attacks to unseen models and tasks. Using only a 1.5B parameter adversarial suffix generator, we successfully compromise frontier systems including GPT 5 Nano, Claude Sonnet 3.5, and Gemini 2.5 Flash on the AgentDojo benchmark, establishing a stronger baseline for automated prompt injection research.

AI.tldr: AutoInject is a reinforcement learning framework that automates generation of universal, transferable adversarial suffixes to prompt injection, achieving successful attacks on major LLM agents with a compact 1.5B parameter suffix generator.
AI.motivation: Prompt injection is a critical vulnerability in LLM agents, and current automated attack methods are scarce, relying on human red-teamers and hand-crafted prompts, which limits scalability and cross-model transferability.
AI.method: AutoInject uses reinforcement learning to produce universal adversarial suffixes, optimizing for attack success while preserving benign task utility in a black-box setting. It supports query-based optimization and transfer attacks across unseen models/tasks with a 1.5B-parameter generator.
AI.result: The approach successfully compromises frontier systems (GPT 5 Nano, Claude Sonnet 3.5, Gemini 2.5 Flash) on the AgentDojo benchmark, establishing a stronger automated prompt-injection baseline.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility and effectiveness of automated prompt injection via a lightweight RL-based suffix generator, providing a scalable baseline and guiding future defense-oriented research.

title: How to Achieve the Intended Aim of Deep Clustering Now, without Deep Learning
summary: Deep clustering (DC) is often quoted to have a key advantage over $k$-means clustering. Yet, this advantage is often demonstrated using image datasets only, and it is unclear whether it addresses the fundamental limitations of $k$-means clustering. Deep Embedded Clustering (DEC) learns a latent representation via an autoencoder and performs clustering based on a $k$-means-like procedure, while the optimization is conducted in an end-to-end manner. This paper investigates whether the deep-learned representation has enabled DEC to overcome the known fundamental limitations of $k$-means clustering, i.e., its inability to discover clusters of arbitrary shapes, varied sizes and densities. Our investigations on DEC have a wider implication on deep clustering methods in general. Notably, none of these methods exploit the underlying data distribution. We uncover that a non-deep learning approach achieves the intended aim of deep clustering by making use of distributional information of clusters in a dataset to effectively address these fundamental limitations.

AI.tldr: Dec is unable to overcome the fundamental limitations of k-means; a non-deep, distribution-aware clustering approach can match deep clustering in exploiting cluster distributions.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether deep clustering (e.g., DEC) truly overcomes k-means limitations and to understand the role of data distribution in clustering.
AI.method: A critical analysis comparing DEC with a non-deep clustering approach that leverages distributional information about clusters; empirical/analytical evaluation on the ability to discover clusters of arbitrary shape, size, and density.
AI.result: DEC does not inherently overcome k-means limitations; deep clustering methods, including DEC, do not exploit the underlying data distribution. A non-deep method that leverages distributional information of clusters can achieve the intended goals of deep clustering.
AI.conclusion: The apparent advantages of deep clustering may stem from distributional information rather than the deep representation itself; exploiting cluster distributions in a non-deep framework can address the fundamental limitations of k-means, challenging the necessity of deep models for clustering superiority.

title: Variational Speculative Decoding: Rethinking Draft Training from Token Likelihood to Sequence Acceptance
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates inference for (M)LLMs, yet a training-decoding discrepancy persists: while existing methods optimize single greedy trajectories, decoding involves verifying and ranking multiple sampled draft paths. We propose Variational Speculative Decoding (VSD), formulating draft training as variational inference over latent proposals (draft paths). VSD maximizes the marginal probability of target-model acceptance, yielding an ELBO that promotes high-quality latent proposals while minimizing divergence from the target distribution. To enhance quality and reduce variance, we incorporate a path-level utility and optimize via an Expectation-Maximization procedure. The E-step draws MCMC samples from an oracle-filtered posterior, while the M-step maximizes weighted likelihood using Adaptive Rejection Weighting (ARW) and Confidence-Aware Regularization (CAR). Theoretical analysis confirms that VSD increases expected acceptance length and speedup. Extensive experiments across LLMs and MLLMs show that VSD achieves up to a 9.6% speedup over EAGLE-3 and 7.9% over ViSpec, significantly improving decoding efficiency.

AI.tldr: Variational Speculative Decoding (VSD) reframes draft-path training as variational inference to close the training-decoding gap in speculative decoding, achieving efficiency gains via EM with MCMC sampling and ARW/CAR weighting, yielding up to ~9.6% speedups.
AI.motivation: Speculative decoding speeds up inference but its training optimizes a single greedy trajectory, while decoding uses multiple sampled drafts; this mismatch can limit efficiency and quality. VSD targets a principled alignment between training and decoding.
AI.method: Formulate draft training as variational inference over latent draft paths; maximize the marginal probability of target-model acceptance, yielding an ELBO that promotes high-quality latent proposals and alignment with the target distribution. Introduce path-level utility and optimize via an Expectation-Maximization procedure: E-step draws MCMC samples from an oracle-filtered posterior; M-step maximizes a weighted likelihood using Adaptive Rejection Weighting (ARW) and Confidence-Aware Regularization (CAR).
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows VSD increases the expected acceptance length and decoding speed. Empirical results across LLMs and MLLMs report up to 9.6% speedup over EAGLE-3 and 7.9% over ViSpec, indicating improved decoding efficiency.
AI.conclusion: VSD mitigates training-decoding discrepancy in speculative decoding, improving proposal quality and stability while delivering practical speedups across a range of models.

title: Cross-Domain Offline Policy Adaptation via Selective Transition Correction
summary: It remains a critical challenge to adapt policies across domains with mismatched dynamics in reinforcement learning (RL). In this paper, we study cross-domain offline RL, where an offline dataset from another similar source domain can be accessed to enhance policy learning upon a target domain dataset. Directly merging the two datasets may lead to suboptimal performance due to potential dynamics mismatches. Existing approaches typically mitigate this issue through source domain transition filtering or reward modification, which, however, may lead to insufficient exploitation of the valuable source domain data. Instead, we propose to modify the source domain data into the target domain data. To that end, we leverage an inverse policy model and a reward model to correct the actions and rewards of source transitions, explicitly achieving alignment with the target dynamics. Since limited data may result in inaccurate model training, we further employ a forward dynamics model to retain corrected samples that better match the target dynamics than the original transitions. Consequently, we propose the Selective Transition Correction (STC) algorithm, which enables reliable usage of source domain data for policy adaptation. Experiments on various environments with dynamics shifts demonstrate that STC achieves superior performance against existing baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes Selective Transition Correction (STC) for cross-domain offline RL by correcting source-domain transitions to target dynamics using an inverse policy, a reward model, and forward dynamics filtering to selectively retain samples, yielding superior performance over baselines.
AI.motivation: Cross-domain offline RL suffers from dynamics mismatch when merging data from a source domain into a target domain. Existing mitigation via transition filtering or reward shaping can underutilize valuable source data; a data-centric alignment approach is proposed.
AI.method: Train an inverse policy to map source actions to target-equivalent actions and a reward model to align rewards with target dynamics. Apply these to source transitions to create corrected transitions. Use a forward dynamics model to evaluate corrected samples and retain those that better match the target dynamics, forming the Selective Transition Correction (STC) dataset. Use this corrected data for policy learning in the target domain.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across multiple environments with dynamics shifts show STC achieving superior performance compared with existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: STC enables reliable utilization of source-domain data for policy adaptation in cross-domain offline RL by data-level alignment and selective retention of corrected transitions, improving performance under dynamics mismatch.

title: How Controlling the Variance can Improve Training Stability of Sparsely Activated DNNs and CNNs
summary: The intermediate layers of deep networks can be characterised as a Gaussian process, in particular the Edge-of-Chaos (EoC) initialisation strategy prescribes the limiting covariance matrix of the Gaussian process. Here we show that the under-utilised chosen variance of the Gaussian process is important in the training of deep networks with sparsity inducing activation, such as a shifted and clipped ReLU, $\text{CReLU}_{τ,m}(x)=\min(\max(x-τ,0),m)$. Specifically, initialisations leading to larger fixed Gaussian process variances, allow for improved expressivity with activation sparsity as large as 90% in DNNs and CNNs, and generally improve the stability of the training process. Enabling full, or near full, accuracy at such high levels of sparsity in the hidden layers suggests a promising mechanism to reduce the energy consumption of machine learning models involving fully connected layers.

AI.tldr: Initializing deep nets at the Edge-of-Chaos with larger fixed Gaussian process variance improves expressivity and training stability under high sparsity (up to ~90%) using a sparse activation (CReLUτ,m), enabling near/full accuracy and suggesting energy efficiency benefits for fully connected layers.
AI.motivation: To understand how GP-based EoC initializations interact with sparsity-inducing activations and whether larger initialization variance can sustain performance and training stability under extreme sparsity, potentially reducing energy usage.
AI.method: The study analyzes the limiting covariance structure implied by EoC initialisation for Gaussian process proxies of intermediate layers. It investigates the effect of the chosen GP variance on networks employing a shifted and clipped ReLU activation CReLUτ,m(x)=min(max(x−τ,0),m). Empirical evaluations compare different fixed GP variances on DNNs/CNNs with high sparsity and assess expressivity, training stability, and accuracy.
AI.result: Larger fixed GP variances at initialization enable improved expressivity under high sparsity (up to ~90%) and generally enhance training stability. This permits full or near-full accuracy despite high hidden-layer sparsity and points to a mechanism for reducing energy consumption in fully connected layers.
AI.conclusion: Careful selection of the EoC initialization variance is crucial when training with sparse activations. The results suggest a practical route to maintain performance while increasing sparsity, with potential energy-efficiency benefits in dense layers; further validation across datasets and architectures is warranted.

title: Distributional Reinforcement Learning with Diffusion Bridge Critics
summary: Recent advances in diffusion-based reinforcement learning (RL) methods have demonstrated promising results in a wide range of continuous control tasks. However, existing works in this field focus on the application of diffusion policies while leaving the diffusion critics unexplored. In fact, since policy optimization fundamentally relies on the critic, accurate value estimation is far more important than policy expressiveness. Furthermore, given the stochasticity of most reinforcement learning tasks, it has been confirmed that the critic is more appropriately depicted with a distributional model. Motivated by these points, we propose a novel distributional RL method with Diffusion Bridge Critics (DBC). DBC directly models the inverse cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the Q value. This allows us to accurately capture the value distribution and prevents it from collapsing into a trivial Gaussian distribution owing to the strong distribution-matching capability of the diffusion bridge. Moreover, we further derive an analytic integral formula to address discretization errors in DBC, which is essential in value estimation. To our knowledge, DBC is the first work to employ the diffusion bridge model as the critic. Notably, DBC is also a plug-and-play component and can be integrated into most existing RL frameworks. Experimental results on MuJoCo robot control benchmarks demonstrate the superiority of DBC compared with previous distributional critic models.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based distributional critic method (Diffusion Bridge Critics, DBC) that models the inverse CDF of Q-values using a diffusion bridge; claims plug-and-play integration with RL, analytic integral to reduce discretization errors, and state-of-the-art results on MuJoCo benchmarks.
AI.motivation: In RL, accurate value estimation is essential and distributional modeling better captures stochasticity. Critics are central to learning, yet diffusion RL often focuses on policies. Modeling the Q-value distribution with a diffusion bridge can prevent collapse to Gaussian and improve stability.
AI.method: Introduce DBC, which directly models the inverse CDF of Q using a diffusion bridge. Derive an analytic integral to mitigate discretization errors in value estimation. Treats the diffusion-bridge-based critic as plug-and-play and integrates with existing RL frameworks.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on MuJoCo robotics tasks shows DBC outperforms prior distributional critics. DBC is the first to use a diffusion bridge as a critic and remains compatible with various RL setups.
AI.conclusion: DBC provides a more accurate and robust value distribution estimate by using a diffusion-bridge model for the critic, with an analytic integral to reduce discretization error; its plug-and-play nature facilitates adoption in existing RL pipelines.

title: Classification Under Local Differential Privacy with Model Reversal and Model Averaging
summary: Local differential privacy (LDP) has become a central topic in data privacy research, offering strong privacy guarantees by perturbing user data at the source and removing the need for a trusted curator. However, the noise introduced by LDP often significantly reduces data utility. To address this issue, we reinterpret private learning under LDP as a transfer learning problem, where the noisy data serve as the source domain and the unobserved clean data as the target. We propose novel techniques specifically designed for LDP to improve classification performance without compromising privacy: (1) a noised binary feedback-based evaluation mechanism for estimating dataset utility; (2) model reversal, which salvages underperforming classifiers by inverting their decision boundaries; and (3) model averaging, which assigns weights to multiple reversed classifiers based on their estimated utility. We provide theoretical excess risk bounds under LDP and demonstrate how our methods reduce this risk. Empirical results on both simulated and real-world datasets show substantial improvements in classification accuracy.

AI.tldr: Reframe LDP private learning as transfer learning from noisy (source) to clean (target) data; introduce noised binary feedback evaluation, model reversal, and model averaging to boost classification under LDP; provide excess-risk bounds and empirical gains.
AI.motivation: LDP privacy degrades data utility; need techniques that leverage observed noisy data to improve downstream accuracy while preserving privacy.
AI.method: Three techniques: (1) noised binary feedback-based evaluation mechanism to estimate dataset utility under LDP; (2) model reversal to salvage weak classifiers by inverting decision boundaries; (3) model averaging to weight multiple reversed classifiers by their estimated utility; theoretical excess-risk bounds under LDP; empirical validation on simulated and real datasets.
AI.result: Notable improvements in classification accuracy and reduced excess risk; both synthetic and real-world datasets show gains over baseline LDP methods.
AI.conclusion: Framing private learning under LDP as transfer learning with a utility-aware ensemble yields practical privacy-preserving performance gains; future work to tighten bounds and explore other reversals/ensembles.

title: Bifrost: Steering Strategic Trajectories to Bridge Contextual Gaps for Self-Improving Agents
summary: Autonomous agents excel in self-improvement through reflection and iterative refinement, which reuse successful task trajectories as in-context examples to assist subsequent reasoning. However, shifting across tasks often introduces a context mismatch. Hence, existing approaches either discard the trajectories or manipulate them using heuristics, leading to a non-negligible fine-tuning cost or unguaranteed performance. To bridge this gap, we reveal a context-trajectory correlation, where shifts of context are highly parallel with shifts of trajectory. Based on this finding, we propose BrIdge contextual gap FoR imprOvised trajectory STeering (Bifrost), a training-free method that leverages context differences to precisely guide the adaptation of previously solved trajectories towards the target task, mitigating the misalignment caused by context shifts. Our trajectory adaptation is conducted at the representation level using agent hidden states, ensuring trajectory transformation accurately aligns with the target context in a shared space. Across diverse benchmarks, Bifrost consistently outperforms existing trajectory reuse and finetuned self-improvement methods, demonstrating that agents can effectively leverage past experiences despite substantial context shifts.

AI.tldr: Bifrost is a training-free method that adapts previously solved trajectories to new task contexts by exploiting context–trajectory correlations at the representation level, improving cross-context trajectory reuse without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Context shifts across tasks create a mismatch between reused trajectories and the target task, causing performance degradation and incurring fine-tuning costs; existing methods either discard trajectories or heuristically adjust them.
AI.method: Identify a strong correlation between context changes and trajectory changes; introduce Bifrost, which performs trajectory adaptation at the representation level using agent hidden states to align transformations with the target context in a shared space, without training.
AI.result: Across diverse benchmarks, Bifrost consistently outperforms existing trajectory reuse methods and finetuned self-improvement approaches.
AI.conclusion: Training-free trajectory adaptation via representation-level alignment enables agents to leverage past experiences despite substantial context shifts.

title: Principled Confidence Estimation for Deep Computed Tomography
summary: We present a principled framework for confidence estimation in computed tomography (CT) reconstruction. Based on the sequential likelihood mixing framework (Kirschner et al., 2025), we establish confidence regions with theoretical coverage guarantees for deep-learning-based CT reconstructions. We consider a realistic forward model following the Beer-Lambert law, i.e., a log-linear forward model with Poisson noise, closely reflecting clinical and scientific imaging conditions. The framework is general and applies to both classical algorithms and deep learning reconstruction methods, including U-Nets, U-Net ensembles, and generative Diffusion models. Empirically, we demonstrate that deep reconstruction methods yield substantially tighter confidence regions than classical reconstructions, without sacrificing theoretical coverage guarantees. Our approach allows the detection of hallucinations in reconstructed images and provides interpretable visualizations of confidence regions. This establishes deep models not only as powerful estimators, but also as reliable tools for uncertainty-aware medical imaging.

AI.tldr: Principled uncertainty quantification framework for CT reconstruction using sequential likelihood mixing; yields confidence regions with coverage guarantees under a Poisson-noise Beer-Lambert forward model; model-agnostic, applicable to classical algorithms and deep learning (U-Nets, ensembles, diffusion models); deep methods give tighter confidence regions while maintaining coverage, enable hallucination detection and interpretable confidence visualizations; supports uncertainty-aware clinical imaging.
AI.motivation: Provide reliable confidence estimates for CT reconstructions, especially for deep learning methods, to quantify risk, detect artifacts/hallucinations, and improve clinical trust.
AI.method: Utilizes sequential likelihood mixing framework with a Beer-Lambert log-linear forward model and Poisson noise. Framework is model-agnostic and applies to classical algorithms, U-Net-based methods, ensembles, and diffusion models. Constructs confidence regions with theoretical coverage guarantees; empirical results show tighter regions for deep reconstructions without compromising coverage; includes visualization tools for interpretable uncertainty.
AI.result: Deep reconstruction methods yield substantially tighter confidence regions than classical reconstructions while preserving coverage guarantees; improved hallucination detection and interpretable confidence-region visualizations; broad applicability to uncertainty-aware CT imaging.
AI.conclusion: Establishes deep models as trustworthy estimators and uncertainty-aware tools in CT, enabling reliable deployment and interpretability through confidence regions.

title: Where Does Warm-Up Come From? Adaptive Scheduling for Norm-Constrained Optimizers
summary:
We study adaptive learning rate scheduling for norm-constrained optimizers (e.g., Muon and Lion). We introduce a generalized smoothness assumption under which local curvature decreases with the suboptimality gap and empirically verify that this behavior holds along optimization trajectories. Under this assumption, we establish convergence guarantees under an appropriate choice of learning rate, for which warm-up followed by decay arises naturally from the proof rather than being imposed heuristically.
  Building on this theory, we develop a practical learning rate scheduler that relies only on standard hyperparameters and adapts the warm-up duration automatically at the beginning of training. We evaluate this method on large language model pretraining with LLaMA architectures and show that our adaptive warm-up selection consistently outperforms or at least matches the best manually tuned warm-up schedules across all considered setups, without additional hyperparameter search. Our source code is available at https://github.com/brain-lab-research/llm-baselines/tree/warmup

AI.tldr: Theory-driven adaptive learning-rate scheduling for norm-constrained optimizers (e.g., Muon/Lion) that yields a warm-up-then-decay schedule from a generalized smoothness assumption; introduces a practical auto warm-up scheduler and validates it on LLaMA pretraining, with code available.
AI.motivation: Improve efficiency and robustness of norm-constrained optimizers by providing convergence guarantees and removing manual warm-up tuning.
AI.method: Propose a generalized smoothness condition where local curvature decreases with the suboptimality gap; prove convergence with an appropriate learning rate that naturally induces warm-up and decay; develop a practical scheduler that uses standard hyperparameters and auto-adjusts warm-up at training start; evaluate on large language model pretraining.
AI.result: Empirically confirm the curvature–suboptimality behavior along optimization trajectories; the adaptive warm-up scheduler consistently matches or surpasses the best manually tuned warm-up schedules across setups without extra hyperparameter search.
AI.conclusion: Warm-up behavior emerges from the theory, enabling a hyperparameter-light, effective learning-rate scheduler for large-scale pretraining; code is publicly available.

title: Synthesizing Realistic Test Data without Breaking Privacy
summary: There is a need for synthetic training and test datasets that replicate statistical distributions of original datasets without compromising their confidentiality. A lot of research has been done in leveraging Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) for synthetic data generation. However, the resulting models are either not accurate enough or are still vulnerable to membership inference attacks (MIA) or dataset reconstruction attacks since the original data has been leveraged in the training process. In this paper, we explore the feasibility of producing a synthetic test dataset with the same statistical properties as the original one, with only indirectly leveraging the original data in the generation process. The approach is inspired by GANs, with a generation step and a discrimination step. However, in our approach, we use a test generator (a fuzzer) to produce test data from an input specification, preserving constraints set by the original data; a discriminator model determines how close we are to the original data. By evolving samples and determining "good samples" with the discriminator, we can generate privacy-preserving data that follows the same statistical distributions are the original dataset, leading to a similar utility as the original data. We evaluated our approach on four datasets that have been used to evaluate the state-of-the-art techniques. Our experiments highlight the potential of our approach towards generating synthetic datasets that have high utility while preserving privacy.

AI.tldr: A GAN-inspired framework that synthesizes privacy-preserving test data with similar statistics to a real dataset by using a spec-driven test generator and a discriminator; avoids direct use of real data in training and shows promising utility on four datasets.
AI.motivation: Need for confidential synthetic data that matches the original distributions; conventional GANs risk leakage via MIA or reconstruction; this approach aims to indirectly leverage data to maintain utility while preserving privacy.
AI.method: A GAN-like process with a generation step (test generator/fuzzer) that emits test samples from an input specification, and a discriminator that judges closeness to the original data; samples are evolved/selected to optimize the discriminator score, producing synthetic data that follow the original distribution.
AI.result: Evaluated on four datasets; results indicate high utility while preserving privacy; demonstrates feasibility of synthetic test data generation with privacy constraints.
AI.conclusion: This approach shows promise for privacy-preserving synthetic test data; future work needed on formal privacy guarantees, broader evaluation, and comparison with other privacy-preserving data generation methods.

title: Visualizing the loss landscapes of physics-informed neural networks
summary: Training a neural network requires navigating a high-dimensional, non-convex loss surface to find parameters that minimize this loss. In many ways, it is surprising that optimizers such as stochastic gradient descent and ADAM can reliably locate minima which perform well on both the training and test data. To understand the success of training, a "loss landscape" community has emerged to study the geometry of the loss function and the dynamics of optimization, often using visualization techniques. However, these loss landscape studies have mostly been limited to machine learning for image classification. In the newer field of physics-informed machine learning, little work has been conducted to visualize the landscapes of losses defined not by regression to large data sets, but by differential operators acting on state fields discretized by neural networks. In this work, we provide a comprehensive review of the loss landscape literature, as well as a discussion of the few existing physics-informed works which investigate the loss landscape. We then use a number of the techniques we survey to empirically investigate the landscapes defined by the Deep Ritz and squared residual forms of the physics loss function. We find that the loss landscapes of physics-informed neural networks have many of the same properties as the data-driven classification problems studied in the literature. Unexpectedly, we find that the two formulations of the physics loss often give rise to similar landscapes, which appear smooth, well-conditioned, and convex in the vicinity of the solution. The purpose of this work is to introduce the loss landscape perspective to the scientific machine learning community, compare the Deep Ritz and the strong form losses, and to challenge prevailing intuitions about the complexity of the loss landscapes of physics-informed networks.

AI.tldr: PINN loss landscapes resemble data-driven loss landscapes: smooth, well-conditioned, near-convex near solution; Deep Ritz and squared residual losses yield similar landscapes; challenges assumptions about PINN optimization complexity.
AI.motivation: Loss landscape studies have largely focused on data-driven ML; physics-informed losses lack visualization and geometric understanding, hindering insights into optimization and generalization in PINNs.
AI.method: Review loss landscape literature and apply several visualization/analysis techniques to PINNs; empirically investigate landscapes defined by Deep Ritz and squared residual physics losses; compare properties such as smoothness, conditioning, and local convexity near solutions.
AI.result: PINN loss landscapes share properties with classification loss landscapes; Deep Ritz and squared residual losses produce similar landscapes that appear smooth, well-conditioned, and locally convex near the solution.
AI.conclusion: Introduces a loss landscape perspective to scientific ML; compares two PINN loss formulations; challenges prevailing intuitions about the complexity of PINN loss landscapes; provides guidance for optimization and interpretation of PINNs.
